instruction: What is the purpose of the VolumeAttributesClass in Kubernetes?
response: The VolumeAttributesClass represents a specification of mutable volume attributes defined by the CSI driver, which can be specified during dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumeClaims and changed in the PersistentVolumeClaim spec after provisioning.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: Can the 'parameters' field in the VolumeAttributesClass be updated directly?
response: No, the 'parameters' field itself is immutable. To invoke a volume update, a new VolumeAttributesClass should be created with new parameters, and the PersistentVolumeClaim should be updated to reference the new VolumeAttributesClass.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: What happens if the CSI driver rejects invalid parameters in the VolumeAttributesClass?
response: If the CSI driver rejects invalid parameters, the target PersistentVolumeClaim will be set to an 'Infeasible' state in the modifyVolumeStatus field.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: What are the restrictions on the keys in the 'parameters' field of the VolumeAttributesClass?
response: The keys in the 'parameters' field cannot be empty, and there is a maximum of 512 key/value pairs with a cumulative max size of 256K.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: Is the 'driverName' field in the VolumeAttributesClass mutable?
response: No, the 'driverName' field is immutable.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: What is the minimum required Kubernetes server version to work with CustomResourceDefinitions?
response: The Kubernetes server must be at or later than version v1.16. You can check the version by entering kubectl version.
title: Versions in CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: How do you add a new version to a CustomResourceDefinition?
response: To add a new version, pick a conversion strategy, create and deploy the conversion webhook if necessary, and update the CustomResourceDefinition to include the new version in the spec.versions list with served:true. Also, set the spec.conversion field to the selected conversion strategy.
title: Versions in CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What is the purpose of a conversion webhook in CustomResourceDefinitions?
response: A conversion webhook is used when the conversion between versions involves schema changes and requires custom logic. It enables the conversion of custom resource objects between the version they are stored at and the version they are served at.
title: Versions in CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What happens when a CustomResourceDefinition is created with an initial version?
response: When a CustomResourceDefinition is created, the first version is set in the CustomResourceDefinition spec.versions list to an appropriate stability level and a version number. All custom resource objects will initially be stored at this version, and clients may begin using this version.
title: Versions in CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What is the difference between the version a custom resource object is stored at and the version it is served at?
response: The version a custom resource object is stored at is the version specified in the CustomResourceDefinition, while the version it is served at may be different if the client requests a different version. The custom resource object must be converted between these versions if they are different, using either the default None conversion strategy or a conversion webhook.
title: Versions in CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What is the default maximum number of volumes that can be attached to a Node for Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS)?
response: The default maximum number of volumes that can be attached to a Node for Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) is 39.
title: Node-specific Volume Limits

instruction: How does Kubernetes determine the maximum number of volumes for a Node when using in-tree volume plugins?
response: For volumes managed by in-tree volume plugins, Kubernetes automatically determines the Node type and enforces the appropriate maximum number of volumes for the node.
title: Node-specific Volume Limits

instruction: What is the maximum number of volumes that can be attached to a Node on Google Compute Engine?
response: On Google Compute Engine, up to 127 volumes can be attached to a node, depending on the node type.
title: Node-specific Volume Limits

instruction: How can CSI drivers dynamically adjust the maximum number of volumes that can be attached to a Node at runtime?
response: CSI drivers can dynamically adjust the maximum number of volumes that can be attached to a Node at runtime by using the Mutable CSI Node Allocatable Count feature, which is enabled by default in Kubernetes v1.36, and requires the MutableCSINodeAllocatableCount feature gate to be enabled on the kube-apiserver and kubelet components.
title: Node-specific Volume Limits

instruction: What field in the CSIDriver specification can be used to request periodic updates to volume limits?
response: The nodeAllocatableUpdatePeriodSeconds field in the CSIDriver specification can be used to request periodic updates to volume limits.
title: Node-specific Volume Limits

instruction: What is the minimum kernel version required for the net.ipv4.ip_local_reserved_ports sysctl?
response: The minimum kernel version required for the net.ipv4.ip_local_reserved_ports sysctl is 3.16+.
title: Linux Kernel Version Requirements

instruction: Can I use an older kernel version with the nftables mode of kube-proxy for production?
response: No, using an older kernel version with the nftables mode of kube-proxy is not recommended in production, as it may cause problems with other nftables users on the system. The recommended kernel version is 5.13 or later.
title: Linux Kernel Version Requirements

instruction: What is the recommended cgroup version to use with Kubernetes?
response: Using cgroup v2 is recommended, as Kubernetes cgroup v1 support is in maintained mode starting from Kubernetes v1.31.
title: Linux Kernel Version Requirements

instruction: What is the minimum kernel version required for the net.ipv4.tcp_rmem sysctl?

title: Linux Kernel Version Requirements

instruction: Is it possible to use a kernel older than 5.2 with Kubernetes?
response: Kernel older than 5.2 is not recommended due to the lack of freezer, according to the runc document.
title: Linux Kernel Version Requirements

instruction: What is the primary purpose of using Secrets in Kubernetes?
response: The primary purpose of using Secrets in Kubernetes is to store sensitive data such as passwords, tokens, or keys, so that you don't need to include confidential data in your application code.
title: Secrets

instruction: How are Kubernetes Secrets stored by default?
response: Kubernetes Secrets are, by default, stored unencrypted in the API server's underlying data store (etcd).
title: Secrets

instruction: What steps can be taken to safely use Secrets in Kubernetes?
response: To safely use Secrets, take at least the following steps: Enable Encryption at Rest for Secrets, enable or configure RBAC rules with least-privilege access to Secrets, restrict Secret access to specific containers, and consider using external Secret store providers.
title: Secrets

instruction: What are some common uses for Secrets in Kubernetes?
response: You can use Secrets for purposes such as setting environment variables for a container, providing credentials such as SSH keys or passwords to Pods, and allowing the kubelet to pull container images from private registries.
title: Secrets

instruction: How can you make data 'hidden' when defining a Secret in Kubernetes?
response: You can make your data 'hidden' by defining a key that begins with a dot, which represents a dotfile or 'hidden' file. For example, when a Secret is mounted into a volume, the volume will contain a single file, and the container will have this file present at a specified path.
title: Secrets

instruction: What is the purpose of the ServiceCIDR in Kubernetes?
response: The ServiceCIDR defines a range of IP addresses using CIDR format, which is used to allocate ClusterIPs to Service objects.

instruction: What is the maximum number of CIDRs allowed for ServiceCIDRSpec?
response: A maximum of two CIDRs is allowed, one of each IP family.

instruction: What is the significance of the 'cidrs' field in ServiceCIDRSpec?
response: The 'cidrs' field defines the IP blocks in CIDR notation from which to assign service cluster IPs, and it is an immutable field.

instruction: What information does the 'conditions' field in ServiceCIDRStatus provide?
response: The 'conditions' field holds an array of metav1.Condition that describe the state of the ServiceCIDR, providing the current service state.

instruction: What is the role of the 'apiVersion' field in the ServiceCIDR object?
response: The 'apiVersion' field defines the versioned schema of the ServiceCIDR object representation, and servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value.

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl top node command?
response: The kubectl top node command is used to display resource (CPU/memory) usage of nodes, allowing you to see the resource consumption of nodes.
title: kubectl top node

instruction: How can you show metrics for all nodes using the kubectl top node command?
response: You can show metrics for all nodes by running the command 'kubectl top node' without specifying a node name.
title: kubectl top node

instruction: What is the function of the --sort-by option in the kubectl top node command?
response: The --sort-by option is used to sort the list of nodes based on a specified field, which can be either 'cpu' or 'memory'.
title: kubectl top node

instruction: How can you filter nodes based on labels when using the kubectl top node command?
response: You can filter nodes based on labels by using the -l or --selector option followed by a label query, such as '-l key1=value1,key2=value2,key3 in (value3)'.
title: kubectl top node

instruction: What is the difference between the --show-capacity and default options in the kubectl top node command?
response: The --show-capacity option prints node resources based on Capacity, whereas the default option prints node resources based on Allocatable.
title: kubectl top node

instruction: What does the 'apiGroups' field in a ResourceRule represent?
response: The 'apiGroups' field is a string array that represents the name of the APIGroup that contains the resources. If multiple API groups are specified, any action requested against one of the enumerated resources in any API group will be allowed. '*' means all.
title: ResourceRule

instruction: How do you specify that a ResourceRule applies to all resources in the specified apiGroups?
response: You can specify that a ResourceRule applies to all resources in the specified apiGroups by using '*' in the 'resources' field.
title: ResourceRule

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceNames' field in a ResourceRule?
response: The 'resourceNames' field is an optional white list of names that the rule applies to. An empty set means that everything is allowed. '*' means all.
title: ResourceRule

instruction: How do you represent the subresource 'foo' for all resources in the specified apiGroups in a ResourceRule?
response: You can represent the subresource 'foo' for all resources in the specified apiGroups by using '*/foo' in the 'resources' field.
title: ResourceRule

instruction: What does the '*' symbol mean in the 'verbs' field of a ResourceRule?
response: The '*' symbol in the 'verbs' field of a ResourceRule means all Kubernetes resource API verbs, such as get, list, watch, create, update, delete, and proxy.
title: ResourceRule

instruction: What Kubernetes version is applicable for changing the container runtime from Docker Engine to containerd?
response: Kubernetes 1.23 or earlier.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd

instruction: What is the command to drain a node before changing the container runtime?
response: kubectl drain <node-to-drain> --ignore-daemonsets, replacing <node-to-drain> with the name of the node being drained.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd

instruction: How do you stop the Docker daemon during the container runtime change process?
response: You stop the Docker daemon by running the commands 'systemctl stop kubelet' and 'systemctl disable docker.service --now'.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd

instruction: What are the steps to install containerd on a Linux system?
response: The steps include installing the containerd.io package from the official Docker repositories, configuring containerd by running 'sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd' and 'containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml', and then restarting containerd with 'sudo systemctl restart containerd'.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd

instruction: What command is used to configure containerd on a Windows system?
response: The command 'cd $Env:ProgramFilescontainerd; .containerd.exe config default | Out-File config.toml -Encoding ascii' is used to configure containerd on a Windows system.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd

instruction: How do I set up kubectl autocomplete in bash?
response: To set up autocomplete in bash, use the command `source <(kubectl completion bash)`. To add it permanently, add `source <(kubectl completion bash)` to your `~/.bashrc` file.
title: kubectl Quick Reference

instruction: What is the shorthand for the --all-namespaces flag in kubectl?
response: The shorthand for the `--all-namespaces` flag is `-A`. For example, `kubectl -A` is equivalent to `kubectl --all-namespaces`.
title: kubectl Quick Reference

instruction: How do I view the merged kubeconfig settings?
response: To view the merged kubeconfig settings, use the command `kubectl config view`. This command displays the merged configuration from all kubeconfig files.
title: kubectl Quick Reference

instruction: How do I get the list of users from the kubeconfig file?
response: To get the list of users from the kubeconfig file, use the command `kubectl config view -o jsonpath='{.users[*].name}'`. This command displays a list of all user names in the kubeconfig file.
title: kubectl Quick Reference

instruction: How do I set the default context to a specific cluster?
response: To set the default context to a specific cluster, use the command `kubectl config use-context <cluster-name>`. Replace `<cluster-name>` with the name of the cluster you want to set as the default context.
title: kubectl Quick Reference

instruction: What is the primary function of the cloud-controller-manager in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The cloud-controller-manager is a Kubernetes control plane component that embeds cloud-specific control logic, allowing you to link your cluster into your cloud provider's API and separating out the components that interact with that cloud platform from components that only interact with your cluster.
title: Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: How does the cloud-controller-manager enable cloud providers to release features at a different pace compared to the main Kubernetes project?
response: The cloud-controller-manager enables cloud providers to release features at a different pace compared to the main Kubernetes project by decoupling the interoperability logic between Kubernetes and the underlying cloud infrastructure, using a plugin mechanism that allows different cloud providers to integrate their platforms with Kubernetes.
title: Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: What are the responsibilities of the node controller in the cloud controller manager?
response: The node controller is responsible for updating Node objects when new servers are created in your cloud infrastructure, annotating and labelling the Node object with cloud-specific information, obtaining the node's hostname and network addresses, verifying the node's health, and deleting the Node object from your Kubernetes cluster if the node has been deleted from the cloud.
title: Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: Can the cloud controller manager be run as a Kubernetes addon rather than as part of the control plane?
response: Yes, the cloud controller manager can be run as a Kubernetes addon rather than as part of the control plane.
title: Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: In what form does the cloud controller manager typically run in the control plane?
response: The cloud controller manager typically runs in the control plane as a replicated set of processes, usually containers in Pods, with each cloud-controller-manager implementing multiple controllers in a single process.
title: Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: What is the purpose of the ShardInfo in Kubernetes?
response: ShardInfo describes the shard selector that was applied to produce a list response, indicating the list is a filtered subset.

instruction: What information does the selector field in ShardInfo provide?
response: The selector field is the shard selector string from the request, echoed back so clients can verify which shard they received and merge responses from multiple shards.

instruction: How can a client determine which shard they received a response from?
response: A client can determine which shard they received a response from by verifying the shard selector string in the ShardInfo, which is echoed back from the request.

instruction: What is the primary interface for exposing networking for Windows containers in Kubernetes?
response: Networking for Windows containers is exposed through CNI plugins.
title: Networking on Windows

instruction: How do Windows containers function in regards to networking, and what components are involved?
response: Windows containers function similarly to virtual machines in regards to networking. Each container has a virtual network adapter (vNIC) which is connected to a Hyper-V virtual switch (vSwitch). The Host Networking Service (HNS) and the Host Compute Service (HCS) work together to create containers and attach container vNICs to networks.
title: Networking on Windows

instruction: What are the responsibilities of the Host Compute Service (HCS) and the Host Networking Service (HNS) in Windows container networking?
response: HCS is responsible for the management of containers, whereas HNS is responsible for the management of networking resources such as virtual networks, endpoints/vNICs, namespaces, and policies including packet encapsulations, load-balancing rules, ACLs, and NAT rules.
title: Networking on Windows

instruction: What are the differences in storing network configurations between Windows and Linux, and how do CNI implementations need to adapt to this?
response: Many configurations such as DNS, routes, and metrics are stored in the Windows registry database rather than as files inside /etc, which is how Linux stores those configurations. The Windows registry for the container is separate from that of the host. CNI implementations need to call the HNS instead of relying on file mappings to pass network details into the pod or container.
title: Networking on Windows

instruction: What are the different networking drivers/modes supported by Windows, and what considerations should be taken into account when selecting a networking solution for a heterogeneous cluster?
response: Windows supports five different networking drivers/modes: L2bridge, L2tunnel, Overlay (Beta), Transparent, and NAT. In a heterogeneous cluster with Windows and Linux worker nodes, you need to select a networking solution that is compatible on both Windows and Linux.
title: Networking on Windows

instruction: What happens to singular and embedded struct fields during the configuration merging process in Kubelet?
response: The configuration merging process handles the overriding of singular and embedded struct fields to create a resulting kubelet configuration. For instance, you may want a baseline kubelet configuration for all nodes, but you may want to customize the address and authorization fields.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging

instruction: How do you override the slices/lists values of the kubelet configuration?
response: You can override the slices/lists values of the kubelet configuration. However, the entire list gets overridden during the merging process. For example, you can override the clusterDNS list by specifying a new list in a file in the --config-dir directory.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging

instruction: What is the resulting configuration when overriding the authorization field in the Kubelet configuration?
response: The resulting configuration will have the overridden authorization field. For example, if you override the authorization field with mode: AlwaysAllow, the resulting configuration will have mode: AlwaysAllow, and the webhook cacheAuthorizedTTL and cacheUnauthorizedTTL will also be overridden if specified.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging

instruction: Can individual fields in maps be selectively overridden during configuration merging?
response: Yes, individual fields in maps, regardless of their value types (boolean, string, etc.), can be selectively overridden during configuration merging.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging

instruction: What happens to the address field when a new value is specified in a file in the --config-dir directory?
response: The address field will be overridden with the new value specified in the file in the --config-dir directory. For example, if the main configuration file has address: 192.168.0.1 and a file in the --config-dir directory has address: 192.168.0.8, the resulting configuration will have address: 192.168.0.8.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging

instruction: What is the purpose of the TokenReview in Kubernetes?
response: TokenReview attempts to authenticate a token to a known user.
title: TokenReview

instruction: What happens to TokenReview requests in the kube-apiserver?
response: TokenReview requests may be cached by the webhook token authenticator plugin in the kube-apiserver.
title: TokenReview

instruction: What is the significance of the 'audiences' field in TokenReviewSpec?
response: The 'audiences' field is a list of the identifiers that the resource server presented with the token identifies as. Audience-aware token authenticators will verify that the token was intended for at least one of the audiences in this list.
title: TokenReview

instruction: What is the default audience if no audiences are provided in the TokenReviewSpec?
response: If no audiences are provided, the audience will default to the audience of the Kubernetes apiserver.
title: TokenReview

instruction: What should a client of the TokenReview API do to ensure compatibility with the TokenReview server?
response: A client of the TokenReview API that sets the spec.audiences field should validate that a compatible audience identifier is returned in the status.audiences field to ensure that the TokenReview server is audience-compatible.
title: TokenReview

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes version required to migrate from PodSecurityPolicies to the built-in PodSecurity admission controller?
response: The minimum Kubernetes version required is v1.22. You can check your version by running the command 'kubectl version'.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller

instruction: What are the limitations of Pod Security Admission compared to PodSecurityPolicy?
response: Pod Security Admission is less flexible than PodSecurityPolicy, with limitations including the inability to set default security constraints, fine-grained control over policy definition, and sub-namespace policy granularity. Additionally, it is a non-mutating admission controller and only supports 3 standard levels.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller

instruction: What should I do if I was relying on the mutating aspect of PodSecurityPolicy?
response: If you were relying on the mutating aspect of PodSecurityPolicy, you will need to either modify your workloads to meet the Pod Security constraints or use a Mutating Admission Webhook to make those changes.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller

instruction: How can I enforce more specific constraints than the 3 standard levels provided by Pod Security Admission?
response: If you require more control over specific constraints, then you will need to use a Validating Admission Webhook to enforce those policies, as Pod Security Admission only supports the 3 standard levels.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller

instruction: What is the first step in the migration process from PodSecurityPolicy to Pod Security Admission?
response: The first step in the migration process is to decide whether Pod Security Admission is the right fit for your use case, considering its limitations and your specific security needs.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller

instruction: What are the two scoring strategies that support bin packing of resources in the NodeResourcesFit scheduling-plugin of kube-scheduler?
response: The two scoring strategies are MostAllocated and RequestedToCapacityRatio.
title: Resource Bin Packing

instruction: How does the MostAllocated strategy score nodes in the context of resource bin packing?
response: The MostAllocated strategy scores nodes based on the utilization of resources, favoring the ones with higher allocation. For each resource type, you can set a weight to modify its influence in the node score.
title: Resource Bin Packing

instruction: What is the purpose of the weights assigned to resources in the MostAllocated strategy?
response: The weights assigned to resources in the MostAllocated strategy modify the influence of each resource type in the node score. Resources with higher weights have more influence on the final node score.
title: Resource Bin Packing

instruction: What is the main difference between the MostAllocated and RequestedToCapacityRatio strategies?
response: The MostAllocated strategy favors nodes with higher allocation, while the RequestedToCapacityRatio strategy allows users to specify resources and weights to score nodes based on the request to capacity ratio, favoring nodes according to a configured function of the allocated resources.
title: Resource Bin Packing

instruction: How can the behavior of the RequestedToCapacityRatio strategy be controlled in the NodeResourcesFit score function?
response: The behavior of the RequestedToCapacityRatio strategy can be controlled by the scoringStrategy field, where you can configure two parameters: requestedToCapacityRatio and resources. The shape in the requestedToCapacityRatio parameter allows the user to tune the function as least requested or most requested based.
title: Resource Bin Packing

instruction: What are the steps to configure a Pod to use a PersistentVolumeClaim for storage?
response: The steps are: create a PersistentVolume backed by physical storage, create a PersistentVolumeClaim that is automatically bound to a suitable PersistentVolume, and create a Pod that uses the above PersistentVolumeClaim for storage.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage

instruction: What is the purpose of creating an index.html file on the Node?
response: The purpose of creating an index.html file on the Node is to test that the file exists and can be accessed, with the output 'Hello from Kubernetes storage'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage

instruction: What type of PersistentVolume is created in this exercise and what is it used for?
response: A hostPath PersistentVolume is created in this exercise, which uses a file or directory on the Node to emulate network-attached storage, and is used for development and testing on a single-node cluster.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage

instruction: What is the alternative to using hostPath in a production cluster?
response: In a production cluster, instead of using hostPath, a cluster administrator would provision a network resource like a Google Compute Engine persistent disk, an NFS share, or an Amazon Elastic Block Store volume, and can also use StorageClasses to set up dynamic provisioning.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage

instruction: What is the command to open a shell to the Node if you are using Minikube?
response: The command to open a shell to the Node if you are using Minikube is 'minikube ssh'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage

instruction: What is the basic syntax to create a role with a single rule in Kubernetes?
response: The basic syntax to create a role with a single rule is kubectl create role NAME --verb=verb --resource=resource.group/subresource [--resource-name=resourcename] [--dry-run=server|client|none].
title: kubectl create role

instruction: How do I create a role named 'pod-reader' that allows users to perform 'get', 'watch', and 'list' on pods?
response: You can create a role named 'pod-reader' that allows users to perform 'get', 'watch', and 'list' on pods by running the command kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get --verb=list --verb=watch --resource=pods.
title: kubectl create role

instruction: What is the purpose of the --resource-name flag when creating a role?
response: The --resource-name flag is used to specify the resource in the white list that the rule applies to. This flag can be repeated for multiple items.
title: kubectl create role

instruction: What are the possible values for the --dry-run flag when creating a role?
response: The possible values for the --dry-run flag are 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If 'client' strategy is used, only the object that would be sent is printed, without sending it. If 'server' strategy is used, a server-side request is submitted without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create role

instruction: What is the default output format for the kubectl create role command?
response: The default output format is not explicitly stated, but the -o flag can be used to specify the output format, which can be one of: json, yaml, kyaml, name, go-template, go-template-file, template, templatefile, jsonpath, jsonpath-as-json, jsonpath-file.
title: kubectl create role

instruction: What are the prerequisites to start debugging DNS resolution in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. The cluster must be configured to use the CoreDNS addon or its precursor, kube-dns, and the Kubernetes server must be at or later than version v1.6.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution

instruction: How can you verify the version of your Kubernetes server?
response: To check the version, enter the command 'kubectl version'.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution

instruction: What is the purpose of creating a simple Pod using the dnsutils.yaml manifest?
response: The purpose of creating a simple Pod using the dnsutils.yaml manifest is to use it as a test environment to debug DNS problems. This Pod can be used to run the 'nslookup' command and verify DNS name resolution for services.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution

instruction: What should you check first if the 'nslookup' command fails?
response: If the 'nslookup' command fails, you should check the local DNS configuration first by taking a look inside the resolv.conf file. Verify that the search path and name server are set up correctly.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution

instruction: What indicates a problem with the CoreDNS (or kube-dns) add-on or with associated Services?
response: Errors such as 'nslookup: ca' when running the 'nslookup' command indicate a problem with the CoreDNS (or kube-dns) add-on or with associated Services.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution

instruction: Who is the target audience for the Extending Kubernetes guide?
response: The guide is aimed at cluster operators who want to understand how to adapt their Kubernetes cluster to the needs of their work environment. Developers who are prospective Platform Developers or Kubernetes Project Contributors will also find it useful.
title: Extending Kubernetes

instruction: What are the two broad categories of customization approaches for a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The two broad categories are configuration, which involves changing command line arguments, local configuration files, or API resources, and extensions, which involve running additional programs, additional network services, or both.
title: Extending Kubernetes

instruction: Why are configuration files and command arguments not recommended for customization?
response: They may not always be changeable in a hosted Kubernetes service or a distribution with managed installation. When they are changeable, they are usually only changeable by the cluster operator. Also, they are subject to change in future Kubernetes versions, and setting them may require restarting processes.
title: Extending Kubernetes

instruction: What are some examples of built-in policy APIs in Kubernetes?
response: Examples include ResourceQuota, NetworkPolicy, and Role-based Access Control (RBAC). These APIs provide declaratively configured policy settings and follow the same conventions as other Kubernetes resources such as Pods.
title: Extending Kubernetes

instruction: What is the benefit of using a stable policy API in Kubernetes?
response: When you use a policy API that is stable, you benefit from a defined support policy like other Kubernetes APIs.
title: Extending Kubernetes

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl create service nodeport command?
response: The kubectl create service nodeport command is used to create a NodePort service with the specified name.
title: kubectl create service nodeport

instruction: How do you specify the port pairs when creating a NodePort service?
response: Port pairs can be specified as '<port>:<targetPort>' using the --tcp option. For example, --tcp=5678:8080.
title: kubectl create service nodeport

instruction: What is the purpose of the --node-port option when creating a NodePort service?
response: The --node-port option is used to specify the port used to expose the service on each node in a cluster.
title: kubectl create service nodeport

instruction: What is the effect of using the --dry-run option with kubectl create service nodeport?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to print the object that would be sent without sending it. It can take one of three values: 'none', 'server', or 'client'.
title: kubectl create service nodeport

instruction: How do you save the configuration of the current object when creating a NodePort service?
response: You can save the configuration of the current object by using the --save-config option. This will save the configuration in its annotation.
title: kubectl create service nodeport

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes server version required for official support of dual-stack networking?
response: The minimum Kubernetes server version required for official support of dual-stack networking is v1.23.
title: Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: How do you validate that a node has both IPv4 and IPv6 interfaces detected?
response: You can validate that a node has both IPv4 and IPv6 interfaces detected by running the command: kubectl get nodes <node-name> -o go-template --template='{{range .status.addresses}}{{printf "%s: %s\n" .type .address}}{{end}}'. This should display both InternalIP addresses for IPv4 and IPv6.
title: Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: How do you verify that a Pod has both an IPv4 and IPv6 address assigned?
response: You can verify that a Pod has both an IPv4 and IPv6 address assigned by running the command: kubectl get pods <pod-name> -o go-template --template='{{range .status.podIPs}}{{printf "%s\n" .ip}}{{end}}'. This should display both the IPv4 and IPv6 addresses of the Pod.
title: Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: What is the expected output for the Pod's IP addresses using the Downward API via the status.podIPs fieldPath?
response: The expected output for the Pod's IP addresses using the Downward API via the status.podIPs fieldPath is a comma-separated list of the Pod's IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. For example: 10.244.1.4,2001:db8::4.
title: Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: How do you validate IPv4/IPv6 Pod address ranges are configured for a node?
response: You can validate IPv4/IPv6 Pod address ranges are configured for a node by running the command: kubectl get nodes <node-name> -o go-template --template='{{range .spec.podCIDRs}}{{printf "%s\n" .}}{{end}}'. This should display one IPv4 block and one IPv6 block allocated for the node.
title: Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: What is the format of the hostname set for pods in a Job in Indexed completion mode?
response: The hostname is set in the format of ${jobName}-${completionIndex}.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication

instruction: Why would you use a headless Service to enable pod-to-pod communication in a Job?
response: A headless Service is used to enable pod-to-pod communication in a Job by creating DNS records for the hostnames of the pods running the Job, allowing pods to communicate with each other using pod hostnames rather than pod IP addresses.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication

instruction: What label selector should be used for a headless Service to target pods created by a Job?
response: The label selector should be set to job-name: <your-job-name>, where <your-job-name> is the name of the Job. This is because Kubernetes automatically adds the job-name label to the pods created by the Job.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication

instruction: What is the purpose of setting the subdomain field in a Job template spec?
response: The subdomain field is set to enable the headless service as a subdomain service for the Job pods, allowing pods to communicate with each other using pod hostnames.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication

instruction: What is a recommended Kubernetes version to run this tutorial on?
response: Your Kubernetes server must be at or later than version v1.21.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'operations' field in NamedRuleWithOperations?
response: The 'operations' field specifies the operations the admission hook cares about. It can include CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, CONNECT, or '*' for all of those operations and any future admission operations that are added.
title: NamedRuleWithOperations

instruction: What does the '*' character signify in the 'apiGroups', 'apiVersions', and 'operations' fields?
response: The '*' character signifies all groups, versions, or operations respectively. If '*' is present, the length of the slice must be one.
title: NamedRuleWithOperations

instruction: How do you specify that a rule applies to all resources and their subresources?
response: To specify that a rule applies to all resources and their subresources, you can use '*/*' in the 'resources' field.
title: NamedRuleWithOperations

instruction: What are the possible values for the 'scope' field in NamedRuleWithOperations?
response: The possible values for the 'scope' field are 'Cluster', 'Namespaced', and '*'. 'Cluster' means only cluster-scoped resources will match, 'Namespaced' means only namespaced resources will match, and '*' means there are no scope restrictions.
title: NamedRuleWithOperations

instruction: What does an empty 'resourceNames' field signify?
response: An empty 'resourceNames' field signifies that everything is allowed, meaning the rule applies to all resource names.
title: NamedRuleWithOperations

instruction: What is the feature state of event-based updates to container status in Kubernetes v1.26?
response: The feature is in alpha state and disabled by default in Kubernetes v1.26.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status

instruction: Why does the Generic PLEG approach incur overhead?
response: The Generic PLEG approach incurs non-negligible overhead due to frequent polling of container statuses, which is exacerbated by Kubelet's parallelized polling of container states.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status

instruction: How do I enable Evented PLEG on a node?
response: To enable Evented PLEG, start the Kubelet with the feature gate EventedPLEG enabled by editing the kubelet config file and restarting the kubelet service. You need to do this on each node where you are using this feature.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status

instruction: What is required from the container runtime to use Evented PLEG?
response: The container runtime must support container lifecycle events. For example, containerd and CRI-O versions 1.7+ and 1.26+ respectively are supported.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status

instruction: How do I verify if CRI-O is configured to emit CRI events?
response: You can verify if CRI-O is configured to emit CRI events by running the command 'crio config | grep enable_pod_events'. If it is enabled, the output should show 'enable_pod_events = true'.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status

instruction: What is the purpose of using a work queue in this Kubernetes Job example?
response: The work queue is used to hold tasks that need to be processed by worker pods. Each pod picks up one unit of work from the queue, processes it, and repeats until the end of the queue is reached.
title: Fine Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue

instruction: Why was Redis chosen as the storage service for the work queue in this example?
response: Redis was chosen because AMQP (used in the previous example with RabbitMQ) does not provide a good way for clients to detect when a finite-length work queue is empty.
title: Fine Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue

instruction: What are the prerequisites for running this tutorial?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Fine Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue

instruction: How do you start a single instance of Redis for this example?
response: To start a single instance of Redis, you need to create the redis pod and redis service by running the following commands: kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/application/job/redis/redis-pod.yaml and kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/application/job/redis/redis-service.yaml
title: Fine Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue

instruction: What is the role of the Job in this example?
response: The Job starts several pods, each taking one task from the message queue, processing it, and repeating until the end of the queue is reached.
title: Fine Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue

instruction: What is the purpose of API Priority and Fairness in Kubernetes?
response: The API Priority and Fairness feature is designed to control the behavior of the Kubernetes API server in an overload situation, ensuring that the most important requests get through in a period of high traffic.
title: API Priority and Fairness

instruction: How does API Priority and Fairness classify and handle requests?
response: API Priority and Fairness classifies and isolates requests in a fine-grained way, introducing a limited amount of queuing so that no requests are rejected in cases of very brief bursts. Requests are dispatched from queues using a fair queuing technique.
title: API Priority and Fairness

instruction: Are all requests subject to the API Priority and Fairness filter?
response: No, some requests classified as 'long-running'—such as remote command execution or log tailing—are not subject to the API Priority and Fairness filter. This also applies to watch requests when API Priority and Fairness is disabled.
title: API Priority and Fairness

instruction: How can you disable the API Priority and Fairness feature?
response: The API Priority and Fairness feature is controlled by a command-line flag and is enabled by default. To disable it, you need to set the --enable-priority-and-fairness flag to false when invoking the kube-apiserver.
title: API Priority and Fairness

instruction: What is the command-line flag to disable the v1beta3 version of the API Priority and Fairness API group?
response: To disable the v1beta3 version of the API Priority and Fairness API group, you need to add the following command-line flags to your kube-apiserver invocation: --runtime-config=flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1beta3=false
title: API Priority and Fairness

instruction: How do I find out what container runtime is used on a node in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can use kubectl to fetch and show node information by running the command kubectl get nodes -o wide. The CONTAINER-RUNTIME column will output the runtime and its version.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node

instruction: What is the command to check the container runtime used on a node?
response: kubectl get nodes -o wide
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node

instruction: How can I determine if I am affected by the removal of dockershim in Kubernetes v1.24?
response: You can check the runtime endpoint to see if you use dockershim. If you don't use dockershim, you aren't affected. You can also check if you are using Docker Engine with cri-dockerd, in which case you aren't affected.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node

instruction: How do I find out what container runtime endpoint my nodes use?
response: You can check the kubelet configuration on your nodes by reading the starting commands for the kubelet process using the command tr \0 ' ' < /proc/$(pgrep kubelet)/cmdline
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node

instruction: What information is output in the CONTAINER-RUNTIME column when using Docker Engine and containerd?
response: For Docker Engine, the output is similar to docker://19.3.1. For containerd, the output is similar to containerd://1.4.1.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node

instruction: What is the purpose of MatchResources in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy?
response: MatchResources decides whether to run the admission control policy on an object based on whether it meets the match criteria.
title: MatchResources

instruction: What is the relationship between exclude and include rules in MatchResources?
response: The exclude rules take precedence over include rules. If a resource matches both, it is excluded.
title: MatchResources

instruction: What are the possible values for matchPolicy in MatchResources?
response: The possible values for matchPolicy are 'Exact' and 'Equivalent'. 'Exact' matches a request only if it exactly matches a specified rule, while 'Equivalent' matches a request if it modifies a resource listed in rules, even via another API group or version.
title: MatchResources

instruction: How does namespaceSelector affect the admission control policy?
response: namespaceSelector decides whether to run the admission control policy on an object based on whether the namespace for that object matches the selector. If the object itself is a namespace, the matching is performed on object.metadata.labels.
title: MatchResources

instruction: What happens to a request if the matchPolicy is set to 'Equivalent' and the request's API group or version does not exactly match a specified rule?
response: If the matchPolicy is set to 'Equivalent' and the request's API group or version does not exactly match a specified rule, but the request modifies a resource listed in rules, the request will be converted to the API group and version specified in the rule and sent to the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy.
title: MatchResources

instruction: What is the purpose of a Binding object in Kubernetes?
response: A Binding object ties one object to another; for example, a pod is bound to a node by a scheduler.
title: Binding

instruction: What is the significance of the 'target' field in a Binding object?
response: The 'target' field is an ObjectReference that specifies the target object that you want to bind to the standard object.
title: Binding

instruction: What is the API version of the Binding object?
response: The API version of the Binding object is 'v1'.
title: Binding

instruction: Can the 'kind' field of a Binding object be updated?
response: No, the 'kind' field of a Binding object cannot be updated. Servers may infer this from the endpoint the client submits requests to.
title: Binding

instruction: What is the metadata field in a Binding object used for?
response: The metadata field in a Binding object is used for standard object's metadata. More information can be found at https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata.
title: Binding

instruction: How can I add entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file?
response: You can add entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file using HostAliases.
title: Networking

instruction: Can I change the IP range for Services in my cluster?
response: Yes, you can extend Service IP ranges. The process is described under 'Extend Service IP Ranges'.
title: Networking

instruction: What is the process for reconfiguring the default ServiceCIDR in Kubernetes?
response: The process for reconfiguring the default ServiceCIDR in Kubernetes is described under 'Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration'.
title: Networking

instruction: How can I validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack by following the relevant documentation under 'Validate IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack'.
title: Networking

instruction: Where should I ask specific questions about using Kubernetes?
response: You should ask specific, answerable questions about how to use Kubernetes on Stack Overflow.
title: Networking

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl set command?
response: The kubectl set command is used to configure application resources. These commands help you make changes to existing application resources.
title: kubectl set

instruction: How can I specify a namespace for the kubectl set command?
response: You can specify a namespace for the kubectl set command using the -n or --namespace flag followed by the namespace name.
title: kubectl set

instruction: What is the default cache directory used by kubectl set?
response: The default cache directory used by kubectl set is $HOME/.kube/cache.
title: kubectl set

instruction: How can I capture a profile using kubectl set?
response: You can capture a profile using kubectl set by specifying the --profile flag followed by the profile name (one of none, cpu, heap, goroutine, threadcreate, block, mutex, or trace) and the --profile-output flag followed by the output file name.
title: kubectl set

instruction: What is the default request timeout for kubectl set commands?
response: The default request timeout for kubectl set commands is 0, which means requests will not timeout.
title: kubectl set

instruction: What is the maximum allowed size of the values in the Data field of a Secret?
response: The total bytes of the values in the Data field must be less than MaxSecretSize bytes.
title: Secret

instruction: What is the purpose of the stringData field in a Secret?
response: stringData allows specifying non-binary secret data in string form. It is provided as a write-only input field for convenience. All keys and values are merged into the data field on write, overwriting any existing values.
title: Secret

instruction: What happens when the immutable field of a Secret is set to true?
response: If set to true, ensures that data stored in the Secret cannot be updated (only object metadata can be modified).
title: Secret

instruction: What are the restrictions on the keys in the Data field of a Secret?
response: Each key must consist of alphanumeric characters, '-', '_' or '.'.
title: Secret

instruction: How is the secret data in a Secret stored?
response: The serialized form of the secret data is a base64 encoded string, representing the arbitrary (possibly non-string) data value.
title: Secret

instruction: What happens to running containers when the kubelet restarts?
response: The containers that are already running continue to run. The kubelet re-establishes its view of the Node, and reconciles the running containers against the desired state.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart

instruction: What is the status of a node while the kubelet is restarting?
response: The node is temporarily reported as NotReady until the kubelet finishes initializing. While the node is NotReady, the scheduler does not place new Pods on it.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart

instruction: Does the kubelet preserve the readiness of running containers across a restart?
response: Yes, the kubelet preserves the readiness of running containers across a restart. Each Pod's readiness drives EndpointSlices, Endpoints, and downstream configuration.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart

instruction: What happens to garbage collection and Pod evictions during the initial kubelet startup?
response: During the initial kubelet startup, garbage collection of unused images and containers, and Pod evictions driven by node-pressure, are paused.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart

instruction: What is the effect of the ChangeContainerStatusOnKubeletRestart feature gate?
response: The ChangeContainerStatusOnKubeletRestart feature gate lets you revert to the deprecated behavior of resetting container readiness to false on every restart, but it is slated for removal and should not be relied upon.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart

instruction: How do you create a secret for use with a Docker registry using kubectl?
response: You can create a secret for use with a Docker registry using kubectl by running the command 'kubectl create secret docker-registry NAME --docker-username=user --docker-password=password --docker-email=email [--docker-server=string] [--from-file=[key=]source] [--dry-run=server|client|none]'.
title: kubectl create secret docker-registry

instruction: What is the purpose of a dockercfg secret in Kubernetes?
response: A dockercfg secret is used to authenticate against Docker registries. It provides the credentials for the nodes to pull images on behalf of the application.
title: kubectl create secret docker-registry

instruction: How can you create a dockercfg secret directly?
response: You can create a dockercfg secret directly by running the command 'kubectl create secret docker-registry my-secret --docker-server=DOCKER_REGISTRY_SERVER --docker-username=DOCKER_USER --docker-password=DOCKER_PASSWORD --docker-email=DOCKER_EMAIL'.
title: kubectl create secret docker-registry

instruction: What is the default server location for Docker registry?
response: The default server location for Docker registry is 'https://index.docker.io/v1/'.
title: kubectl create secret docker-registry

instruction: How can you create a new secret named my-secret from ~/.docker/config.json?
response: You can create a new secret named my-secret from ~/.docker/config.json by running the command 'kubectl create secret docker-registry my-secret --from-file=path/to/.docker/config.json'.
title: kubectl create secret docker-registry

instruction: What is the purpose of the WatchEvent in Kubernetes?
response: WatchEvent represents a single event to a watched resource.
title: WatchEvent

instruction: What is the content of the 'object' field in a WatchEvent depending on the 'type' field?
response: If Type is Added or Modified, the object field contains the new state of the object. If Type is Deleted, it contains the state of the object immediately before deletion. If Type is Error, Status is recommended; other types may make sense depending on context.
title: WatchEvent

instruction: What is the data type of the 'type' field in a WatchEvent?
response: The data type of the 'type' field in a WatchEvent is string.
title: WatchEvent

instruction: What is the data type of the 'object' field in a WatchEvent?
response: The data type of the 'object' field in a WatchEvent is '*', which implies it can be of any type.
title: WatchEvent

instruction: How should the 'object' field be interpreted when the 'type' field is 'Error'?
response: When the 'type' field is 'Error', the 'Status' is recommended for the 'object' field; however, other types may make sense depending on the context.
title: WatchEvent

instruction: What are the two types of resources that make up a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Kubernetes cluster consists of two types of resources: The Control Plane and Nodes.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster

instruction: What is the role of the Control Plane in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The Control Plane coordinates all activities in your cluster, such as scheduling applications, maintaining applications' desired state, scaling applications, and rolling out new updates.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster

instruction: What is a node in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A node is a VM or a physical computer that serves as a worker machine in a Kubernetes cluster. Each node has a Kube
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster

instruction: What is required to run the shell commands in this tutorial on a Windows system?
response: Windows users must use a POSIX-compatible shell such as Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) or Git Bash to run the commands as written.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster

instruction: What needs to be installed on your system before you can start a Kubernetes cluster using Minikube?
response: You need to have minikube and kubectl installed on your system. See minikube start for installation instructions and Install tools for kubectl installation instructions.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster

response: You can create a Deployment in Kubernetes using kubectl.

instruction: What command can I use to create a Deployment?
response: The documentation does not specify a particular command, but it mentions using kubectl to create a Deployment.
