instruction: How can I access a service running on a Kubernetes cluster from outside the cluster?
response: You have several options: access services through public IPs, use a service with type NodePort or LoadBalancer, place pods behind services, use the Proxy Verb, or access from a node or pod in the cluster.
title: Access Services Running on Clusters

instruction: What are the implications of exposing a service to the internet using type NodePort or LoadBalancer?
response: Exposing a service to the internet may make it insecure. You should consider whether the service being exposed does its own authentication.
title: Access Services Running on Clusters

instruction: How can I access a specific pod from a set of replicas for debugging purposes?
response: Place a unique label on the pod and create a new service which selects this label.
title: Access Services Running on Clusters

instruction: What are the limitations of using the Proxy Verb to access services?
response: Proxies may cause problems for some web applications, only works for HTTP/HTTPS.
title: Access Services Running on Clusters

instruction: Can I access cluster services by ssh-ing to a node in the cluster?
response: Some clusters may allow you to ssh to a node in the cluster, but this is a non-standard method and may not work on all clusters.
title: Access Services Running on Clusters

instruction: How do I create a pod disruption budget named my-pdb that selects all pods with the app=rails label and requires at least one of them to be available?
response: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget my-pdb --selector=app=rails --min-available=1
title: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

instruction: What is the purpose of the --min-available option when creating a pod disruption budget?
response: The --min-available option specifies the minimum number or percentage of available pods that the budget requires.
title: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

instruction: Can I use a percentage value for --min-available or --max-unavailable?
response: Yes, you can use a percentage value by appending a '%' symbol to the number, for example, '50%'.
title: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

instruction: How do I create a pod disruption budget that requires at least half of the selected pods to be available?
response: You can use the --min-available option with a percentage value, for example, kubectl create pdb my-pdb --selector=app=nginx --min-available=50%
title: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

instruction: What is the effect of using --dry-run=server when creating a pod disruption budget?
response: When using --dry-run=server, kubectl will submit a server-side request without persisting the resource, allowing you to test the creation of the pod disruption budget without actually creating it.
title: kubectl create poddisruptionbudget

instruction: What is the purpose of the caBundle field in WebhookClientConfig?
response: The caBundle field is a PEM encoded CA bundle which will be used to validate the webhook's server certificate. If unspecified, system trust roots on the apiserver are used.
title: WebhookClientConfig

instruction: How do I specify the location of a webhook when it is running outside the cluster?
response: When the webhook is running outside the cluster, you should use the `url` field to specify its location in standard URL form (`scheme://host:port/path`). The scheme must be 'https'.
title: WebhookClientConfig

instruction: Can I use 'localhost' or '127.0.0.1' as the host in the url field of WebhookClientConfig?
response: Using 'localhost' or '127.0.0.1' as the host is risky unless you take great care to run this webhook on all hosts which run an apiserver that might need to make calls to this webhook. Such installs are likely to be non-portable.
title: WebhookClientConfig

instruction: What are the requirements for the url field in WebhookClientConfig?
response: The url field must be in standard URL form (`scheme://host:port/path`), with the scheme being 'https'. Fragments ('#...') and query parameters ('?...') are not allowed. You may use the path to pass an arbitrary string to the webhook.
title: WebhookClientConfig

instruction: How do I configure a webhook that is running within the cluster?
response: If the webhook is running within the cluster, you should use the `service` field to reference the service for this webhook.
title: WebhookClientConfig

instruction: What are the allowed values for the imagePullPolicy field in kubeadm InitConfiguration and JoinConfiguration?
response: The allowed values for the imagePullPolicy field are 'Always', 'Never', or 'IfNotPresent'. The default value is 'IfNotPresent'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3)

instruction: How can you configure kubeadm to skip certain phases during the init or join command execution?
response: You can configure kubeadm to skip certain phases during the init or join command execution by using the 'skipPhases' field in the InitConfiguration or JoinConfiguration. This field allows specifying a list of phases to skip.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3)

instruction: What is the default behavior for image pulling in kubeadm prior to the addition of the imagePullPolicy field?
response: The default behavior for image pulling in kubeadm prior to the addition of the imagePullPolicy field was 'IfNotPresent'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3)

instruction: How can you specify patches for components deployed by kubeadm?
response: You can specify patches for components deployed by kubeadm by using the 'patches.directory' field in the InitConfiguration or JoinConfiguration. This field allows configuring a directory from which to take patches.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3)

instruction: What is the recommended way to configure kubeadm?
response: The preferred way to configure kubeadm is to pass a YAML configuration file with the --config option.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3)

instruction: What is the primary difference between using labels and annotations to attach metadata to Kubernetes objects?
response: Labels can be used to select objects and find collections of objects that satisfy certain conditions, whereas annotations are not used to identify and select objects.
title: Annotations

instruction: What type of data can be stored in annotations?
response: Annotations can store small or large, structured or unstructured metadata, including characters not permitted by labels, such as numeric, boolean, list or other types, but keys and values must be strings.
title: Annotations

instruction: What are some examples of information that could be recorded in annotations?
response: Examples include fields managed by a declarative configuration layer, build, release, or image information, pointers to logging, monitoring, analytics, or audit repositories, client library or tool information, user or tool/system provenance information, lightweight rollout tool metadata, phone or pager numbers of persons responsible, and directives from the end-user to the implementations to modify behavior or engage non-standard features.
title: Annotations

instruction: What is the syntax for annotation keys?
response: Annotation keys have two segments: an optional prefix and name, separated by a slash (/), with the name segment being required and 63 characters or less.
title: Annotations

instruction: Why might you choose to use annotations instead of an external database or directory to store metadata?
response: Using annotations makes it easier to produce shared client libraries and tools for deployment, management, introspection, and the like, as the metadata is directly associated with the object.
title: Annotations

instruction: How can I customize the flags passed to control plane components such as the APIServer, ControllerManager, Scheduler, and Etcd?
response: You can customize the flags by using the ClusterConfiguration structure, specifically by adding extraArgs to the apiServer, controllerManager, scheduler, or etcd structures.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API

instruction: How do I override a flag for a control plane component using kubeadm?
response: To override a flag, add the appropriate extraArgs to your configuration, add flags to the extraArgs field, and then run kubeadm init with --config <YOUR CONFIG YAML>.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API

instruction: Can I apply individual configuration per component on different nodes?
response: The ClusterConfiguration object is currently global in kubeadm clusters, but you can use patches to apply individual configuration per component on different nodes.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API

instruction: What happens if I pass the same flag multiple times or use duplicate flags (keys)?
response: Duplicate flags (keys) or passing the same flag multiple times is currently not supported. To work around this, you must use patches.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API

instruction: How can I generate a ClusterConfiguration object with default values?
response: You can generate a ClusterConfiguration object with default values by running kubeadm config print init-defaults and saving the output to a file of your choice.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API

instruction: How do I apply a configuration from a JSON file named 'pod.json' to a pod using kubectl?
response: You can apply the configuration in 'pod.json' to a pod using the command: kubectl apply -f ./pod.json
title: kubectl apply

instruction: What is the purpose of the --prune option in kubectl apply, and what is its current status?
response: The --prune option in kubectl apply is used to delete resources that are not in the applied file and match a specified label or selector. It is currently in Alpha status, and its functionality is not yet complete.
title: kubectl apply

instruction: How can I apply configurations from all files with a '.json' extension in the current directory?
response: You can apply the configurations from all files that end with '.json' using the command: kubectl apply -f '*.json'
title: kubectl apply

instruction: What is the use of the --all option in kubectl apply?
response: The --all option in kubectl apply is used to select all resources in the namespace of the specified resource types.
title: kubectl apply

instruction: How do I apply a configuration from a directory containing a 'kustomization.yaml' file?
response: You can apply resources from a directory containing 'kustomization.yaml' using the command: kubectl apply -k dir/
title: kubectl apply

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes server version required to perform a rollback on a DaemonSet?
response: The Kubernetes server must be at or later than version 1.7.
title: Perform a Rollback on a DaemonSet

instruction: How do I list all revisions of a DaemonSet?
response: You can list all revisions of a DaemonSet by running the command: kubectl rollout history daemonset <daemonset-name>
title: Perform a Rollback on a DaemonSet

instruction: How do I roll back to a specific revision of a DaemonSet?
response: To roll back to a specific revision, use the command: kubectl rollout undo daemonset <daemonset-name> --to-revision=<revision>
title: Perform a Rollback on a DaemonSet

instruction: What happens if I don't specify the --to-revision flag when rolling back a DaemonSet?
response: If you don't specify the --to-revision flag, kubectl picks the most recent revision.
title: Perform a Rollback on a DaemonSet

instruction: How do I watch the progress of a DaemonSet rollback?
response: You can watch the progress of the rollback by running the command: kubectl rollout status ds/<daemonset-name>
title: Perform a Rollback on a DaemonSet

instruction: What are the possible responses from the API server when requesting an eviction using the API?
response: The API server responds in one of the following ways: 200 OK (the eviction is allowed), 429 Too Many Requests (the eviction is not currently allowed), or 500 Internal Server Error (the eviction is not allowed due to misconfiguration).
title: API-initiated Eviction

instruction: How can you request eviction of a Pod using the Kubernetes API?
response: You can request eviction by creating an Eviction object using a Kubernetes language client, or by using a tool like curl or wget to access the API. You POST the attempted operation with the Eviction object details, including the Pod name and namespace.
title: API-initiated Eviction

instruction: What happens to a Pod when its eviction is allowed by the API server?
response: If the API server allows the eviction, the Pod is deleted. The Pod resource in the API server is updated with a deletion timestamp, and the Pod is terminated. This process respects configured PodDisruptionBudgets and terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
title: API-initiated Eviction

instruction: What is the difference between policy/v1 and policy/v1beta1 for Eviction objects?
response: policy/v1 Eviction is available in Kubernetes v1.22 and later. policy/v1beta1 was used in prior releases and is deprecated in v1.22 in favor of policy/v1.
title: API-initiated Eviction

instruction: Can API-initiated evictions be used to delete Pods that are part of a workload with a PodDisruptionBudget?
response: Yes, API-initiated evictions can be used to delete Pods that are part of a workload with a PodDisruptionBudget. The eviction will respect the configured PodDisruptionBudget and terminationGracePeriodSeconds. If the eviction is not allowed due to the PodDisruptionBudget, the API server will return a 429 Too Many Requests response.
title: API-initiated Eviction

instruction: What is a workload in Kubernetes?
response: A workload is an application running on Kubernetes, which can be a single component or several that work together, and is run inside a set of pods.
title: Workloads

instruction: What is a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: In Kubernetes, a Pod represents a set of one or more running containers on your cluster.
title: Workloads

instruction: What happens to a Pod if a node fails?
response: If a node fails, all the pods on that node fail. Kubernetes treats that level of failure as final, and you would need to create a new Pod to recover, even if the node later becomes healthy.
title: Workloads

instruction: What is the purpose of a Deployment in Kubernetes?
response: A Deployment is a good fit for managing a stateless application workload on your cluster, where any Pod in the Deployment is interchangeable and can be replaced if needed.
title: Workloads

instruction: What is the difference between a Job and a CronJob?
response: You can use a Job to define a task that runs to completion, just once. You can use a CronJob to run the same Job multiple times.
title: Workloads

instruction: What is the purpose of the apiVersion field in a ManagedFieldsEntry?
response: The apiVersion field defines the version of the resource that this field set applies to. It is necessary to track the version of a field set because it cannot be automatically converted. The format is 'group/version'.
title: ManagedFieldsEntry

instruction: What are the possible values for the operation field in a ManagedFieldsEntry?
response: The only valid values for the operation field are 'Apply' and 'Update'.
title: ManagedFieldsEntry

instruction: What is the purpose of the subresource field in a ManagedFieldsEntry?
response: The subresource field is the name of the subresource used to update that object, or an empty string if the object was updated through the main resource. It is used to distinguish between managers, even if they share the same name.
title: ManagedFieldsEntry

instruction: What happens to the time field in a ManagedFieldsEntry when a field is added or removed?
response: The timestamp will be updated if a field is added, the manager changes any of the owned fields value or removes a field. However, the timestamp does not update when a field is removed from the entry because another manager took it over.
title: ManagedFieldsEntry

instruction: What is the discriminator for the different fields format and version in a ManagedFieldsEntry?
response: The fieldsType field is the discriminator for the different fields format and version. There is currently only one possible value: 'FieldsV1'.
title: ManagedFieldsEntry

instruction: What is the purpose of the CredentialProviderConfig resource type in Kubelet configuration?
response: The CredentialProviderConfig resource type contains information about each exec credential provider. Kubelet reads this configuration from disk and enables each provider as specified by the CredentialProvider type.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1)

instruction: What happens when multiple credential providers match against a single image?
response: When multiple providers match against a single image, the results are combined. If providers return overlapping auth keys, the value from the provider earlier in the list is attempted first.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1)

instruction: What are the requirements for the name field in a CredentialProvider?
response: The name field is required and must match the name of the provider executable as seen by the kubelet. The executable must be in the kubelet's bin directory. It must be unique across all providers.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1)

instruction: How does the kubelet determine if a credential provider should be invoked for an image?
response: The kubelet checks if the requested image matches any of the strings in the matchImages list of the credential provider. A match exists when both the domain parts match and the URL path of the matchImage is a prefix of the target image URL path.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1)

instruction: What are the rules for matching images against a matchImage pattern?
response: A match exists when both contain the same number of domain parts and each part matches. The URL path of an imageMatch must be a prefix of the target image URL path. If the imageMatch contains a port, then the port must match in the image. Globs can be used in the domain, but not in the port or the path.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1)

instruction: What is the current state of the Workload-Aware Preemption feature in Kubernetes v1.36?
response: The Workload-Aware Preemption feature is in alpha state and disabled by default in Kubernetes v1.36.
title: Workload-Aware Preemption

instruction: How does Workload-Aware Preemption differ from default preemption when evaluating nodes for preemption?
response: Workload-Aware Preemption evaluates the entire cluster as a single domain, whereas default preemption evaluates node by node.
title: Workload-Aware Preemption

instruction: What is the hierarchy used by the scheduler to decide which units are more critical and should be spared from preemption?
response: The scheduler uses a strict hierarchy: Priority, Workload type, Group size (for PodGroups), and Start time.
title: Workload-Aware Preemption

instruction: Does the scheduler respect the priority or disruptionMode fields of a PodGroup when performing default preemption for a single Pod?
response: No, as of Kubernetes v1.36, the scheduler does not respect the priority or disruptionMode fields of a PodGroup when performing default preemption for a single Pod.
title: Workload-Aware Preemption

instruction: What are the prerequisites for enabling Workload-Aware Preemption in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Ensure the GenericWorkload and GangScheduling feature gates and the scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2 API group are enabled in the cluster, and that Gang Scheduling and the Workload API are available.
title: Workload-Aware Preemption

instruction: What is the purpose of Vertical Pod Autoscaling in Kubernetes?
response: Vertical Pod Autoscaling automatically updates a workload management resource, such as a Deployment or StatefulSet, to adjust infrastructure resource requests and limits to match actual usage.
title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling

instruction: How does Vertical Pod Autoscaling differ from horizontal scaling in Kubernetes?
response: Vertical scaling means assigning more resources to the Pods that are already running for the workload, whereas horizontal scaling means deploying more Pods to distribute the load.
title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling

instruction: What happens if the resource usage decreases and the Pod resource requests are above optimal levels?
response: The VerticalPodAutoscaler instructs the workload resource to adjust resource requests back down, preventing resource waste.
title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling

instruction: What are the main components of a VerticalPodAutoscaler?
response: The VerticalPodAutoscaler consists of three main components: the recommender, the updater, and the admission controller webhook.
title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling

instruction: Is Vertical Pod Autoscaling part of the core Kubernetes API?
response: No, unlike HorizontalPodAutoscaler, VerticalPodAutoscaler is not part of the core Kubernetes API and must be installed separately in your cluster.
title: Vertical Pod Autoscaling

instruction: What are the phases a Pod can be in during its lifecycle?
response: A Pod can be in the following phases: Pending, Running, Succeeded, or Failed.
title: Pod Lifecycle

instruction: What happens to Pods running on a Node if the Node dies?
response: If a Node dies, the Pods running on (or scheduled to run on) that Node are marked for deletion. The control plane marks the Pods for removal after a timeout period.
title: Pod Lifecycle

instruction: Can Pods be scheduled to run on multiple nodes?
response: No, Pods are only scheduled once in their lifetime; assigning a Pod to a specific node is called binding, and the process of selecting which node to use is called scheduling.
title: Pod Lifecycle

instruction: What happens if one of the containers in a Pod fails?
response: If one of the containers in the Pod fails, then Kubernetes may try to restart that specific container.
title: Pod Lifecycle

instruction: How does Kubernetes manage the health of applications running in Pods?
response: The kubelet manages executing probes that track the health of your application. You can also inject custom readiness information into the condition data for a Pod, if that is useful to your application.
title: Pod Lifecycle

instruction: What is Kompose and what is it used for?
response: Kompose is a conversion tool for translating Docker Compose files to container orchestrators such as Kubernetes or OpenShift.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources

instruction: How do I install Kompose on Linux?
response: To install Kompose on Linux, you can download the binary from the GitHub release page using the following command: curl -L https://github.com/kubernetes/kompose/releases/download/v1.34.0/kompose-linux-amd64 -o kompose, then make it executable with chmod +x kompose and move it to /usr/local/bin/kompose.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources

instruction: What is the recommended way to run the tutorial?
response: It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources

instruction: How do I convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources?
response: To convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources, run kompose convert in the directory containing your docker-compose.yml file, then apply the resulting output file with kubectl apply -f <output file>.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources

instruction: What is the command to verify the kubectl version?
response: To check the kubectl version, enter kubectl version.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources

instruction: How do you create a new ClusterIP service named my-cs that exposes port 5678 and targets port 8080?
response: kubectl create service clusterip my-cs --tcp=5678:8080
title: kubectl create service clusterip

instruction: What is the purpose of setting --clusterip to 'None' when creating a ClusterIP service?
response: Setting --clusterip to 'None' creates a 'headless' service, which means no load balancing is performed.
title: kubectl create service clusterip

instruction: What is the effect of using --dry-run=client when creating a ClusterIP service?
response: Using --dry-run=client will only print the object that would be sent, without actually sending it or persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create service clusterip

instruction: How can you specify multiple port pairs when creating a ClusterIP service?
response: You can specify multiple port pairs using the --tcp flag multiple times, e.g., --tcp=8080:80 --tcp=8443:443.
title: kubectl create service clusterip

instruction: What is the default validation mode when creating a ClusterIP service?
response: The default validation mode is 'strict', which uses a schema to validate the input and fails the request if invalid.
title: kubectl create service clusterip

instruction: Where does kubectl look for a configuration file by default?
response: kubectl looks for a file named 'config' in the $HOME/.kube directory.
title: The kubectl command-line tool

instruction: How can you specify a different kubeconfig file for kubectl?
response: You can specify other kubeconfig files by setting the KUBECONFIG environment variable or by setting the --kubeconfig flag.
title: The kubectl command-line tool

instruction: What is the primary role of the kubectl tool in the Kubernetes ecosystem?
response: The kubectl tool is the primary interface for creating, inspecting, updating, and deleting Kubernetes objects. It communicates with the Kubernetes API to perform these actions.
title: The kubectl command-line tool

instruction: How does kubectl authenticate when running outside a cluster?
response: When running outside a cluster, kubectl uses the kubeconfig file to find the API server address and credentials.
title: The kubectl command-line tool

instruction: How can you switch between clusters using kubectl?
response: You can use kubectl to switch between clusters without reconfiguring your environment by running 'kubectl config use-context' to change the active context.
title: The kubectl command-line tool

instruction: What are the read operations allowed by the Node authorizer for kubelets?
response: The Node authorizer allows kubelets to perform read operations on services, endpoints, nodes, pods, secrets, configmaps, persistent volume claims, and persistent volumes related to pods bound to the kubelet's node.
title: Using Node Authorization

instruction: What is the required credential for a kubelet to be authorized by the Node authorizer?
response: The kubelet must use a credential that identifies it as being in the system:nodes group, with a username of system:node:<nodeName>.
title: Using Node Authorization

instruction: How does the kubelet determine its hostname for Node authorizer purposes?
response: The kubelet determines its hostname by default using the host name as provided by hostname, or overridden via the kubelet option --hostname-override. However, when using the --cloud-provider kubelet option, the specific hostname may be determined by the cloud provider, ignoring the local hostname and the --hostname-override option.
title: Using Node Authorization

instruction: How do you enable the Node authorizer in the API server?
response: To enable the Node authorizer, start the API server with the --authorization-config flag set to a file that includes the Node authorizer, or start the API server with the --authorization-mode flag set to a comma-separated list that includes Node.
title: Using Node Authorization

instruction: What admission plugin needs to be enabled to limit a kubelet to modifying its own node and pods bound to itself?
response: The NodeRestriction admission plugin needs to be enabled to limit a kubelet to modifying its own node and pods bound to itself.
title: Using Node Authorization

instruction: What is a VolumeSnapshot in Kubernetes?
response: A VolumeSnapshot in Kubernetes represents a snapshot of a volume on a storage system. It is a request for a snapshot of a volume by a user, similar to a PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Volume Snapshots

instruction: What is the role of the snapshot controller in Kubernetes?
response: The snapshot controller watches VolumeSnapshot and VolumeSnapshotContent objects and is responsible for the creation and deletion of VolumeSnapshotContent objects.
title: Volume Snapshots

instruction: What is the purpose of the csi-snapshotter sidecar helper container?
response: The csi-snapshotter sidecar helper container watches VolumeSnapshotContent objects and triggers CreateSnapshot and DeleteSnapshot operations against a CSI endpoint.
title: Volume Snapshots

instruction: Are VolumeSnapshot, VolumeSnapshotContent, and VolumeSnapshotClass part of the core Kubernetes API?
response: No, VolumeSnapshot, VolumeSnapshotContent, and VolumeSnapshotClass are CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions), not part of the core API.
title: Volume Snapshots

instruction: Is VolumeSnapshot support available for all types of drivers in Kubernetes?
response: No, VolumeSnapshot support is only available for CSI drivers.
title: Volume Snapshots

instruction: What is the purpose of the GroupVersionForDiscovery struct?
response: The GroupVersionForDiscovery struct contains the 'group/version' and 'version' string of a version. It is made a struct to keep extensibility.
title: GroupVersionForDiscovery

instruction: What is the format of the groupVersion field in GroupVersionForDiscovery?
response: The groupVersion field specifies the API group and version in the form 'group/version'.
title: GroupVersionForDiscovery

instruction: What is the purpose of the version field in GroupVersionForDiscovery?
response: The version field specifies the version in the form of 'version'. This is to save the clients the trouble of splitting the GroupVersion.
title: GroupVersionForDiscovery

instruction: What is the purpose of the LocalObjectReference in Kubernetes?
response: LocalObjectReference contains enough information to let you locate the referenced object inside the same namespace.
title: LocalObjectReference

instruction: What is the significance of the 'name' field in LocalObjectReference?
response: The 'name' field is effectively required and represents the name of the referent. It is allowed to be empty due to backwards compatibility, but instances with an empty value are likely incorrect.
title: LocalObjectReference

instruction: Can LocalObjectReference be used to reference objects across different namespaces?
response: No, LocalObjectReference can only be used to locate the referenced object inside the same namespace.
title: LocalObjectReference

instruction: What should I do if I encounter an issue with this page?
response: If you want to report a problem or suggest an improvement, open an issue in the GitHub Repository. If you have a specific, answerable question about how to use Kubernetes, ask it on Stack Overflow.
title: LocalObjectReference

instruction: Is the 'name' field in LocalObjectReference optional?
response: While the 'name' field is allowed to be empty due to backwards compatibility, it is effectively required. Instances with an empty value are almost certainly wrong.
title: LocalObjectReference

instruction: What is the primary design goal of the kubectl top command?
response: The primary design goal of the kubectl top command is to provide a low-overhead signal for autoscalers, not to be a perfectly accurate monitoring tool.
title: kubectl top

instruction: What are some use cases for the kubectl top command?
response: The kubectl top command can be used for on-the-fly spot-checks of resource usage, understanding current resource consumption patterns, and validating the behavior of HPA or VPA configurations.
title: kubectl top

instruction: Why may the metrics shown by kubectl top not match those from standard OS tools like 'top'?
response: The metrics shown by kubectl top are specifically optimized for Kubernetes autoscaling decisions and are designed to provide a stable signal for autoscalers rather than for pinpoint accuracy.
title: kubectl top

instruction: What is required for the kubectl top command to work?
response: The Metrics Server must be installed and running in the cluster for the kubectl top command to work.
title: kubectl top

instruction: What type of solutions should be used for high-accuracy reporting, historical analysis, dashboarding, or alerting instead of kubectl top?
response: A dedicated monitoring solution should be used for high-accuracy reporting, historical analysis, dashboarding, or alerting instead of kubectl top.
title: kubectl top

instruction: What are the prerequisites for running this tutorial on a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster, and a dynamic PersistentVolume provisioner with a default StorageClass or statically provision PersistentVolumes. Additionally, you should be familiar with PersistentVolumes, StatefulSets, Pods, Services, and ConfigMaps.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application

instruction: What is the purpose of the ConfigMap created in the mysql-configmap.yaml file?
response: The ConfigMap provides my.cnf overrides that let you independently control configuration on the primary MySQL server and its replicas. Specifically, it configures the primary server to serve replication logs to replicas and configures replicas to reject any writes.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application

instruction: What is the topology of the MySQL deployment example provided?

instruction: Why is the MySQL configuration not considered production-ready in this example?

instruction: What are the objectives of this tutorial?
response: The objectives are to deploy a replicated MySQL topology with a StatefulSet, send MySQL client traffic, observe resistance to downtime, and scale the StatefulSet up and down.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application

instruction: What does the 'fieldSelector' field in ResourceAttributes do?
response: The 'fieldSelector' field describes the limitation on access based on field. It can only limit access, not broaden it.
title: ResourceAttributes

instruction: How do you specify all resources in the 'resource' field of ResourceAttributes?
response: To specify all resources, you can use '*' in the 'resource' field.
title: ResourceAttributes

instruction: What is the meaning of an empty string in the 'namespace' field of ResourceAttributes?
response: An empty string in the 'namespace' field means 'all' for namespace scoped resources from a SubjectAccessReview or SelfSubjectAccessReview, and there is no distinction between no namespace and all namespaces for LocalSubjectAccessReviews. For cluster-scoped resources, it is empty.
title: ResourceAttributes

instruction: What does the 'verb' field in ResourceAttributes represent?
response: The 'verb' field represents a Kubernetes resource API verb, such as get, list, watch, create, update, delete, proxy. '*' means all verbs.
title: ResourceAttributes

instruction: How do you limit access based on labels in ResourceAttributes?
response: You can limit access based on labels by using the 'labelSelector' field in ResourceAttributes. It can only limit access, not broaden it.
title: ResourceAttributes

instruction: What is the purpose of RuntimeClass in Kubernetes?
response: RuntimeClass defines a class of container runtime supported in the cluster and is used to determine which container runtime is used to run all containers in a pod.
title: Node

instruction: Who is responsible for resolving the RuntimeClassName reference before running a pod?

instruction: Where can I find more details about RuntimeClass?

instruction: What is the purpose of a liveness probe in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of a liveness probe in Kubernetes is to detect and remedy situations where an application running for a long period of time transitions to a broken state and cannot recover except by being restarted.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

instruction: How often does the kubelet perform a liveness probe in the example configuration?
response: The kubelet performs a liveness probe every 5 seconds, as specified by the periodSeconds field in the livenessProbe configuration.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

instruction: What command does the kubelet execute to perform a liveness probe in the example configuration?
response: The kubelet executes the command 'cat /tmp/healthy' to perform a liveness probe in the example configuration.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

instruction: What happens to the container if the liveness probe command returns a non-zero value?
response: If the liveness probe command returns a non-zero value, the kubelet kills the container and restarts it.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

instruction: Why does the example container initially return a success code for the liveness probe?
response: The example container initially returns a success code for the liveness probe because it creates a /tmp/healthy file during the first 30 seconds of its life, and the liveness probe command 'cat /tmp/healthy' succeeds during this time.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

instruction: What is a PersistentVolume in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A PersistentVolume (PV) is a piece of storage in the cluster that has been manually provisioned by an administrator, or dynamically provisioned by Kubernetes using a StorageClass.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes

instruction: What is the purpose of a PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes?
response: A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a request for storage by a user that can be fulfilled by a PersistentVolume. PersistentVolumeClaims are independent from Pod lifecycles and preserve data through restarting, rescheduling, and even deleting Pods.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes

instruction: Why is the deployment of WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes not suitable for production use cases?
response: This deployment is not suitable for production use cases because it uses single instance WordPress and MySQL Pods. For production, consider using the WordPress Helm Chart to deploy WordPress.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes

instruction: How are PersistentVolumes and PersistentVolumeClaims related to Pod lifecycles?
response: PersistentVolumes and PersistentVolumeClaims are independent from Pod lifecycles and preserve data through restarting, rescheduling, and even deleting Pods.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes

instruction: What happens when a PersistentVolumeClaim is created in a cluster with a default StorageClass?
response: When a PersistentVolumeClaim is created, a PersistentVolume is dynamically provisioned based on the StorageClass configuration. If no StorageClass is specified in the PersistentVolumeClaim, the cluster's default StorageClass is used instead.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes

instruction: What is the primary use case for static Pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The main use for static Pods is to run a self-hosted control plane, using the kubelet to supervise the individual control plane components.
title: Static Pods

instruction: How does the kubelet handle a static Pod that fails?
response: The kubelet watches each static Pod and restarts it if it fails.
title: Static Pods

instruction: What is a mirror Pod, and how is it related to a static Pod?
response: A mirror Pod is a Pod that the kubelet automatically tries to create on the Kubernetes API server for each static Pod. The mirror Pod has the same labels as the static Pod, and its name is suffixed with the node hostname with a leading hyphen.
title: Static Pods

instruction: Can you use kubectl to delete a mirror Pod, and what happens if you try?
response: If you try to use kubectl to delete the mirror Pod from the API server, the kubelet does not remove the static Pod. The kubelet will recreate the mirror Pod.
title: Static Pods

instruction: What is a key difference between static Pods and DaemonSets in Kubernetes?
response: Static Pods are not managed by the control plane, so they cannot be rolled out, rolled back, or scaled using standard Kubernetes mechanisms. DaemonSets provide these capabilities and are the recommended approach for running node-level workloads.
title: Static Pods

instruction: What are the three management techniques for Kubernetes objects?
response: The three management techniques for Kubernetes objects are: Imperative commands, Imperative object configuration, and Declarative object configuration.
title: Kubernetes Object Management

instruction: What is the recommended way to get started or to run a one-off task in a cluster?
response: The recommended way to get started or to run a one-off task in a cluster is by using imperative commands.
title: Kubernetes Object Management

instruction: What are the disadvantages of using imperative commands compared to object configuration?
response: The disadvantages of using imperative commands compared to object configuration are: Commands do not integrate with change review processes, Commands do not provide an audit trail associated with changes, Commands do not provide a source of records except for what is live, and Commands do not provide a template for creating new objects.
title: Kubernetes Object Management

instruction: What is the effect of using the imperative replace command on an object's spec?
response: The imperative replace command replaces the existing spec with the newly provided one, dropping all changes to the object missing from the configuration file.
title: Kubernetes Object Management

instruction: Which management technique is recommended for production projects and supports multiple writers?
response: Declarative object configuration is recommended for production projects and supports multiple writers.
title: Kubernetes Object Management

instruction: What are the three main tracks for API versions in Kubernetes, and what do they represent?
response: The three main tracks for API versions in Kubernetes are: GA (generally available, stable) such as v1, Beta (pre-release) such as v1beta1, and Alpha (experimental) such as v1alpha1.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy

instruction: What are the rules that govern the deprecation of elements of the Kubernetes API?
response: The rules that govern the deprecation of elements of the Kubernetes API include: REST resources (aka API objects), fields of REST resources, annotations on REST resources (excluding 'alpha' annotations), enumerated or constant values, and component config structures.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy

instruction: How can API elements be removed from a Kubernetes API group?
response: API elements may only be removed by incrementing the version of the API group. Once an API element has been added to an API group at a particular version, it cannot be removed from that version or have its behavior significantly changed, regardless of track.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy

instruction: What is the requirement for API objects when moving between API versions in a given release?
response: API objects must be able to round-trip between API versions in a given release without information loss, with the exception of whole REST resources that do not exist in some versions. For example, an object can be written as v1 and then read back as v2 and converted to v1, and the resulting v1 resource will be identical to the original.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy

instruction: Are 'alpha' annotations included in the deprecation rules for Kubernetes API elements?
response: No, 'alpha' annotations are not included in the deprecation rules for Kubernetes API elements. Only 'beta' annotations are considered, while 'alpha' annotations are excluded.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle communication between pods?
response: Kubernetes assumes that pods can communicate with other pods, regardless of which host they land on. Every pod has its own cluster-private IP address, allowing containers within a Pod to reach each other's ports on localhost, and all pods in a cluster to see each other without NAT.
title: Connecting Applications with Services

instruction: Can multiple pods with the same container port run on the same node?
response: Yes, you can run multiple pods with the same container port on the same node. The pods are accessed using their assigned IP address, and the container port is not exposed on the node.
title: Connecting Applications with Services

instruction: How do I expose a pod to the cluster?
response: You can expose a pod to the cluster by specifying a container port in the pod's configuration. For example, in the provided nginx example, the container port is set to 80, making it accessible from any node in the cluster.
title: Connecting Applications with Services

instruction: What is the purpose of the containerPort specification in a pod?
response: The containerPort specification in a pod makes it accessible from any node in the cluster. It allows other pods or nodes to communicate with the pod using the assigned IP address and port number.
title: Connecting Applications with Services

instruction: How can I access a pod from another node in the cluster?
response: You can access a pod from another node in the cluster using the pod's assigned IP address and container port. For example, you can ssh into any node in the cluster and use a tool like curl to make queries against the pod's IP address.
title: Connecting Applications with Services

instruction: What is the purpose of a ValidatingWebhookConfiguration in Kubernetes?
response: A ValidatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of an admission webhook that accepts or rejects an object without changing it.
title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration

instruction: What is the API version for ValidatingWebhookConfiguration?
response: The API version for ValidatingWebhookConfiguration is admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1.
title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration

instruction: What is the patch strategy for the webhooks field in ValidatingWebhookConfiguration?
response: The patch strategy for the webhooks field in ValidatingWebhookConfiguration is merge on key.
title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the metadata field in ValidatingWebhookConfiguration?
response: The metadata field in ValidatingWebhookConfiguration is the standard object metadata. For more information, see https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata.
title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration

instruction: What is the type of the items field in ValidatingWebhookConfigurationList?
response: The items field in ValidatingWebhookConfigurationList is an array of ValidatingWebhookConfiguration.
title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration

instruction: How do I create a Kubernetes Secret object to store a username and password for accessing a database?
response: You can create a Secret object by passing raw data in the kubectl command or by storing credentials in files and passing the file paths. For example, to store the username 'admin' and password 'S!B*d$zDsb=', you can use the command: kubectl create secret generic db-user-pass --from-literal=username=admin --from-literal=password='S!B*d$zDsb='
title: Managing Secrets using kubectl

instruction: Why do I need to use single quotes when passing literal values for Secret data in the kubectl command?
response: You need to use single quotes to escape special characters such as $, , *, =, and ! in your strings. If you don't, your shell will interpret these characters.
title: Managing Secrets using kubectl

instruction: How can I store credentials in files to create a Secret object?
response: You can store the credentials in files, for example, echo -n 'admin' > ./username.txt and echo -n 'S!B*d$zDsb=' > ./password.txt. Then, pass the file paths in the kubectl command: kubectl create secret generic db-user-pass --from-file=./username.txt --from-file=./password.txt
title: Managing Secrets using kubectl

instruction: What is the purpose of the -n flag when creating files for Secret data?
response: The -n flag ensures that the generated files do not have an extra newline character at the end of the text. This is important because when kubectl reads a file and encodes the content into a base64 string, the extra newline character gets encoded too.
title: Managing Secrets using kubectl

instruction: How can I verify that a Secret object was created successfully?
response: You can verify that the Secret was created by running the command: kubectl get secrets
title: Managing Secrets using kubectl

instruction: How do I assign memory resources to containers and pods in Kubernetes?
response: You can assign memory resources to containers and pods in Kubernetes by specifying the memory limit and request in the container or pod configuration. This allows you to control the amount of memory allocated to your workloads.
title: Configure Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of configuring Quality of Service (QoS) for pods in Kubernetes?
response: Configuring QoS for pods in Kubernetes allows you to control the priority and resource allocation for your pods. This ensures that critical pods receive the necessary resources to function properly.
title: Configure Pods and Containers

instruction: How do I configure a pod to use a volume for storage in Kubernetes?
response: You can configure a pod to use a volume for storage in Kubernetes by specifying a volume in the pod configuration and mounting it to a container. This allows you to persist data across pod restarts.
title: Configure Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of assigning devices to pods and containers in Kubernetes?
response: Assigning devices to pods and containers in Kubernetes allows you to provide access to specific hardware resources, such as GPUs or NICs, to your workloads. This enables you to run device-intensive applications in your cluster.
title: Configure Pods and Containers

instruction: How do I configure liveness, readiness, and startup probes for a container in Kubernetes?
response: You can configure liveness, readiness, and startup probes for a container in Kubernetes by specifying the probe configuration in the container configuration. Liveness probes check if a container is alive, readiness probes check if a container is ready to handle requests, and startup probes check if a container has started successfully.
title: Configure Pods and Containers

instruction: How do I list all contexts in my kubeconfig file?
response: You can list all contexts in your kubeconfig file by running the command `kubectl config get-contexts`.
title: kubectl config get-contexts

instruction: What is the purpose of the `--no-headers` option when using `kubectl config get-contexts`?
response: The `--no-headers` option is used to prevent printing headers when using the default or custom-column output format.
title: kubectl config get-contexts

instruction: Can I describe a specific context in my kubeconfig file using `kubectl config get-contexts`?
response: Yes, you can describe a specific context in your kubeconfig file by running the command `kubectl config get-contexts my-context`, replacing `my-context` with the name of the context you want to describe.
title: kubectl config get-contexts

instruction: What output format is supported by `kubectl config get-contexts`?
response: The supported output format is `name`.
title: kubectl config get-contexts

instruction: How can I specify a kubeconfig file to use with `kubectl config get-contexts`?
response: You can specify a kubeconfig file to use with `kubectl config get-contexts` by using the `--kubeconfig` option followed by the path to the kubeconfig file.
title: kubectl config get-contexts

instruction: How do you execute a command in a specific container in a pod using kubectl exec?
response: You can use the -c flag followed by the container name to execute a command in a specific container in a pod. For example, to run the 'date' command in the ruby-container from pod mypod, you would use: kubectl exec mypod -c ruby-container -- date
title: kubectl exec

instruction: What is the purpose of the --pod-running-timeout flag in kubectl exec?
response: The --pod-running-timeout flag specifies the length of time to wait until at least one pod is running. The default is 1 minute (1m0s).
title: kubectl exec

instruction: How do you switch to raw terminal mode and send stdin to a container using kubectl exec?
response: You can use the -i and -t flags to switch to raw terminal mode and send stdin to a container. For example, to send stdin to 'bash' in ruby-container from pod mypod and send stdout/stderr from 'bash' back to the client, you would use: kubectl exec mypod -c ruby-container -i -t -- bash -il
title: kubectl exec

instruction: Can you use kubectl exec to execute a command in a pod from a deployment or service?
response: Yes, you can use kubectl exec to execute a command in a pod from a deployment or service. For example, to get output from running the 'date' command from the first pod of the deployment mydeployment, you would use: kubectl exec deploy/mydeployment -- date. Similarly, you can use kubectl exec svc/myservice -- date to execute a command in a pod from the service myservice.
title: kubectl exec

instruction: What is the default behavior if you omit the -c flag when using kubectl exec?
response: If you omit the -c flag, kubectl exec will use the kubectl.kubernetes.io/default-container annotation to select the container to be attached. If no annotation is present, it will choose the first container in the pod by default.
title: kubectl exec

instruction: How are Service ClusterIPs allocated in Kubernetes?
response: Service ClusterIPs are allocated either dynamically, where the cluster's control plane automatically picks a free IP address from within the configured IP range for type: ClusterIP Services, or statically, where you specify an IP address of your choice from within the configured IP range.
title: Service ClusterIP allocation

instruction: Why must every Service ClusterIP be unique across the cluster?
response: Every Service ClusterIP must be unique across the cluster because clients connect to the Service using that virtual IP address, and Kubernetes load-balances traffic to the Service across the different backing Pods.
title: Service ClusterIP allocation

instruction: Why is it necessary to reserve Service Cluster IPs?
response: Reserving Service Cluster IPs is necessary when you want Services to run on well-known IP addresses that other components and users in the cluster can use. For example, the DNS Service for the cluster often uses a specific IP address.
title: Service ClusterIP allocation

instruction: What happens if you try to create a Service with a specific ClusterIP that has already been allocated?
response: Trying to create a Service with a specific ClusterIP that has already been allocated will return an error.
title: Service ClusterIP allocation

instruction: How does Kubernetes divide the ClusterIP range to reduce the risk of collision?
response: The ClusterIP range is divided based on the formula min(max(16, cidrSize / 16), 256), which results in a number between 16 and 256. This division helps reduce the risk of collision when dynamically assigning IP addresses.
title: Service ClusterIP allocation

instruction: What is the smallest deployable unit of computing that can be created and managed in Kubernetes?
response: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit of computing that can be created and managed in Kubernetes.
title: Pods

instruction: What is a Pod composed of?
response: A Pod is a group of one or more containers, with shared storage and network resources, and a specification for how to run the containers.
title: Pods

instruction: Can a Pod contain multiple containers?
response: Yes, a Pod can contain one or more application containers, and also init containers that run during Pod startup, as well as ephemeral containers for debugging a running Pod.
title: Pods

instruction: What are the two main use cases for Pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Pods are used in two main ways: Pods that run a single container, and Pods that run multiple containers that need to work together.
title: Pods

instruction: Is it necessary to run multiple containers in a Pod to provide replication?
response: No, you don't need to run multiple containers to provide replication; if you need multiple replicas, you should use Workload management.
title: Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of checkpointing a container in Kubernetes?
response: Checkpointing a container creates a stateful copy of a running container, allowing it to be moved to a different computer for debugging or similar purposes, and enabling the restored container to continue running at the same point it was checkpointed.
title: Kubelet Checkpoint API

instruction: Where does the kubelet store the checkpoint archive?
response: The kubelet stores the checkpoint archive in the 'checkpoints' directory below its root directory, which is defined by the '--root-dir' flag and defaults to '/var/lib/kubelet/checkpoints'.
title: Kubelet Checkpoint API

instruction: What is the format of the checkpoint archive created by the kubelet?
response: The checkpoint archive is in tar format, and its contents depend on the underlying CRI implementation (the container runtime on that node).
title: Kubelet Checkpoint API

instruction: How is access to the kubelet checkpoint interface controlled?
response: Access to the kubelet checkpoint interface is controlled by Kubelet authentication and authorization, as described in the Kubelet authentication/authorization reference.
title: Kubelet Checkpoint API

instruction: What is the default timeout value for checkpoint creation if no timeout is specified?
response: If zero or no timeout is specified, the default CRI timeout value will be used for checkpoint creation.
title: Kubelet Checkpoint API

instruction: How do I run an interactive busybox Pod to test connectivity in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can run an interactive busybox Pod using the command: kubectl run -it --rm --restart=Never busybox --image=registry.k8s.io/busybox:1.27.2 sh
title: Debug Services

instruction: How do I run a command in an existing Pod in my Kubernetes cluster?

instruction: What is the deployment and scaling command used to create and scale a Deployment in the example?
response: The commands used are: kubectl create deployment hostnames --image=registry.k8s.io/serve_hostname and kubectl scale deployment hostnames --replicas=3
title: Debug Services

instruction: How do I confirm that my Pods are running and get their IP addresses?
response: You can confirm that your Pods are running using the command: kubectl get pods -l app=hostnames. To get the IP addresses of the Pods, use the command: kubectl get pods -l app=hostnames -o go-template='{{range .items}}{{.status.podIP}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'
title: Debug Services

instruction: What is the label automatically set by kubectl create deployment to the name of the Deployment?
response: The label "app" is automatically set by kubectl create deployment to the name of the Deployment.
title: Debug Services

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl auth reconcile?
response: kubectl auth reconcile reconciles rules for RBAC role, role binding, cluster role, and cluster role binding objects. It creates missing objects, updates existing roles to include permissions, and removes extra permissions or subjects if specified.
title: kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: How do you reconcile RBAC resources from a file using kubectl auth reconcile?
response: You can reconcile RBAC resources from a file using kubectl auth reconcile by running the command kubectl auth reconcile -f FILENAME, replacing FILENAME with the path to your file, for example: kubectl auth reconcile -f my-rbac-rules.yaml.
title: kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: What is the effect of the --remove-extra-permissions flag in kubectl auth reconcile?
response: The --remove-extra-permissions flag removes extra permissions added to roles when used with kubectl auth reconcile.
title: kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: Can kubectl auth reconcile be used with directories and URLs?
response: Yes, kubectl auth reconcile can be used with directories and URLs. You can specify a directory or URL to files identifying the resource to reconcile using the -f or --filename option. The -R or --recursive flag can be used to process the directory recursively.
title: kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: What is the difference between kubectl auth reconcile and kubectl apply for RBAC resources?
response: kubectl auth reconcile is preferred over kubectl apply for RBAC resources because it performs semantically-aware merging of rules and subjects, ensuring accurate reconciliation of permissions and bindings.
title: kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: What is the unique identifier for a device in a ResourceSlice?
response: The unique identifier for a device is the tuple <driver name>, <pool name>, <device name>.
title: ResourceSlice

instruction: How does a driver update a pool of resources in ResourceSlice?
response: Whenever a driver needs to update a pool, it increments the pool.Spec.Pool.Generation number and updates all ResourceSlices with that new number and new resource definitions.
title: ResourceSlice

instruction: What should a consumer do when it detects multiple ResourceSlices for the same pool?
response: A consumer should check the number of ResourceSlices in a pool to determine whether its view of a pool is complete and if not, should wait until the driver has completed updating the pool.
title: ResourceSlice

instruction: How does a consumer determine which ResourceSlices to use?
response: A consumer must only use ResourceSlices with the highest generation number and ignore all others.
title: ResourceSlice

instruction: What type of resources are currently supported by ResourceSlice?
response: At the moment, the only supported resources are devices with attributes and capacities.
title: ResourceSlice

instruction: What is the feature state of Manifest-Based Admission Control in Kubernetes v1.36?
response: The feature is in alpha state and disabled by default in Kubernetes v1.36.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control

instruction: How do you enable Manifest-Based Admission Control?
response: To use the feature, enable the ManifestBasedAdmissionControlConfig feature gate and configure the staticManifestsDir field in the AdmissionConfiguration file passed to the kube-apiserver via --admission-control-config-file.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control

instruction: What are the limitations of admission policies and webhooks registered through the Kubernetes API?
response: The limitations include a bootstrap gap where policies are not enforced until API objects are created and loaded, a self-protection gap where admission configuration resources are not subject to webhook admission, and an etcd dependency where REST-based admission configurations depend on etcd availability.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control

instruction: What are the benefits of using manifest-based admission control over REST-based admission configurations?
response: Manifest-based admission control addresses the limitations of REST-based admission configurations by loading configurations from files on disk, making them active as soon as the API server is ready, not visible or changeable through the Kubernetes API, and independent of etcd availability.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control

instruction: What resource types are supported in manifest files for manifest-based admission control?
response: The supported resource types include ValidatingWebhookConfiguration, MutatingWebhookConfiguration, ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding, MutatingAdmissionPolicy, and MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding, all using the admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1 API version.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control

instruction: What are the required PKI certificates for a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Kubernetes requires PKI certificates for authentication over TLS. The cluster requires server certificates for the API server endpoint, etcd server, each kubelet, and optional server certificate for the front-proxy. Additionally, client certificates are required for each kubelet, API server, controller manager, scheduler, kube-proxy, and optional client certificates for administrators and front-proxy.
title: PKI certificates and requirements

instruction: Where are certificates stored when installing Kubernetes with kubeadm?
response: When installing Kubernetes with kubeadm, most certificates are stored in /etc/kubernetes/pki.
title: PKI certificates and requirements

instruction: What are the two approaches for certificate usage between the API Server and kubelet?
response: There are two approaches for certificate usage: Shared Certificates, where the kube-apiserver uses the same certificate and key pair to authenticate its clients, and Separate Certificates, where the kube-apiserver generates a new client certificate and key pair to authenticate its communication with the kubelet servers.
title: PKI certificates and requirements

instruction: Do I need to generate certificates if I install Kubernetes with kubeadm?
response: No, if you install Kubernetes with kubeadm, the certificates that your cluster requires are automatically generated.
title: PKI certificates and requirements

instruction: Are front-proxy certificates always required in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: No, front-proxy certificates are required only if you run kube-proxy to support an extension API server.
title: PKI certificates and requirements

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl cordon command?
response: The kubectl cordon command marks a node as unschedulable.
title: kubectl cordon

instruction: How do you use kubectl cordon to mark a node named 'foo' as unschedulable?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl cordon foo' to mark the node 'foo' as unschedulable.
title: kubectl cordon

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run option with kubectl cordon?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to specify a dry run strategy. It can be set to 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If set to 'client', it prints the object that would be sent without sending it. If set to 'server', it submits a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl cordon

instruction: Can you use a label selector with kubectl cordon to filter nodes?
response: Yes, you can use the -l or --selector option with kubectl cordon to filter nodes based on label constraints. For example, 'kubectl cordon -l key1=value1,key2=value2'.
title: kubectl cordon

instruction: What happens to new pods when a node is cordoned?
response: The text does not explicitly state what happens to new pods when a node is cordoned, but based on the context of cordoning a node, it implies that new pods will not be scheduled on that node.
title: kubectl cordon

instruction: How can I update Kubernetes API objects in place?
response: You can use kubectl patch to update Kubernetes API objects in place. This allows you to do a strategic merge patch or a JSON merge patch.
title: Manage Kubernetes Objects

instruction: What are the two paradigms for interacting with the Kubernetes API?
response: The two paradigms for interacting with the Kubernetes API are declarative and imperative.
title: Manage Kubernetes Objects

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl patch?
response: The purpose of kubectl patch is to update Kubernetes API objects in place, supporting both strategic merge patch and JSON merge patch.
title: Manage Kubernetes Objects

instruction: How can I manage Kubernetes objects?
response: You can manage Kubernetes objects declaratively using configuration files or Kustomize, or imperatively using commands or configuration files.
title: Manage Kubernetes Objects

instruction: What is the tool used for migrating Kubernetes objects?
response: The tool used for migrating Kubernetes objects is Storage Version Migration.
title: Manage Kubernetes Objects

instruction: How do I roll back to the previous deployment using kubectl rollout undo?
response: You can roll back to the previous deployment by running the command 'kubectl rollout undo deployment/abc'. This will undo the most recent rollout.
title: kubectl rollout undo

instruction: Can I roll back to a specific revision of a daemonset using kubectl rollout undo?
response: Yes, you can roll back to a specific revision of a daemonset by specifying the --to-revision flag. For example, 'kubectl rollout undo daemonset/abc --to-revision=3' will roll back to revision 3.
title: kubectl rollout undo

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run option in kubectl rollout undo?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to see what changes would be made without actually applying them. You can use it with 'server' or 'client' strategy. For example, 'kubectl rollout undo --dry-run=server deployment/abc' will show you the changes that would be made to the deployment.
title: kubectl rollout undo

instruction: How do I specify a file to identify the resource to roll back using kubectl rollout undo?
response: You can specify a file to identify the resource by using the -f or --filename flag. For example, 'kubectl rollout undo -f filename.yaml deployment/abc' will roll back the deployment specified in the file.
title: kubectl rollout undo

instruction: What is the default revision that kubectl rollout undo rolls back to?
response: The default revision that kubectl rollout undo rolls back to is 0, which is the last revision. You can specify a different revision using the --to-revision flag.
title: kubectl rollout undo

instruction: What is the purpose of using a user namespace with a pod in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of using a user namespace with a pod in Kubernetes is to isolate the user running inside the container from the one in the host, reducing the damage a compromised container can do to the host or other pods in the same node.
title: Use a User Namespace With a Pod

instruction: What are the requirements for a node to support user namespaces with pods?
response: The node OS needs to be Linux, and the cluster must include at least one node that meets the requirements for using user namespaces with Pods.
title: Use a User Namespace With a Pod

instruction: How do you enable a user namespace for a pod?
response: A user namespace for a pod is enabled by setting the hostUsers field of .spec to false in the pod configuration.
title: Use a User Namespace With a Pod

instruction: What is the effect of running a process as root in a container when user namespaces are used?
response: A process running as root in a container can run as a different (non-root) user in the host; in other words, the process has full privileges for operations inside the user namespace, but is unprivileged for operations outside the namespace.
title: Use a User Namespace With a Pod

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes version required to use user namespaces with pods?
response: Your Kubernetes server must be at or later than version v1.25 to use user namespaces with pods.
title: Use a User Namespace With a Pod

response: Kubeadm is used for bootstrapping clusters.

instruction: Can kubeadm be used to create highly available clusters?
response: Yes, kubeadm can be used to create highly available clusters. There are specific instructions for creating highly available clusters with kubeadm.

response: Components can be customized using the kubeadm API.

response: Each kubelet in a cluster can be configured using kubeadm.

response: Run the command 'kubectl get nodes' and verify that all expected nodes are present and in the 'Ready' state.

instruction: What command can I use to get detailed information about the overall health of my cluster?
response: Run the command 'kubectl cluster-info dump' to get detailed information about the overall health of your cluster.

instruction: What happens to pods running on a node that becomes NotReady?
response: If a node becomes NotReady, the pods running on it are evicted after five minutes of NotReady status.

instruction: How can I identify if a node is down or unreachable?

instruction: What is the purpose of Webhook mode in Kubernetes?
response: Webhook mode causes Kubernetes to query an outside REST service when determining user privileges.
title: Webhook Mode

instruction: What is the configuration file format used by Webhook mode?
response: The configuration file uses the kubeconfig file format.
title: Webhook Mode

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'clusters' section in the Webhook configuration file?
response: In the configuration file, 'clusters' refers to the remote service.
title: Webhook Mode

instruction: What information is sent in the request payload when the API Server queries the Webhook service?
response: The API Server POSTs a JSON-serialized authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1 SubjectAccessReview object describing the action, including fields about the user and the resource being accessed.
title: Webhook Mode

instruction: How does the API Server enable the authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1 API extensions group?
response: The API Server must be started with the --runtime-config=authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1=true flag to enable the authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1 API extensions group.
title: Webhook Mode

instruction: Can I use Windows as a worker node in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Yes, it is possible to join a Windows server as a worker node to a Kubernetes cluster. Kubernetes supports worker nodes running either Linux or Microsoft Windows.
title: Windows in Kubernetes

instruction: Can I install and set up kubectl on Windows?
response: Yes, you can install and set up kubectl on Windows, regardless of the operating system used within your cluster.

instruction: What resources are available for configuring and managing Windows nodes in Kubernetes?
response: Several resources are available, including Networking On Windows, Windows Storage In Kubernetes, Resource Management for Windows Nodes, Configure RunAsUserName for Windows Pods and Containers, Create A Windows HostProcess Pod, Configure Group Managed Service Accounts for Windows Pods and Containers, Security For Windows Nodes, and Windows Debugging Tips.

response: You can ask specific, answerable questions about how to use Kubernetes on Stack Overflow. For reporting problems or suggesting improvements, open an issue in the GitHub Repository.

response: No, the Kubernetes project authors aren't responsible for those third-party products or projects. See the CNCF website guidelines for more details.

instruction: What happens to a Container's file system when it terminates and restarts?
response: A Container's file system lives only as long as the Container does. So when a Container terminates and restarts, filesystem changes are lost.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: Why is it important to use a Volume for stateful applications?
response: For more consistent storage that is independent of the Container, you can use a Volume. This is especially important for stateful applications, such as key-value stores (such as Redis) and databases.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: How do you create a Pod that uses a Volume of type emptyDir?
response: You can create a Pod that uses a Volume of type emptyDir by specifying the Volume in the Pod's configuration file. For example, you can use the configuration file pods/storage/redis.yaml, which creates a Pod with a Volume of type emptyDir that lasts for the life of the Pod.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: What command can you use to verify that the Pod's Container is running and watch for changes to the Pod?
response: You can use the command kubectl get pod redis --watch to verify that the Pod's Container is running and watch for changes to the Pod.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: How do you verify that data persists in a Volume even after a Container terminates?
response: You can verify that data persists in a Volume even after a Container terminates by creating a file in the Volume, killing the Container, and then checking if the file still exists when the Container restarts. For example, you can create a file in the /data/redis directory, kill the Redis process, and then check if the file still exists.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: What is the recommended version compatibility between the kubectl client and the cluster control plane?
response: The kubectl client version should be within one minor version difference of the cluster control plane. For example, a v1.36 client can communicate with v1.35, v1.36, and v1.37 control planes.
title: Install and Set Up kubectl on Windows

instruction: How can I install the kubectl binary on Windows using curl?
response: You can install the kubectl binary on Windows using curl with the following command: curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/v1.36.0/bin/windows/amd64/kubectl.exe". Make sure to replace the version number with the latest stable version.
title: Install and Set Up kubectl on Windows

instruction: How can I validate the kubectl binary against the checksum file using PowerShell?
response: To validate the kubectl binary against the checksum file using PowerShell, use the following command: $(Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 .\kubectl.exe).Hash -eq $(Get-Content .\kubectl.exe.sha256)
title: Install and Set Up kubectl on Windows

instruction: What should I do if I have installed Docker Desktop before and it has added its own version of kubectl to PATH?
response: If you have installed Docker Desktop before, you may need to place your PATH entry before the one added by the Docker Desktop installer or remove the Docker Desktop's kubectl.
title: Install and Set Up kubectl on Windows

instruction: How can I test the version of kubectl after installation?
response: You can test the version of kubectl by running the command "kubectl version --client" or "kubectl version --client --output=yaml" for a detailed view of the version.
title: Install and Set Up kubectl on Windows

instruction: What is the purpose of GroupResource in Kubernetes?
response: GroupResource specifies a Group and a Resource, but does not force a version. This is useful for identifying concepts during lookup stages without having partially valid types.
title: GroupResource

instruction: What are the fields in the GroupResource object?
response: The GroupResource object has two fields: 'group' and 'resource', both of which are strings.
title: GroupResource

instruction: Is the 'group' field in GroupResource optional?
response: No, the 'group' field in GroupResource is required, denoted by the '*' symbol.
title: GroupResource

instruction: Can GroupResource be used to specify a version?
response: No, GroupResource does not force a version.
title: GroupResource

instruction: Where can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for the Kubernetes documentation?
