response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: GroupResource

instruction: How does kubectl config determine which kubeconfig file to load?
response: The loading order follows these rules: If the --kubeconfig flag is set, then only that file is loaded. If $KUBECONFIG environment variable is set, then it is used as a list of paths. Otherwise, ${HOME}/.kube/config is used and no merging takes place.
title: kubectl config

instruction: What happens when a value is modified using kubectl config?
response: When a value is modified, it is modified in the file that defines the stanza. When a value is created, it is created in the first file that exists. If no files in the chain exist, then it creates the last file in the list.
title: kubectl config

instruction: Can the --kubeconfig flag be set multiple times?
response: No, the --kubeconfig flag may only be set once and no merging takes place.
title: kubectl config

instruction: What is the purpose of the $KUBECONFIG environment variable in kubectl config?
response: The $KUBECONFIG environment variable is used as a list of paths (normal path delimiting rules for your system). These paths are merged.
title: kubectl config

instruction: Where is the default kubeconfig file located?
response: ${HOME}/.kube/config
title: kubectl config

instruction: What is the purpose of z-pages in Kubernetes?
response: z-pages provide runtime diagnostics for Kubernetes components, offering insights into component runtime status and configuration flags.
title: Kubernetes z-pages

instruction: How do you enable the /statusz endpoint for a Kubernetes component?
response: The /statusz endpoint is enabled using the ComponentStatusz feature gate.
title: Kubernetes z-pages

instruction: What is the difference between the plain text and structured responses of the /statusz endpoint?
response: The plain text response is for debugging purposes only and is not machine-parseable. The structured response, available with a specific Accept header, provides a versioned JSON format with detailed information about the component.
title: Kubernetes z-pages

instruction: What Accept header should be used to request the structured response from the /statusz endpoint?
response: The Accept header should be set to 'application/json;v=v1beta1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Statusz' to request the structured response.
title: Kubernetes z-pages

instruction: What information is included in the plain text response of the /statusz endpoint?
response: The plain text response includes information such as the component's Kubernetes version, emulation version, start time, uptime, Go version, binary version, and available paths.
title: Kubernetes z-pages

instruction: How do you use kubectl describe to get details of a pod named 'nginx'?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl describe pods/nginx' to get details of a pod named 'nginx'.
title: kubectl describe

instruction: What is the purpose of the --all-namespaces option in kubectl describe?
response: The --all-namespaces option allows you to list the requested object(s) across all namespaces, ignoring the namespace in the current context even if specified with --namespace.
title: kubectl describe

instruction: How can you describe all pods managed by a replication controller named 'frontend'?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl describe pods frontend' to describe all pods managed by the 'frontend' replication controller.
title: kubectl describe

instruction: What is the effect of the --show-events option in kubectl describe?
response: The --show-events option determines whether to display events related to the described object. It defaults to true for a single object, and false for multiple objects and prefix matching.
title: kubectl describe

instruction: How do you describe a node named 'kubernetes-node-emt8.c.myproject.internal'?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl describe nodes kubernetes-node-emt8.c.myproject.internal' to describe a node named 'kubernetes-node-emt8.c.myproject.internal'.
title: kubectl describe

instruction: What is the purpose of creating an ExternalName service in Kubernetes?
response: An ExternalName service references to an external DNS address instead of only pods, allowing application authors to reference services that exist off platform, on other clusters, or locally.
title: kubectl create service externalname

instruction: How do you create a new ExternalName service named 'my-service' that points to 'example.com'?
response: You can create a new ExternalName service named 'my-service' that points to 'example.com' by running the command: kubectl create service externalname my-service --external-name example.com
title: kubectl create service externalname

instruction: What is the use of the --dry-run option when creating an ExternalName service?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to test the creation of an ExternalName service without actually creating it. You can specify 'client', 'server', or 'none' as the value. If 'client', only prints the object that would be sent. If 'server', submits a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create service externalname

instruction: Can you explain the --external-name option when creating an ExternalName service?
response: The --external-name option specifies the external name of the service, which is the DNS name of the external service you want to reference.
title: kubectl create service externalname

instruction: What output formats are supported by the kubectl create service externalname command?
response: The supported output formats are: json, yaml, kyaml, name, go-template, go-template-file, template, templatefile, jsonpath, jsonpath-as-json, and jsonpath-file.
title: kubectl create service externalname

instruction: What is the file format for specifying policies in ABAC mode?
response: The file format is one JSON object per line. There should be no enclosing list or map, only one map per line.
title: Using ABAC Authorization

instruction: How do I enable ABAC mode and specify a policy file?
response: To enable ABAC mode, specify --authorization-policy-file=SOME_FILENAME and --authorization-mode=ABAC on startup.
title: Using ABAC Authorization

instruction: What is the effect of an unset property in a policy object?
response: An unset property is the same as a property set to the zero value for its type (e.g. empty string, 0, false).
title: Using ABAC Authorization

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'readonly' property in a policy object?
response: When true, the 'readonly' property means that the Resource-matching policy only applies to get, list, and watch operations, and Non-resource-matching policy only applies to get operation.
title: Using ABAC Authorization

instruction: What special values can be used for the 'user' property in a policy object?
response: The special values are 'system:authenticated' which matches all authenticated requests, and 'system:unauthenticated' which matches all unauthenticated requests.
title: Using ABAC Authorization

instruction: What are the three policy levels defined in the Pod Security Standards?
response: The three policy levels defined in the Pod Security Standards are Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted.
title: Pod Security Standards

instruction: What type of workloads is the Privileged policy typically aimed at?
response: The Privileged policy is typically aimed at system- and infrastructure-level workloads managed by privileged, trusted users.
title: Pod Security Standards

instruction: What is the main goal of the Baseline policy?
response: The main goal of the Baseline policy is to provide ease of adoption for common containerized workloads while preventing known privilege escalations.
title: Pod Security Standards

instruction: What is disallowed in the Baseline policy regarding HostProcess containers on Windows Pods?
response: Privileged access to the host via HostProcess containers is disallowed in the Baseline policy. Specifically, the fields spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess, spec.containers[*].securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess, spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess, and spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess are restricted and can only have values of undefined/nil or false.
title: Pod Security Standards

instruction: What is the purpose of the Restricted policy in Pod Security Standards?
response: The Restricted policy is a heavily restricted policy that follows current Pod hardening best practices.
title: Pod Security Standards

instruction: What is the default format for log messages in Kubelet Configuration?
response: The default format for log messages is 'text'.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'verbosity' field in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The 'verbosity' field determines which log messages are logged. It is a threshold that enables additional messages. Error messages are always logged. The default is zero, which logs only the most important messages.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'options' field in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The 'options' field holds additional parameters specific to different logging formats. Only the options for the selected format get used, but all of them get validated. This field is only available when the LoggingAlphaOptions feature gate is enabled.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: Can the verbosity threshold be overridden for individual files?
response: Yes, the verbosity threshold can be overridden for individual files using the 'vmodule' field in LoggingConfiguration. However, this is only supported for the 'text' log format.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'flushFrequency' field in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The 'flushFrequency' field specifies the maximum time between log flushes. It can be provided as a string parsed as a duration (e.g., '1s') or as an integer representing the maximum number of nanoseconds. This field is ignored if the selected logging backend writes log messages without buffering.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: How do I start debugging a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: The first step in debugging a Pod is to check its current state and recent events with the command 'kubectl describe pods <POD_NAME>'.
title: Debug Pods

instruction: Why is my Pod stuck in the Pending state?
response: A Pod stuck in the Pending state means it cannot be scheduled onto a node, usually due to insufficient resources. Check the output of 'kubectl describe ...' for messages from the scheduler about why it cannot schedule your Pod.
title: Debug Pods

instruction: What are common causes of a Pod being stuck in the Waiting state?
response: The most common cause of Waiting pods is a failure to pull the image. Check that the image name is correct, that the image has been pushed to the registry, and try manually pulling the image.
title: Debug Pods

instruction: How can I troubleshoot a Pod that is stuck in the Terminating state?
response: A Pod stuck in the Terminating state means a deletion has been issued for the Pod, but the control plane is unable to delete the Pod object. This typically happens if there are still resources or dependencies holding onto the Pod.
title: Debug Pods

instruction: What should I do if my Pod is using a hostPort and is stuck in the Pending state?
response: When using hostPort, there are limited places that a Pod can be scheduled. Try using a Service object to expose your Pod instead, or add new nodes to your cluster if necessary.
title: Debug Pods

instruction: How do I change the access mode of a PersistentVolume to ReadWriteOncePod?
response: You can change the access mode of a PersistentVolume to ReadWriteOncePod by updating the PersistentVolume's configuration.
title: Administer a Cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of the Cloud Controller Manager in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The Cloud Controller Manager is responsible for managing cloud-specific resources and integrating with cloud providers.
title: Administer a Cluster

instruction: How can I configure a kubelet image credential provider?
response: You can configure a kubelet image credential provider by specifying the provider in the kubelet configuration file.
title: Administer a Cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of Topology Management Policies in a Kubernetes node?
response: Topology Management Policies are used to control the allocation of resources on a node, taking into account the node's topology.
title: Administer a Cluster

instruction: How do I safely drain a node in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can safely drain a node in a Kubernetes cluster by using the drain command, which evicts pods from the node and reschedules them on other nodes.
title: Administer a Cluster

instruction: How can I access the logs of a running Pod named 'counter'?
response: You can use the kubectl logs command, as follows: kubectl logs counter
title: Logging Architecture

instruction: What happens to logs when a container crashes, a pod gets evicted, or a node dies?
response: In a cluster, logs should have a separate storage and lifecycle independent of nodes, pods, or containers. This concept is called cluster-level logging.
title: Logging Architecture

instruction: How can I retrieve logs from a previous instantiation of a container?
response: You can use kubectl logs --previous to retrieve logs from a previous instantiation of a container.
title: Logging Architecture

instruction: Can I access logs from a specific container in a Pod with multiple containers?
response: Yes, you can specify which container's logs you want to access by appending a container name to the command, with a -c flag, like so: kubectl logs counter -c count
title: Logging Architecture

instruction: Does Kubernetes provide a native storage solution for log data?
response: No, Kubernetes does not provide a native storage solution for log data. Instead, there are many logging solutions that integrate with Kubernetes.
title: Logging Architecture

instruction: What is StorageVersionMigration in Kubernetes?
response: StorageVersionMigration represents a migration of stored data to the latest storage version.
title: Storagemigration

instruction: Where should I ask specific, answerable questions about using Kubernetes?
response: You should ask specific, answerable questions about how to use Kubernetes on Stack Overflow.
title: Storagemigration

instruction: How can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for the Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Storagemigration

instruction: What should I mention when reporting an issue with an auto-generated page in the Kubernetes documentation?
response: When reporting an issue with an auto-generated page, you should mention that the page is auto-generated in your issue description.
title: Storagemigration

instruction: When was the Storagemigration page last modified?
response: The Storagemigration page was last modified on May 13, 2026 at 6:59 PM PST.
title: Storagemigration

instruction: How can I access node metrics data in Kubernetes?
response: You can access node metrics data by sending a proxied request to the stats summary API via the Kubernetes API server. For example, you can use the command 'kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/minikube/proxy/stats/summary' or use curl with 'kubectl proxy'.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What is the default source of node summary metrics data in Kubernetes?
response: By default, Kubernetes fetches node summary metrics data using an embedded cAdvisor that runs within the kubelet.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: How does the kubelet gather metric statistics?
response: The kubelet gathers metric statistics at the node, volume, pod and container level, and emits this information in the Summary API.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What are the requirements for Pressure Stall Information (PSI) in Kubernetes?
response: Pressure Stall Information requires Linux kernel versions 4.20 or later and cgroup v2.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: Can I disable the KubeletPSI feature gate in Kubernetes v1.36 or later?
response: No, starting with Kubernetes v1.36, the KubeletPSI feature gate is locked to true and cannot be disabled.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What are the minimum daemons required to be installed on a node to run the node conformance test?
response: A node must have CRI-compatible container runtimes such as Docker, containerd and CRI-O, and kubelet installed.
title: Validate node setup

instruction: How do you run the node conformance test?
response: To run the node conformance test, use the command: sudo docker run -it --rm --privileged --net=host -v /:/rootfs -v $CONFIG_DIR:$CONFIG_DIR -v $LOG_DIR:/var/result registry.k8s.io/node-test:0.2. The $CONFIG_DIR is the pod manifest path of your kubelet and $LOG_DIR is the test output path.
title: Validate node setup

instruction: What is the URL of the API server to use when running the node conformance test?
response: Use http://localhost:8080 as the URL of the API server.
title: Validate node setup

instruction: How can you run a specific test, such as the MirrorPod test?
response: To run a specific test, overwrite the environment variable FOCUS with the regular expression of tests you want to run. For example, to run the MirrorPod test: sudo docker run -it --rm --privileged --net=host -v /:/rootfs:ro -v $CONFIG_DIR:$CONFIG_DIR -v $LOG_DIR:/var/result -e FOCUS=MirrorPod registry.k8s.io/node-test:0.2.
title: Validate node setup

instruction: Are there node conformance test docker images available for architectures other than amd64?
response: Yes, Kubernetes provides node conformance test docker images for other architectures: arm (node-test-arm), arm64 (node-test-arm64).
title: Validate node setup

instruction: What is the recommended approach for configuring ServiceAccounts for workloads in Kubernetes?
response: Avoid using the default ServiceAccount. Instead, create ServiceAccounts for each workload or microservice. Additionally, set automountServiceAccountToken to false unless the pod specifically requires access to the Kubernetes API to operate.
title: Application Security Checklist

instruction: What security principle should be followed when designing applications for Kubernetes?
response: Follow the right security principles when designing applications. This is a general guideline to ensure application security.
title: Application Security Checklist

instruction: What is recommended for configuring pod-level securityContext in Kubernetes?
response: Set runAsNonRoot: true. Configure the container to execute as a less privileged user (for example, using runAsUser and runAsGroup), and configure appropriate permissions on files or directories inside the container image. Optionally add a supplementary group with fsGroup to access persistent volumes.
title: Application Security Checklist

instruction: What should be considered when deploying an application to a namespace in Kubernetes?
response: The application should deploy into a namespace that enforces an appropriate Pod security standard. If you cannot control this enforcement for the cluster(s) where the application is deployed, take this into account either through documentation or additional defense in depth.
title: Application Security Checklist

instruction: Are checklists sufficient on their own for attaining a good security posture in Kubernetes?
response: No, checklists are not sufficient for attaining a good security posture on their own. A good security posture requires constant attention and improvement, but a checklist can be the first step on the never-ending journey towards security preparedness.
title: Application Security Checklist

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm join phase command?
response: The kubeadm join phase command enables you to invoke atomic steps of the join process, allowing you to let kubeadm do some of the work and fill in the gaps if you wish to apply customization.
title: kubeadm join phase

instruction: What is the purpose of the preflight phase in kubeadm join phase?
response: The preflight phase is used to execute preflight checks on a joining node, ensuring that the node is ready to join the cluster.
title: kubeadm join phase

instruction: How do you run join pre-flight checks using a config file with kubeadm join phase preflight?
response: You can run join pre-flight checks using a config file by executing the command 'kubeadm join phase preflight --config kubeadm-config.yaml'.
title: kubeadm join phase

instruction: What is the significance of the --control-plane option in kubeadm join phase preflight?
response: The --control-plane option is used to create a new control plane instance on the node that is joining the cluster.
title: kubeadm join phase

instruction: What is the effect of the --dry-run option in kubeadm join phase preflight?
response: The --dry-run option doesn't apply any changes; it only outputs what would be done, allowing you to test the command without making any actual changes.
title: kubeadm join phase

instruction: What is the purpose of a ResourceClaimTemplate in Kubernetes?
response: A ResourceClaimTemplate is used to produce ResourceClaim objects.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What happens to the spec field of a ResourceClaimTemplate after it is set?
response: The spec field of a ResourceClaimTemplate is immutable. A ResourceClaim will get created by the control plane for a Pod when needed and then not get updated anymore.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: Can labels and annotations be added to a ResourceClaimTemplate?
response: Yes, labels and annotations can be added to a ResourceClaimTemplate in the metadata field. These will be copied into the ResourceClaim when creating it.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What is the relationship between a ResourceClaimTemplate and a ResourceClaim?
response: A ResourceClaimTemplate is used to generate a ResourceClaim object. The spec field of the ResourceClaimTemplate is copied unchanged into the ResourceClaim that gets created from this template.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What is the apiVersion of the ResourceClaimTemplate object?
response: The apiVersion of the ResourceClaimTemplate object is resource.k8s.io/v1.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What is the purpose of MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding in Kubernetes?
response: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the MutatingAdmissionPolicy with parametrized resources and together with the optional parameter resource, defines how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: How many times is a policy evaluated for a given admission request?
response: For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: What constrains each evaluation of a policy?
response: Each evaluation is constrained by a runtime cost budget.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: Can adding or removing policies, bindings, or params affect the CEL budget of a given combination?
response: No, adding/removing policies, bindings, or params can not affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: What is the apiVersion of MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding?
response: The apiVersion of MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding is admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: What is the recommended replacement for the Endpoints API?
response: The recommended replacement for the Endpoints API is discoveryv1.EndpointSlice.
title: Endpoints

instruction: What is the deprecation version for the Endpoints API?
response: The Endpoints API is deprecated in v1.33+.
title: Endpoints

instruction: What information is missing from the Endpoints API?
response: The Endpoints API does not contain information about all Service features.
title: Endpoints

instruction: How are addresses placed into subsets in the Endpoints API?
response: Addresses are placed into subsets according to the provided text, but the specific criteria are not detailed.
title: Endpoints

instruction: What is the structure of the Endpoints object in terms of subsets?
response: The set of all endpoints is the union of all subsets. Each subset contains a list of addresses and a list of ports.
title: Endpoints

instruction: What happens when both rawSelector and requirements are present in a SubjectAccessReview request to the kube-apiserver?
response: The request is invalid.
title: FieldSelectorAttributes

instruction: How should webhook authors handle the rawSelector field in FieldSelectorAttributes?
response: Webhook authors are encouraged not to try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. Instead, they should consider the requirements field if set.
title: FieldSelectorAttributes

instruction: What is the purpose of the requirements field in FieldSelectorAttributes?
response: The requirements field is the parsed interpretation of a field selector. All requirements must be met for a resource instance to match the selector.
title: FieldSelectorAttributes

instruction: What happens when rawSelector is present and requirements are empty in a SubjectAccessReview request to the kube-apiserver?
response: The rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds.
title: FieldSelectorAttributes

instruction: What is the recommended approach for webhook authors when handling FieldSelectorAttributes?
response: Webhook authors are encouraged to ensure rawSelector and requirements are not both set, and to consider the requirements field if set, while ignoring the rawSelector field.
title: FieldSelectorAttributes

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm init command?
response: The kubeadm init command initializes a Kubernetes control plane node and sets up the Kubernetes control plane.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What phases does the kubeadm init command execute?
response: The kubeadm init command executes the following phases: preflight, certs, kubeconfig, etcd, and control-plane.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What certificates are generated during the certs phase of kubeadm init?
response: During the certs phase, kubeadm init generates several certificates, including the self-signed Kubernetes CA, the certificate for serving the Kubernetes API, the certificate for the API server to connect to kubelet, and others.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What kubeconfig files are generated during the kubeconfig phase of kubeadm init?
response: During the kubeconfig phase, kubeadm init generates several kubeconfig files, including the admin kubeconfig file, the super-admin kubeconfig file, the kubelet kubeconfig file, the controller manager kubeconfig file, and the scheduler kubeconfig file.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What is generated during the etcd phase of kubeadm init?
response: During the etcd phase, kubeadm init generates a static Pod manifest file for a local, single-node etcd instance.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What is the purpose of the NodeMetrics resource in Kubernetes?
response: NodeMetrics sets resource usage metrics of a node.
title: Kubernetes Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the meaning of the timestamp field in NodeMetrics and PodMetrics?
response: The timestamp field defines the time interval from which metrics were collected, specifically from the interval [Timestamp-Window, Timestamp].
title: Kubernetes Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the structure of a NodeMetricsList in Kubernetes?
response: NodeMetricsList is a list of NodeMetrics, containing standard list metadata and a list of node metrics.
title: Kubernetes Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: What information is provided by the PodMetrics resource?
response: PodMetrics sets resource usage metrics of a pod, including metrics for all containers collected within the same time window.
title: Kubernetes Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the apiVersion for metrics in Kubernetes v1beta1?
response: The apiVersion for metrics in Kubernetes v1beta1 is metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1.
title: Kubernetes Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: What API does Kubernetes provide for provisioning TLS certificates signed by a controlled Certificate Authority (CA)?
response: Kubernetes provides the certificates.k8s.io API for provisioning TLS certificates signed by a controlled Certificate Authority (CA).
title: Manage TLS Certificates in a Cluster

instruction: What is the recommended way to trust a custom CA from an application running as a pod in a cluster?
response: To trust a custom CA from an application running as a pod, you need to add the CA certificate bundle to the list of CA certificates that the TLS client or server trusts. For example, in a golang TLS config, you would parse the certificate chain and add the parsed certificates to the RootCAs field in the tls.Config struct.
title: Manage TLS Certificates in a Cluster

instruction: Can you use the cluster root CA to sign certificates created using the certificates.k8s.io API?
response: It is possible to configure your cluster to use the cluster root CA for signing certificates created using the certificates.k8s.io API, but you should never rely on this. Do not assume that these certificates will validate against the cluster root CA.
title: Manage TLS Certificates in a Cluster

instruction: What tool is required to be installed for some steps in managing TLS certificates in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You need to have the cfssl tool installed. You can download cfssl from https://github.com/cloudflare/cfssl/releases. Additionally, some steps use the jq tool, which you can install via your operating system's software sources or fetch from https://jqlang.github.io/jq/.
title: Manage TLS Certificates in a Cluster

instruction: How should you distribute a custom CA certificate for your workloads in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: If you want to use a custom certificate authority for your workloads, you should generate that CA separately, and distribute its CA certificate using a ConfigMap that your pods have access to read.
title: Manage TLS Certificates in a Cluster

instruction: What happened to PodSecurityPolicy in Kubernetes v1.25?
response: PodSecurityPolicy was removed from Kubernetes in v1.25.
title: Pod Security Policies

instruction: What are the recommended alternatives to PodSecurityPolicy for enforcing restrictions on Pods?
response: You can use either or both Pod Security Admission or a 3rd party admission plugin to enforce similar restrictions on Pods.
title: Pod Security Policies

instruction: Where can I find a migration guide from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller?
response: You can find a migration guide at Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller.
title: Pod Security Policies

instruction: Why might I be seeing different documentation for PodSecurityPolicy depending on my Kubernetes version?
response: If you are not running Kubernetes v1.36, you should check the documentation for your version of Kubernetes.
title: Pod Security Policies

instruction: In which Kubernetes version was PodSecurityPolicy deprecated?
response: PodSecurityPolicy was deprecated in Kubernetes v1.21.
title: Pod Security Policies

instruction: What is the purpose of the schedulingGates field in a Pod's specification?
response: The schedulingGates field contains a list of strings, and each string literal is perceived as a criteria that Pod should be satisfied before considered schedulable.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness

instruction: How can you mark a Pod as not-ready for scheduling?
response: You can mark a Pod as not-ready for scheduling by creating it with one or more scheduling gates in its spec.schedulingGates field.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness

instruction: What is the status of a Pod with scheduling gates applied?
response: A Pod with scheduling gates applied will have a status of 'SchedulingGated'.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness

instruction: How can you inform the scheduler that a Pod is ready for scheduling?
response: You can inform the scheduler that a Pod is ready for scheduling by removing its schedulingGates entirely.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness

instruction: Can you add a new scheduling gate to a Pod after it has been created?
response: No, addition of a new scheduling gate is disallowed after a Pod has been created. However, each schedulingGate can be removed in arbitrary order.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness

instruction: What is recommended for production clusters with multiple users directly accessing the Kubernetes API?
response: For production clusters with multiple users directly accessing the Kubernetes API, it is recommended to use external authentication sources such as OIDC.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms

instruction: Why might X.509 client certificate authentication not be suitable for production use for user authentication?
response: X.509 client certificate authentication might not be suitable for production use due to several restrictions: client certificates cannot be individually revoked, there is no permanent record of client certificates created in the cluster, and private keys used for client certificate authentication cannot be password-protected.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms

instruction: What is a risk associated with revoking a client certificate?
response: If a certificate needs to be invalidated, the certificate authority must be re-keyed, which can introduce availability risks to the cluster.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms

instruction: How should user authentication credentials created using client certificates be managed to mitigate risks?
response: To mitigate risks, it is recommended to configure short lifetimes for user authentication credentials created using client certificates.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms

instruction: Does Kubernetes have an in-built user database within the cluster?
response: No, Kubernetes does not have an in-built user database within the cluster. Instead, it takes user information from the configured authentication system and uses that to make authorization decisions.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms

instruction: What are Kubernetes z-pages used for?
response: Kubernetes z-pages provide runtime diagnostics for Kubernetes components, offering insights into component runtime status and configuration flags.
title: Instrumentation

instruction: How can I access metrics at node, volume, pod, and container level in Kubernetes?
response: You can access metrics at node, volume, pod, and container level in Kubernetes through mechanisms provided by the kubelet.
title: Instrumentation

instruction: What is Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics in Kubernetes?
response: Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics in Kubernetes provide detailed information to identify resource pressure. They can be used to understand and troubleshoot performance issues related to resource constraints.
title: Instrumentation

instruction: Where can I find details of the metric data that Kubernetes components export?
response: Details of the metric data that Kubernetes components export can be found in the Kubernetes Metrics Reference.
title: Instrumentation

instruction: How are Pod and Container metrics collected in Kubernetes?
response: Pod and Container metrics are collected via the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) in Kubernetes.
title: Instrumentation

instruction: What are the prerequisites for manually rotating CA certificates in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates

instruction: What files need to be distributed to all control plane nodes during manual rotation of CA certificates?
response: The new CA certificates and private keys, specifically ca.crt, ca.key, front-proxy-ca.crt, and front-proxy-ca.key, need to be distributed to all control plane nodes in the Kubernetes certificates directory.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates

instruction: How should the --root-ca-file flag be updated for the kube-controller-manager during CA certificate rotation?
response: The --root-ca-file flag for the kube-controller-manager should be updated to include both old and new CA certificates.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates

instruction: What is a potential issue when updating the kube-controller-manager flags, and how can it be worked around?
response: If the files specified by the kube-controller-manager flags --client-ca-file and --cluster-signing-cert-file point to the same ca.crt file which is now a bundle (includes both old and new CA), an error will occur. To work around this, copy the new CA to a separate file and make the flags --client-ca-file and --cluster-signing-cert-file point to the copy.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates

instruction: What should be done before restarting the API server during manual rotation of CA certificates?
response: Make sure to back up your certificate directory along with configuration files and any other necessary files. Also, ensure that the API server can be gracefully terminated so clients can cleanly disconnect from one API server and reconnect to another.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of device plugins in Kubernetes?
response: Device plugins let you configure your cluster with support for devices or resources that require vendor-specific setup, such as GPUs, NICs, FPGAs, or non-volatile main memory.
title: Device Plugins

instruction: How does a device plugin register itself with the kubelet?
response: A device plugin can register itself with the kubelet through a gRPC service called Registration. During registration, the device plugin needs to send the name of its Unix socket, the Device Plugin API version, and the ResourceName it wants to advertise.
title: Device Plugins

instruction: What is the format for ResourceName in device plugin registration?
response: The ResourceName needs to follow the extended resource naming scheme as vendor-domain/resourcetype. For example, an NVIDIA GPU is advertised as nvidia.com/gpu.
title: Device Plugins

instruction: Can devices be shared between containers in a pod?
response: No, devices cannot be shared between containers.
title: Device Plugins

instruction: How are extended resources, such as those provided by device plugins, handled in terms of requests and limits?
response: Extended resources are only supported as integer resources and cannot be overcommitted. Requesting extended resources is similar to managing requests and limits for other resources, with the specified constraints.
title: Device Plugins

instruction: What is the default reclaim policy for dynamically provisioned PersistentVolumes in Kubernetes?
response: The default reclaim policy for dynamically provisioned PersistentVolumes is 'Delete'.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolume when its reclaim policy is set to 'Retain' and the corresponding PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted?
response: When a PersistentVolume's reclaim policy is set to 'Retain' and the corresponding PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted, the PersistentVolume is moved to the 'Released' phase, allowing for manual data recovery.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: How do I list all PersistentVolumes in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can list all PersistentVolumes in your cluster by running the command 'kubectl get pv'.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: How do I change the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume to 'Retain'?
response: You can change the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume to 'Retain' by running the command 'kubectl patch pv <your-pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}''.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: Why might the automatic deletion of a dynamically provisioned PersistentVolume be inappropriate?
response: The automatic deletion of a dynamically provisioned PersistentVolume might be inappropriate if the volume contains precious data that needs to be manually recovered.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the purpose of the ExecCredential resource in Kubernetes client authentication?
response: ExecCredential is used by exec-based plugins to communicate credentials to HTTP transports.
title: Client Authentication (v1beta1)

instruction: What information does the Cluster field in ExecCredentialSpec contain?
response: Cluster contains information to allow an exec plugin to communicate with the Kubernetes cluster being authenticated to, including server address, TLS server name, and certificate authority data.
title: Client Authentication (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the effect of setting InsecureSkipTLSVerify to true in the Cluster field?
response: InsecureSkipTLSVerify skips the validity check for the server's certificate, making HTTPS connections insecure.
title: Client Authentication (v1beta1)

instruction: How is additional config data for the exec plugin stored in the Cluster object?
response: Additional config data specific to the exec plugin is stored in the clientcmd Cluster object's extensions[client.authentication.k8s.io/exec] field and sourced into the Config field of ExecCredentialSpec.
title: Client Authentication (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the CAData field in the Cluster object?
response: CAData contains PEM-encoded certificate authority certificates. If empty, system roots should be used.
title: Client Authentication (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl config rename-context command?
response: The kubectl config rename-context command renames a context from the kubeconfig file.
title: kubectl config rename-context

instruction: What happens if the context being renamed is the 'current-context'?
response: If the context being renamed is the 'current-context', this field will also be updated.
title: kubectl config rename-context

instruction: What are the required arguments for the kubectl config rename-context command?
response: The required arguments for the kubectl config rename-context command are CONTEXT_NAME (the context name that you want to change) and NEW_NAME (the new name you want to set).
title: kubectl config rename-context

instruction: Can you provide an example of how to rename the context 'old-name' to 'new-name' in your kubeconfig file?
response: To rename the context 'old-name' to 'new-name' in your kubeconfig file, use the command: kubectl config rename-context old-name new-name
title: kubectl config rename-context

instruction: What is the effect of the --kubeconfig option on the kubectl config rename-context command?
response: The --kubeconfig option specifies the kubeconfig file to use. If specified, the command will use the provided kubeconfig file instead of the default one.
title: kubectl config rename-context

instruction: What are the two main ways to add Nodes to the API server in Kubernetes?
response: The two main ways to add Nodes to the API server in Kubernetes are: 1) the kubelet on a node self-registers to the control plane, and 2) a human user manually adds a Node object.
title: Nodes

instruction: What happens if a Node object is created with a JSON manifest and the kubelet has not registered to the API server with a matching name?
response: Kubernetes checks that a kubelet has registered to the API server that matches the metadata.name field of the Node. If the node is healthy, it is eligible to run a Pod. Otherwise, that node is ignored for any cluster activity until it becomes healthy.
title: Nodes

instruction: What are the necessary components that must be running on a node for it to be considered healthy and eligible to run a Pod?
response: The necessary components that must be running on a node for it to be considered healthy and eligible to run a Pod are the kubelet, a container runtime, and the kube-proxy.
title: Nodes

instruction: What happens to a Node object that is created but is not healthy?
response: Kubernetes keeps the object for the invalid Node and continues checking to see whether it becomes healthy. You, or a controller, must explicitly delete the Node object to stop that health checking.
title: Nodes

instruction: What is the requirement for the name of a Node object in Kubernetes?
response: The name of a Node object must be a valid DNS subdomain name.
title: Nodes

instruction: What is the purpose of a PodGroup in Kubernetes?
response: A PodGroup is a runtime object that represents a group of Pods scheduled together as a single unit, carrying both the scheduling policy and the scheduling status for a specific instance of that group.
title: PodGroup API

instruction: What are the requirements to use the PodGroup API?
response: Your cluster must have the scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2 API group enabled, as well as the GenericWorkload feature gate.
title: PodGroup API

instruction: What are the types of scheduling policies supported by a PodGroup?
response: A PodGroup supports two types of scheduling policies: basic and gang.
title: PodGroup API

instruction: How can devices available through Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) be requested for a PodGroup?
response: Devices available through DRA can be requested by a PodGroup through its spec.resourceClaims field, where ResourceClaims associated with PodGroups can be shared by all Pods belonging to the group.
title: PodGroup API

instruction: What is the purpose of spec.podGroupTemplateRef in a PodGroup?
response: The optional spec.podGroupTemplateRef links the PodGroup back to the PodGroupTemplate in the Workload it was created from, which is useful for observability and tooling.
title: PodGroup API

instruction: What is the recommended way to create a Kubernetes cluster for testing purposes?
response: You can create a Kubernetes cluster using minikube or use one of the Kubernetes playgrounds such as iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, or KodeKloud.
title: Managing Secrets using Kustomize

instruction: How do you generate a Secret using Kustomize?
response: You can generate a Secret by defining a secretGenerator in a kustomization.yaml file that references other existing files, .env files, or literal values.
title: Managing Secrets using Kustomize

instruction: What is the purpose of the -n flag when creating files for Secret values?
response: The -n flag ensures that there's no newline character at the end of your files.
title: Managing Secrets using Kustomize

instruction: How do you apply a kustomization file to create a Secret?
response: To create the Secret, apply the directory that contains the kustomization file using the command: kubectl apply -k <directory-path>.
title: Managing Secrets using Kustomize

instruction: Why does the Secret name include a hash value?
response: The Secret name is created by hashing the Secret data and appending the hash value to the name. This ensures that a new Secret is generated each time the data is modified.
title: Managing Secrets using Kustomize

instruction: How do you create a new LoadBalancer service named my-lbs with port 5678 targeting port 8080?
response: kubectl create service loadbalancer my-lbs --tcp=5678:8080
title: kubectl create service loadbalancer

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run option when creating a LoadBalancer service?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to test the creation of a LoadBalancer service without actually creating it. It can be set to 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If set to 'client', it will only print the object that would be sent without sending it. If set to 'server', it will submit a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create service loadbalancer

instruction: How do you specify port pairs when creating a LoadBalancer service?
response: You can specify port pairs using the --tcp option, in the format '<port>:<targetPort>'. For example, --tcp=5678:8080.
title: kubectl create service loadbalancer

instruction: What is the default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option when creating a LoadBalancer service?
response: The default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option is to ignore any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing in the template, and it is set to true by default.
title: kubectl create service loadbalancer

instruction: Can you save the configuration of a LoadBalancer service in its annotation for future kubectl apply operations?
response: Yes, you can save the configuration of a LoadBalancer service in its annotation for future kubectl apply operations by using the --save-config option.
title: kubectl create service loadbalancer

instruction: How can I view a Node's status and other details in Kubernetes?
response: You can use kubectl to view a Node's status and other details by running the command: kubectl describe node <insert-node-name-here>
title: Node Status

instruction: What does the 'Ready' condition in a Node's status indicate?
response: The 'Ready' condition in a Node's status indicates that the node is healthy and ready to accept pods if its status is 'True', not healthy and not accepting pods if its status is 'False', and the node controller has not heard from the node in the last node-monitor-grace-period (default is 50 seconds) if its status is 'Unknown'.
title: Node Status

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'Addresses' field in a Node's status?
response: The 'Addresses' field in a Node's status contains information about the node's IP addresses, including the hostname as reported by the node's kernel, the externally routable IP address, and the IP address routable only within the cluster. The usage of these fields varies depending on your cloud provider or bare metal configuration.
title: Node Status

instruction: How does the 'SchedulingDisabled' condition relate to cordoned nodes?
response: If you use command-line tools to print details of a cordoned Node, the Condition includes 'SchedulingDisabled'. However, 'SchedulingDisabled' is not a Condition in the Kubernetes API; instead, cordoned nodes are marked 'Unschedulable' in their spec.
title: Node Status

instruction: What are the possible conditions that can be reported in a Node's status?
response: The possible conditions that can be reported in a Node's status include 'Ready', 'DiskPressure', 'MemoryPressure', 'PIDPressure', and 'NetworkUnavailable'. These conditions describe the node's health and ability to accept pods.
title: Node Status

instruction: What is the purpose of the uid field in an AdmissionRequest?
response: The uid field is an identifier for the individual request/response. It allows distinguishing instances of requests which are otherwise identical, and is meant to track the round trip between the KAS and the WebHook, not the user request. It is suitable for correlating log entries between the webhook and apiserver, for either auditing or debugging.
title: kube-apiserver Admission (v1)

instruction: What is the difference between kind and requestKind in an AdmissionRequest?
response: The kind field is the fully-qualified type of object being submitted, while the requestKind field is the fully-qualified type of the original API request. If they differ, an equivalent match and conversion was performed. For example, if a webhook registered a rule for apps/v1 deployments, and an API request is made to apps/v1beta1 deployments, the kind field would be apps/v1 Deployment, and the requestKind field would be apps/v1beta1 Deployment.
title: kube-apiserver Admission (v1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the subResource field in an AdmissionRequest?
response: The subResource field is the subresource being requested, if any. For example, it could be 'status' or 'scale'.
title: kube-apiserver Admission (v1)

instruction: What is the relationship between the resource and requestResource fields in an AdmissionRequest?
response: The resource field is the fully-qualified resource being requested, while the requestResource field is the fully-qualified resource of the original API request. If they differ, an equivalent match and conversion was performed.
title: kube-apiserver Admission (v1)

instruction: What is the apiVersion of the AdmissionReview resource?
response: The apiVersion of the AdmissionReview resource is admission.k8s.io/v1.
title: kube-apiserver Admission (v1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'command' and 'args' fields in a Kubernetes Pod configuration file?
response: The 'command' field overrides the default command provided by the container image, and the 'args' field provides arguments for the command. If 'args' is defined but not 'command', the default command is used with the new arguments.
title: Define a Command and Arguments for a Container

instruction: How do you define a command and arguments for a container in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: You define a command and arguments for a container in a Kubernetes Pod by including the 'command' and 'args' fields in the Pod's configuration file. The 'command' field corresponds to ENTRYPOINT, and the 'args' field corresponds to CMD in some container runtimes.
title: Define a Command and Arguments for a Container

instruction: What happens to the command and arguments of a container in a Pod after the Pod is created?
response: The command and arguments that you define in the configuration file cannot be changed after the Pod is created.
title: Define a Command and Arguments for a Container

instruction: How can you view the output of a command that ran in a container in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: You can view the output of a command that ran in a container in a Kubernetes Pod by using the 'kubectl logs' command followed by the name of the Pod.
title: Define a Command and Arguments for a Container

instruction: Can you use environment variables to define arguments for a command in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: Yes, you can use environment variables to define arguments for a command in a Kubernetes Pod. Instead of providing strings directly for 'args', you can define environment variables using the 'env' field.
title: Define a Command and Arguments for a Container

instruction: What is the purpose of a LeaseCandidate in Kubernetes?
response: A LeaseCandidate defines a candidate for a Lease object, used in coordinated leader election to pick the best leader from a list of candidates.
title: LeaseCandidate

instruction: What is the format required for BinaryVersion and EmulationVersion fields in LeaseCandidateSpec?
response: Both BinaryVersion and EmulationVersion must be in a semver format without leading 'v'.
title: LeaseCandidate

instruction: What is the relationship between EmulationVersion and BinaryVersion in LeaseCandidateSpec?
response: EmulationVersion must be less than or equal to BinaryVersion. EmulationVersion is required when the strategy is 'OldestEmulationVersion'.
title: LeaseCandidate

instruction: What is the significance of PingTime and RenewTime in LeaseCandidateSpec?
response: PingTime is the last time the server requested the LeaseCandidate to renew, used to check if LeaseCandidates have become ineligible. RenewTime is the last update time of the LeaseCandidate, updated when a Lease needs to perform leader election.
title: LeaseCandidate

instruction: Can multiple LeaseCandidates reference the same LeaseName?
response: Yes, multiple lease candidates may reference the same Lease.name.
title: LeaseCandidate

instruction: What is the primary benefit of using NodeLocal DNSCache in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: NodeLocal DNSCache improves Cluster DNS performance by running a DNS caching agent on cluster nodes as a DaemonSet, reducing latency and conntrack races, and avoiding UDP DNS entries filling up conntrack table.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters

instruction: How do Pods in 'ClusterFirst' DNS mode reach out to the DNS caching agent when NodeLocal DNSCache is enabled?
response: Pods will reach out to the DNS caching agent running on the same node, thereby avoiding iptables DNAT rules and connection tracking.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters

instruction: What is the default suffix for cluster hostnames that the local caching agent will query kube-dns service for cache misses?
response: The default suffix is "cluster.local".
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters

instruction: What is the benefit of upgrading DNS queries from UDP to TCP in the context of NodeLocal DNSCache?
response: Upgrading DNS queries from UDP to TCP would reduce tail latency attributed to dropped UDP packets and DNS timeouts usually up to 30s (3 retries + 10s timeout), and TCP conntrack entries will be removed on connection close.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters

instruction: Does enabling NodeLocal DNSCache require changes to applications?
response: No, since the nodelocal cache listens for UDP DNS queries, applications don't need to be changed.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters

instruction: How do you create a job named 'my-job' using the 'busybox' image?
response: You can create a job named 'my-job' using the 'busybox' image by running the command: kubectl create job my-job --image=busybox
title: kubectl create job

instruction: Can you create a job from a cron job and if so, how?
response: Yes, you can create a job from a cron job. To do so, use the --from option followed by the cron job name. For example, to create a job from a cron job named 'a-cronjob', run: kubectl create job test-job --from=cronjob/a-cronjob
title: kubectl create job

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run option when creating a job?
response: The --dry-run option allows you to test the creation of a job without actually creating it. It can be set to 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If set to 'client', it will only print the object that would be sent without sending it. If set to 'server', it will submit a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create job

instruction: How do you specify a command to run in a job?
response: You can specify a command to run in a job by adding the command and its arguments after a double dash (--). For example, to create a job that runs the 'date' command using the 'busybox' image, run: kubectl create job my-job --image=busybox -- date
title: kubectl create job

instruction: What is the default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option?
response: The default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option is to be set to true. This means that if there are errors in templates due to missing fields or map keys, they will be ignored. This option only applies to golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl create job

instruction: What is the origin of the name Kubernetes?
response: The name Kubernetes originates from Greek, meaning helmsman or pilot. The abbreviation K8s results from counting the eight letters between the 'K' and the 's'.
title: Overview

instruction: What problem does Kubernetes solve in a production environment?
response: In a production environment, Kubernetes manages containers that run applications and ensures there is no downtime. For example, if a container goes down, Kubernetes starts another container.
title: Overview

instruction: What are some of the features provided by Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes provides service discovery and load balancing, storage orchestration, automated rollouts and rollbacks, automatic bin packing, and self-healing.
title: Overview

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle traffic to a container?
response: If traffic to a container is high, Kubernetes is able to load balance and distribute the network traffic so that the deployment is stable.
title: Overview

instruction: What happens to a container that fails in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Kubernetes restarts containers that fail, and replaces containers that do not respond.
title: Overview

instruction: What is crictl used for in Kubernetes?
response: crictl is a command-line interface for inspecting and debugging CRI-compatible container runtimes.
title: Other Tools

instruction: What are the primary functions of the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: The Kubernetes Dashboard allows you to deploy containerized applications to a Kubernetes cluster, troubleshoot them, and manage the cluster and its resources.
title: Other Tools

instruction: What is Helm used for in Kubernetes?
response: Helm is a tool for managing packages of pre-configured Kubernetes resources, known as Helm charts. It can be used to find and use popular software packaged as Kubernetes charts, share applications as Kubernetes charts, create reproducible builds, and manage releases.
title: Other Tools

instruction: What is the main purpose of kind in Kubernetes?
response: kind is a tool for running local Kubernetes clusters using Docker container nodes, primarily designed for testing Kubernetes itself, but also usable for local development or CI.
title: Other Tools

instruction: What does Kompose help with in Kubernetes?
response: Kompose helps Docker Compose users move to Kubernetes by translating Docker Compose files into Kubernetes objects, allowing users to go from local Docker development to managing applications via Kubernetes.
title: Other Tools

instruction: What is a PodGroup in Kubernetes?
response: A PodGroup represents a runtime instance of pods grouped together. PodGroups are created by workload controllers from Workload.podGroupTemplates.
title: Scheduling

instruction: How is the PodGroup API enabled?
response: The PodGroup API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.
title: Scheduling

instruction: What does a PriorityClass define?
response: A PriorityClass defines mapping from a priority class name to the priority integer value. The value can be any valid integer.
title: Scheduling

instruction: What is the purpose of the Workload API?
response: The Workload API allows for expressing scheduling constraints that should be used when managing the lifecycle of workloads from the scheduling perspective, including scheduling, preemption, eviction and other phases.
title: Scheduling

instruction: How is the Workload API enabled?
response: The Workload API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.
title: Scheduling

instruction: What is the primary function of an Ingress in Kubernetes?
response: An Ingress is a collection of rules that allow inbound connections to reach the endpoints defined by a backend. It can be configured to give services externally-reachable URLs, load balance traffic, terminate SSL, and offer name-based virtual hosting.
title: Ingress

instruction: What are some of the features that can be configured in an Ingress?
response: An Ingress can be configured to give services externally-reachable URLs, load balance traffic, terminate SSL, and offer name-based virtual hosting.
title: Ingress

instruction: What is the purpose of the defaultBackend field in an IngressSpec?
response: The defaultBackend field is the backend that should handle requests that don't match any rule. If Rules are not specified, DefaultBackend must be specified.
title: Ingress

instruction: What is the role of the Ingress concept in Kubernetes?
response: The Ingress concept lets you map traffic to different backends based on rules you define via the Kubernetes API. It makes your HTTP (or HTTPS) network service available using a protocol-aware configuration mechanism.
title: Ingress

instruction: What are the main components of an Ingress object in Kubernetes?
response: An Ingress object consists of apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec, and status. The spec field describes the desired state of the Ingress, while the status field describes the current state of the Ingress.
title: Ingress

instruction: How does a Pod in one namespace access a Service in another namespace using DNS?
response: By specifying the namespace in the DNS query. For example, a Pod in the 'test' namespace can access a Service named 'data' in the 'prod' namespace by querying for 'data.prod'.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: What is the default DNS search list for a client Pod?
response: The default DNS search list for a client Pod includes the Pod's own namespace and the cluster's default domain.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: How are DNS queries expanded for Pods?
response: DNS queries may be expanded using the Pod's /etc/resolv.conf, which is configured by kubelet. The values of the search option are used to expand queries.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: What is the DNS record format for a normal Service?
response: Normal Services are assigned DNS A and/or AAAA records with a name of the form 'my-svc.my-namespace.svc.cluster-domain.example', which resolves to the cluster IP of the Service.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: Can a Pod resolve a Service name without specifying the namespace?
response: No, a DNS query that doesn't specify a namespace is limited to the Pod's namespace. For example, a Pod in the 'test' namespace querying for 'data' will return no results, but querying for 'data.test' or 'data.test.svc.cluster.local' will.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: How do I expose a deployment named 'nginx' as a new Kubernetes service on port 80?
response: You can expose a deployment named 'nginx' as a new Kubernetes service on port 80 using the command: kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80
title: kubectl expose

instruction: What happens if I don't specify a port when exposing a resource with multiple ports?
response: If you don't specify a port via --port and the exposed resource has multiple ports, all ports will be re-used by the new service.
title: kubectl expose

instruction: Can I expose a pod as a service with a specific name?
response: Yes, you can expose a pod as a service with a specific name. For example, to expose a pod named 'valid-pod' as a service named 'frontend' on port 444, use the command: kubectl expose pod valid-pod --port=444 --name=frontend
title: kubectl expose

instruction: What types of resources can I expose as a new Kubernetes service?
response: You can expose the following types of resources as a new Kubernetes service: pod (po), service (svc), replicationcontroller (rc), deployment (deploy), replicaset (rs)
title: kubectl expose

instruction: How do I expose a replication controller named 'nginx' as a service on port 80 and connect to the containers on port 8000?
response: You can expose a replication controller named 'nginx' as a service on port 80 and connect to the containers on port 8000 using the command: kubectl expose rc nginx --port=80 --target-port=8000
title: kubectl expose

instruction: What happens to a critical add-on pod if it is evicted and becomes pending in a highly utilized cluster?
response: The cluster may stop working properly if a critical add-on is evicted and becomes pending. This can occur if the evicted pod is critical and either there are other pending pods that schedule into the space vacated by the evicted critical add-on pod or the amount of resources available on the node changed for some other reason.
title: Guaranteed Scheduling For Critical Add-On Pods

instruction: How can a pod be marked as critical to prevent it from becoming permanently unavailable?
response: To mark a Pod as critical, set priorityClassName for that Pod to either system-cluster-critical or system-node-critical. The system-node-critical priority is the highest available priority, even higher than system-cluster-critical.
title: Guaranteed Scheduling For Critical Add-On Pods

instruction: What is the difference between system-cluster-critical and system-node-critical priority classes?
response: system-node-critical is the highest available priority, even higher than system-cluster-critical. This means that a pod with system-node-critical priority will be given more scheduling preference than one with system-cluster-critical priority.
title: Guaranteed Scheduling For Critical Add-On Pods

instruction: Can a static pod marked as critical be evicted?
response: No, a static pod marked as critical can't be evicted. However, non-static pods marked as critical are always rescheduled.
title: Guaranteed Scheduling For Critical Add-On Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of marking a pod as critical in Kubernetes?
response: Marking a pod as critical is not meant to prevent evictions entirely; it only prevents the pod from becoming permanently unavailable. This ensures that critical add-ons, such as metrics-server, DNS, and UI, continue to run and provide necessary functionality to the cluster.
title: Guaranteed Scheduling For Critical Add-On Pods

instruction: What are the available configuration APIs for client authentication in Kubernetes?
response: The available configuration APIs for client authentication in Kubernetes are Client Authentication (v1) and Client Authentication (v1beta1).
title: Configuration APIs

instruction: What configuration API is used for kube-apiserver audit configuration?
response: The configuration API used for kube-apiserver audit configuration is kube-apiserver Audit Configuration (v1).
title: Configuration APIs

instruction: What is the configuration API for kubeadm, and what versions are available?
response: The configuration API for kubeadm is kubeadm Configuration, and the available versions are v1beta3 and v1beta4.
title: Configuration APIs

instruction: What configuration API is used for Kubelet CredentialProvider?
response: The configuration API used for Kubelet CredentialProvider is Kubelet CredentialProvider (v1).
title: Configuration APIs

instruction: What configuration API is used for Event Rate Limit Configuration?
response: The configuration API used for Event Rate Limit Configuration is Event Rate Limit Configuration (v1alpha1).
title: Configuration APIs

instruction: What is the primary purpose of sidecar containers in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: Sidecar containers are used to enhance or extend the functionality of the primary application container by providing additional services, such as logging, monitoring, security, or data synchronization, without directly altering the primary application code.
title: Sidecar Containers

instruction: How are sidecar containers implemented in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes implements sidecar containers as a special case of init containers; sidecar containers remain running after Pod startup.
title: Sidecar Containers

instruction: Can sidecar containers be restarted independently of the main application container?
response: Yes, provided that the cluster has the SidecarContainers feature gate enabled, you can specify a restartPolicy for containers listed in a Pod's initContainers field, allowing sidecar containers to be started, stopped, or restarted without affecting the main application container and other init containers.
title: Sidecar Containers

instruction: How do you define a sidecar container in a Kubernetes Deployment?
response: You can define a sidecar container by listing it under initContainers with a restartPolicy set to Always. Kubernetes treats such containers as sidecars that continue running for the lifetime of the Pod.
title: Sidecar Containers

instruction: Is it possible to run multiple containers in a Pod without using sidecar or init container definitions?
response: Yes, you can run a Pod with multiple containers that are not marked as init or sidecar containers. This is appropriate if the containers within the Pod are required for the Pod to work overall, but you don't need to control which containers start or stop first.
title: Sidecar Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of Services in Kubernetes?
response: Services enable connecting applications in Kubernetes.
title: Services

instruction: How can users get help with specific questions about using Kubernetes?
response: Users can ask questions on Stack Overflow.
title: Services

instruction: What should users do if they want to report a problem or suggest an improvement for Kubernetes documentation?
response: Users should open an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Services

instruction: What was updated on June 16, 2021, at 5:57 PM PST in the Kubernetes documentation?
response: The update removed exec permission on markdown files.
title: Services

instruction: What are some topics related to Services that users can explore in the Kubernetes documentation?
response: Users can explore using Source IP, termination behavior for Pods and their endpoints.
title: Services

instruction: How do you update a cluster role binding to use a different service account?
response: You can update a cluster role binding to use a different service account using the command `kubectl set subject clusterrolebinding <binding-name> --serviceaccount=<namespace>:<serviceaccount-name>`.
title: kubectl set subject

instruction: Can you update multiple users in a role binding at once?
response: Yes, you can update multiple users in a role binding at once by specifying multiple `--user` options. For example: `kubectl set subject rolebinding <binding-name> --user=<user1> --user=<user2>`. You can also update groups using the `--group` option.
title: kubectl set subject

instruction: What is the purpose of the `--dry-run` option when updating a role binding?
response: The `--dry-run` option allows you to see the changes that would be made without actually updating the role binding. You can choose from three modes: `none` (default), `client`, or `server`. In `client` mode, the command prints the updated object locally without sending it to the server. In `server` mode, the command sends a request to the server but does not persist the changes.
title: kubectl set subject

instruction: How do you update a role binding using a file?
response: You can update a role binding using a file by specifying the file path with the `-f` option. For example: `kubectl set subject -f <file-path> --user=<new-user>`. You can also use directory or URL to files.
title: kubectl set subject

instruction: What output formats are supported by the `kubectl set subject` command?
response: The `kubectl set subject` command supports the following output formats: json, yaml, kyaml, name, go-template, go-template-file, template, templatefile, jsonpath, jsonpath-as-json, and jsonpath-file. You can specify the output format using the `-o` or `--output` option.
title: kubectl set subject

instruction: What is a key security mechanism for any Kubernetes cluster?
response: A key security mechanism for any Kubernetes cluster is to control access to the Kubernetes API.
title: Security

instruction: How does Kubernetes protect configuration values that require confidentiality?
response: The Secret API provides basic protection for configuration values that require confidentiality.
title: Security

instruction: What are network policies used for in Kubernetes?
response: Network policies let you control network traffic between Pods, or between Pods and the network outside your cluster.
title: Security

instruction: What is the purpose of admission controllers in Kubernetes?
response: Admission controllers are plugins that intercept Kubernetes API requests and can validate or mutate the requests based on specific fields in the request.
title: Security

instruction: What does Kubernetes audit logging provide?
response: Kubernetes audit logging provides a security-relevant, chronological set of records documenting the sequence of actions in a cluster.
title: Security

instruction: What happens when both rawSelector and requirements are present in LabelSelectorAttributes for SubjectAccessReview endpoints of the kube-apiserver?
response: The request is invalid.
title: LabelSelectorAttributes

instruction: How should webhook implementations handle the rawSelector field in LabelSelectorAttributes?
response: Webhook authors are encouraged not to try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. Instead, they should consider the requirements field if set.
title: LabelSelectorAttributes

instruction: What is the effect of having rawSelector present and requirements empty in LabelSelectorAttributes for SubjectAccessReview endpoints?
response: The rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds.
title: LabelSelectorAttributes

instruction: Can webhook implementations parse the rawSelector field in LabelSelectorAttributes?
response: Webhook implementations are encouraged to ignore rawSelector. The kube-apiserver's SubjectAccessReview will parse the rawSelector as long as the requirements are not present.
title: LabelSelectorAttributes

instruction: What is the purpose of the requirements field in LabelSelectorAttributes?
response: requirements is the parsed interpretation of a label selector. All requirements must be met for a resource instance to match the selector. Webhook implementations should handle requirements.
title: LabelSelectorAttributes

instruction: How do I list all Container images running in a cluster using kubectl?
response: You can use kubectl to fetch all Pods running in a cluster and format the output to pull out the list of Containers for each. Specifically, use kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath={.items[*].spec['initContainers', 'containers'][*].image}.
title: List All Container Images Running in a Cluster

instruction: How do I format the output of kubectl to include only the list of Container image names?
response: Use -o jsonpath={.items[*].spec['initContainers', 'containers'][*].image}. This will recursively parse out the image field from the returned json.
title: List All Container Images Running in a Cluster

instruction: What tools can I use to further format the output of kubectl and aggregate image counts?
response:
You can use tr to replace spaces with newlines, sort to sort the results, and uniq to aggregate image counts. The full command would be kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath={.items[*].spec['initContainers', 'containers'][*].image} | tr -s '[[:space:]]' '
' | sort | uniq -c.
title: List All Container Images Running in a Cluster

instruction: How do I list Container images for Pods matching a specific label?
response: Use the -l flag to target only Pods matching a specific label. For example, kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath={.items[*].spec.containers[*].image} -l app=nginx.
title: List All Container Images Running in a Cluster

instruction: Can I use jsonpath to iterate over elements individually when listing Container images?
