instruction: What tools are required to be installed to verify signed Kubernetes artifacts?
response: You will need to have the following tools installed: cosign, curl, and jq.
title: Verify Signed Kubernetes Artifacts

instruction: How do you verify the signature of a binary artifact, such as kubectl, using cosign?
response: To verify a particular binary, retrieve it together with its signature and certificate using curl, then verify the blob using cosign verify-blob with the --signature, --certificate, --certificate-identity, and --certificate-oidc-issuer options.
title: Verify Signed Kubernetes Artifacts

instruction: What is the command to verify the signature of an image, such as kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.36.0, using cosign?
response: The command to verify the signature of an image is: cosign verify registry.k8s.io/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.36.0 --certificate-identity krel-trust@k8s-releng-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com --certificate-oidc-issuer https://accounts.google.com | jq .
title: Verify Signed Kubernetes Artifacts

instruction: How do you verify all signed control plane images for the latest stable version (v1.36.0)?
response: To verify all signed control plane images for the latest stable version (v1.36.0), you can run a series of commands that involve curl, grep, cut, sed, and sort to generate a list of images, then use a while loop to verify each image using cosign verify.
title: Verify Signed Kubernetes Artifacts

instruction: What information must be specified when verifying an image using cosign verify?
response: When verifying an image using cosign verify, you must specify the --certificate-identity and --certificate-oidc-issuer options, such as: --certificate-identity krel-trust@k8s-releng-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com --certificate-oidc-issuer https://accounts.google.com.
title: Verify Signed Kubernetes Artifacts

instruction: What are the two ways to expose Pod and container fields to a running container using the Downward API?
response: The two ways to expose Pod and container fields to a running container using the Downward API are: as environment variables and as files in a downwardAPI volume.
title: Downward API

instruction: Which Pod-level fields can be made available to a container using fieldRef?
response: The Pod-level fields that can be made available to a container using fieldRef are: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, metadata.uid, metadata.annotations['<KEY>'], and metadata.labels['<KEY>'].
title: Downward API

instruction: Can you expose the Pod's node name as an environment variable using the Downward API?
response: Yes, you can expose the Pod's node name as an environment variable using the Downward API. The field spec.nodeName is available through environment variables.
title: Downward API

instruction: What is the purpose of the Downward API?
response: The purpose of the Downward API is to allow containers to consume information about themselves or the cluster without using the Kubernetes client or API server, and to reduce coupling between the container and Kubernetes.
title: Downward API

instruction: Can you expose the Pod's primary IP address as a file in a downwardAPI volume?
response: No, the Pod's primary IP address (status.hostIP) is only available through environment variables, not as a downwardAPI volume fieldRef.
title: Downward API

instruction: What happens to pods running on a node if it is shut down without being drained?
response: A node shutdown could lead to workload failure if the node is not drained before the shutdown.
title: Node Shutdowns

instruction: How can the kubelet ensure that pods follow the normal pod termination process during node shutdown?
response: The kubelet ensures that pods follow the normal pod termination process during the node shutdown. During node shutdown, the kubelet does not accept new Pods (even if those Pods are already bound to the node).
title: Node Shutdowns

instruction: What is the conflict with the unattended-upgrades package from Debian and node graceful shutdown?
response: The unattended-upgrades package from Debian conflicts with node graceful shutdown in its normal configuration. If you use the default configuration of unattended-upgrades, which customizes the server shutdown grace period, then the kubelet fails to obtain the necessary lock to handle shutdown events properly if the shutdownGracePeriod value is greater than 30 seconds.
title: Node Shutdowns

instruction: How can you avoid the conflict between unattended-upgrades and node graceful shutdown on Debian?
response: To avoid this conflict, you can suppress part of the unattended-upgrades configuration by making /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/unattended-upgrades-logind-maxdelay.conf be a symbolic link to /dev/null.
title: Node Shutdowns

instruction: What is the feature gate that controls the graceful node shutdown on Linux?
response: On Linux, the graceful node shutdown feature is controlled with the GracefulNodeShutdown feature gate which is enabled by default in Kubernetes v1.21.
title: Node Shutdowns

instruction: What is Pod Security Admission in Kubernetes?
response: Pod Security Admission is an admission controller that carries out checks against the Kubernetes Pod Security Standards when new pods are created.
title: Apply Pod Security Standards at the Cluster Level

instruction: What are the available modes for applying Pod Security Standards?
response: Pod Security Admission lets you apply built-in Pod Security Standards with the following modes: enforce, audit, and warn.
title: Apply Pod Security Standards at the Cluster Level

instruction: How do you create a cluster with no Pod Security Standards applied for testing purposes?
response: You can create a cluster with no Pod Security Standards applied using the command: kind create cluster --name psa-wo-cluster-pss
title: Apply Pod Security Standards at the Cluster Level

instruction: What is the purpose of this tutorial?
response: This tutorial shows you how to enforce the baseline Pod Security Standard at the cluster level which applies a standard configuration to all namespaces in a cluster.
title: Apply Pod Security Standards at the Cluster Level

instruction: Is Pod Security Admission applicable to managed clusters where you cannot configure the control plane?
response: No, for managed clusters where you are not able to configure the control plane, you should read 'Apply Pod Security Standards at the namespace level'.
title: Apply Pod Security Standards at the Cluster Level

instruction: What is the recommended setting for the endpoint in TracingConfiguration?
response: The recommended setting for the endpoint in TracingConfiguration is unset, which defaults to localhost:4317.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What are the required claims in a JWT payload for authentication?
response: The minimum valid JWT payload must contain the following claims: "iss", "aud", "exp", and "username".
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the purpose of the jwt field in AuthenticationConfiguration?
response: The jwt field in AuthenticationConfiguration is a list of authenticators to authenticate Kubernetes users using JWT compliant tokens.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the requirement for the authorizers field in AuthorizationConfiguration?
response: The authorizers field in AuthorizationConfiguration is required and must be an ordered list of at least one authorizer to authorize requests against.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is the relationship between JWT authenticators and other authenticators in AuthenticationConfiguration?
response: The specific position of JWT authenticators in relation to other authenticators is neither defined nor stable across releases. However, for an incoming token, each JWT authenticator will be attempted in the order in which it is specified in the list.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1beta1)

instruction: What is a custom resource in Kubernetes?
response: A custom resource is an extension of the Kubernetes API that is not necessarily available in a default Kubernetes installation. It represents a customization of a particular Kubernetes installation.
title: Custom Resources

instruction: How can users interact with custom resources?
response: Once a custom resource is installed, users can create and access its objects using kubectl, just as they do for built-in resources like Pods.
title: Custom Resources

instruction: What is the benefit of combining a custom resource with a custom controller?
response: When you combine a custom resource with a custom controller, custom resources provide a true declarative API. The Kubernetes declarative API enforces a separation of responsibilities. You declare the desired state of your resource. The Kubernetes controller keeps the current state of Kubernetes objects in sync with your declared desired state.
title: Custom Resources

instruction: When should I add a custom resource to my Kubernetes cluster?
response: Consider adding a custom resource to your Kubernetes cluster if your API is declarative, you want your new types to be readable and writable using kubectl, and you want to aggregate your API with the Kubernetes cluster APIs.
title: Custom Resources

instruction: What is the Operator pattern in Kubernetes?
response: The Operator pattern combines custom resources and custom controllers. You can use custom controllers to encode domain knowledge for specific applications into an extension of the Kubernetes API.
title: Custom Resources

instruction: What is the purpose of a PodGroup in Kubernetes?
response: A PodGroup represents a runtime instance of pods grouped together, created by workload controllers from Workload.podGroupTemplates.
title: PodGroup

instruction: How is the PodGroup API enabled or disabled?
response: The PodGroup API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.
title: PodGroup

instruction: What are the possible disruption modes for a PodGroup?
response: The possible disruption modes for a PodGroup are 'Pod' and 'PodGroup'. 'Pod' means individual pods can be disrupted or preempted independently, while 'PodGroup' means the whole PodGroup needs to be disrupted or preempted together.
title: PodGroup

instruction: What is the purpose of the disruptionMode field in PodGroupSpec?
response: The disruptionMode field defines the mode in which a given PodGroup can be disrupted. It is immutable and defaults to 'Pod' if unset.
title: PodGroup

instruction: What is the significance of the priority field in PodGroupSpec?
response: The priority field represents the priority value of the pod group, influencing various system behaviors.
title: PodGroup

instruction: How can I replace a pod using the data in a file named pod.json?
response: You can replace a pod using the data in pod.json by running the command: kubectl replace -f ./pod.json
title: kubectl replace

instruction: What is the purpose of the --force flag in kubectl replace?
response: The --force flag in kubectl replace immediately removes resources from API and bypasses graceful deletion. Note that immediate deletion of some resources may result in inconsistency or data loss and requires confirmation.
title: kubectl replace

instruction: How can I update a single-container pod's image version to v4?
response: You can update a single-container pod's image version to v4 by running the command: kubectl get pod mypod -o yaml | sed 's/\(image: myimage\):.*$/\1:v4/' | kubectl replace -f -
title: kubectl replace

instruction: What is the default behavior of the --dry-run flag in kubectl replace?
response: The default behavior of the --dry-run flag in kubectl replace is 'none', which means the command will not print the object that would be sent, nor will it send it. You can set it to 'server' or 'client' to test the request.
title: kubectl replace

instruction: Can I process a kustomization directory with kubectl replace?
response: Yes, you can process a kustomization directory with kubectl replace by using the -k or --kustomize flag, but it cannot be used together with -f or -R flags.
title: kubectl replace

instruction: What is the uniqueness scope for a Kubernetes object's Name?
response: A Kubernetes object's Name is unique within its type of resource in a given namespace. However, it can be duplicated across different types of resources or namespaces.
title: Object Names and IDs

instruction: Can two objects with the same name and resource type be created in the same namespace using different API versions?
response: No, you cannot create two objects with the same name and resource type in the same namespace, even using different API versions, as the API version is not part of the unique identification.
title: Object Names and IDs

instruction: What are the constraints for a Kubernetes object's Name that follows DNS Subdomain Names?
response: A Kubernetes object's Name following DNS Subdomain Names must: contain no more than 253 characters, contain only lowercase alphanumeric characters, '-', or '.', start with an alphanumeric character, and end with an alphanumeric character.
title: Object Names and IDs

instruction: What happens if an object is deleted and a new object with the same name is created?
response: If an object is deleted, you can create a new object with the same name. The uniqueness of the name is only enforced within the existence of objects.
title: Object Names and IDs

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle name generation when `generateName` is provided in a resource create request?
response: When `generateName` is provided, the server uses it as a prefix and appends a generated suffix. The server makes up to 8 attempts to generate a unique name before returning an HTTP 409 response if conflicts occur.
title: Object Names and IDs

instruction: What is the purpose of the ObjectReference object in Kubernetes?
response: ObjectReference contains enough information to let you inspect or modify the referred object.
title: ObjectReference

instruction: What is the significance of the 'uid' field in an ObjectReference?
response: The 'uid' field represents the UID of the referent. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names/#uids
title: ObjectReference

instruction: How do you specify a reference to a container within a pod using the 'fieldPath' field?
response: You can specify a reference to a container within a pod using a valid JSON/Go field access statement, such as 'spec.containers{name}' or 'spec.containers[2]'.
title: ObjectReference

instruction: What is the 'resourceVersion' field used for in an ObjectReference?
response: The 'resourceVersion' field represents a specific resourceVersion to which this reference is made, if any. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#concurrency-control-and-consistency
title: ObjectReference

instruction: What information does the 'kind' field in an ObjectReference provide?
response: The 'kind' field represents the Kind of the referent. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds
title: ObjectReference

instruction: What is the purpose of Pod conditions in Kubernetes?
response: Pod conditions are markers for some aspect of the actual state of a Pod, allowing controllers and people doing troubleshooting to understand the health of a Pod. They complement the phase by tracking multiple aspects of the Pod's state independently.
title: Pod Conditions

instruction: What are the possible values for the status field of a Pod condition?
response: The possible values for the status field of a Pod condition are "True", "False", or "Unknown".
title: Pod Conditions

instruction: What is the Pod condition that indicates the Pod has been scheduled to a node?
response: The Pod condition that indicates the Pod has been scheduled to a node is PodScheduled.
title: Pod Conditions

instruction: Can you define custom Pod conditions in Kubernetes?
response: Yes, in addition to the built-in conditions, you can define custom conditions using Pod readiness gates.
title: Pod Conditions

instruction: What is the difference between a Pod's phase and its conditions?
response: A Pod's phase provides a high-level summary of where the Pod is in its lifecycle, but a single value cannot capture the full picture. Pod conditions track multiple aspects of the Pod's state independently, such as whether it has been scheduled, whether its containers are ready, and more.
title: Pod Conditions

instruction: What is the purpose of MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding in Kubernetes?
response: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the MutatingAdmissionPolicy with parametrized resources and defines how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.
title: Admissionregistration

instruction: How many times is a policy evaluated for a given admission request when using params?
response: For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding.
title: Admissionregistration

instruction: What is the main difference between MutatingAdmissionPolicy and ValidatingAdmissionPolicy?
response: MutatingAdmissionPolicy mutates the object coming into the admission chain, while ValidatingAdmissionPolicy accepts or rejects an object without changing it.
title: Admissionregistration

instruction: What is the CEL budget constraint for policy evaluations?
response: The CEL expressions of a policy must have a computed CEL cost below the maximum CEL budget. Each evaluation of the policy is given an independent CEL cost budget.
title: Admissionregistration

instruction: Can adding or removing policies, bindings, or params affect the CEL budget of a policy?
response: No, adding/removing policies, bindings, or params cannot affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.
title: Admissionregistration

instruction: How do I unset the current-context in a kubeconfig file using kubectl?
response: You can unset the current-context in a kubeconfig file using the command: kubectl config unset current-context
title: kubectl config unset

instruction: What is the syntax to unset an individual value in a kubeconfig file?
response: The syntax to unset an individual value in a kubeconfig file is: kubectl config unset PROPERTY_NAME, where PROPERTY_NAME is a dot delimited name.
title: kubectl config unset

instruction: Can I use kubectl config unset to remove a namespace from a specific context?
response: Yes, you can use kubectl config unset to remove a namespace from a specific context. For example, to unset the namespace in the 'foo' context, use the command: kubectl config unset contexts.foo.namespace
title: kubectl config unset

instruction: What are some of the parent options that can be used with kubectl config unset?
response: Some of the parent options that can be used with kubectl config unset include --as, --as-group, --as-uid, --as-user-extra, --cache-dir, --certificate-authority, --client-certificate, --client-key, --cluster, --context, --kubeconfig, and others.
title: kubectl config unset

instruction: How do I specify a particular kubeconfig file to use with kubectl config unset?
response: You can specify a particular kubeconfig file to use with kubectl config unset by using the --kubeconfig option, followed by the path to the kubeconfig file.
title: kubectl config unset

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes version required to reconfigure the default Service IP range?
response: Kubernetes v1.33
title: Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration

instruction: How are the IP families available for Service ClusterIPs determined in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The IP families available for Service ClusterIPs are determined by the --service-cluster-ip-range flag to kube-apiserver.
title: Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration

instruction: What is the name of the ServiceCIDR object created by the first kube-apiserver instance that starts?
response: The ServiceCIDR object is named 'kubernetes'.
title: Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration

instruction: Can extending the existing ServiceCIDRs be done dynamically?
response: Yes, extending the existing ServiceCIDRs can be done dynamically by adding new ServiceCIDR objects without the need for reconfiguring the kube-apiserver.
title: Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration

instruction: What is required for single-to-dual-stack conversion while preserving the primary ServiceCIDR?
response: Single-to-dual-stack conversion preserving the primary ServiceCIDR requires an update to the kube-apiserver configuration and a corresponding modification of various cluster components that need to handle this additional IP family.
title: Kubernetes Default ServiceCIDR Reconfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of Node Declared Features in Kubernetes?
response: Node Declared Features helps manage version skew and improve cluster stability, especially during cluster upgrades or in mixed-version environments where nodes might not all have the same features enabled.
title: Node Declared Features

instruction: How does the kubelet report features on a node?
response: At startup, the kubelet on each node detects which managed Kubernetes features are currently enabled and reports them in the .status.declaredFeatures field of the Node.
title: Node Declared Features

instruction: What is the role of the NodeDeclaredFeatures plugin in the kube-scheduler?
response: The NodeDeclaredFeatures plugin ensures pods are only placed on nodes that explicitly support the features the pod requires. It checks the PodSpec to infer the set of node features required by the pod and then checks if the features listed in the node's .status.declaredFeatures satisfy the requirements inferred for the Pod.
title: Node Declared Features

instruction: How can custom schedulers utilize Node Declared Features?
response: Custom schedulers can utilize the .status.declaredFeatures field to enforce similar constraints as the default kube-scheduler.
title: Node Declared Features

instruction: What needs to be enabled to use Node Declared Features?
response: To use Node Declared Features, the NodeDeclaredFeatures feature gate must be enabled on the kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, and kubelet components.
title: Node Declared Features

instruction: What is the default disruption mode for a PodGroup in Kubernetes?
response: The default disruption mode for a PodGroup is 'Pod', which instructs the scheduler to treat all Pods in the group as separate entities, allowing independent disruption of a single pod from a PodGroup.
title: Pod Group Disruption and Priority

instruction: How is the priority of a PodGroup determined if no priorityClassName is set?
response: If no priorityClassName is set for a PodGroup, Kubernetes looks for a default PriorityClass with globalDefault set to true. If no such PriorityClass exists, the PodGroup has a priority of zero.
title: Pod Group Disruption and Priority

instruction: What is the effect of setting the disruption mode to 'PodGroup'?
response: When the disruption mode is set to 'PodGroup', it instructs the scheduler that all pods from the PodGroup have to be disrupted together, emphasizing 'all-or-nothing' semantics for disruption.
title: Pod Group Disruption and Priority

instruction: How does the priority of a PodGroup affect its pods during workload-aware preemption events?
response: The priority of the PodGroup is an authoritative priority for all pods in the group during workload-aware preemption events, even when priorities of individual pods forming this PodGroup differ.
title: Pod Group Disruption and Priority

instruction: What happens if the specified priorityClassName for a PodGroup is not found?
response: If the specified priorityClassName for a PodGroup is not found, the PodGroup is rejected.
title: Pod Group Disruption and Priority

instruction: What are the valid operators for a FieldSelectorRequirement?
response: The valid operators for a FieldSelectorRequirement are In, NotIn, Exists, and DoesNotExist.
title: FieldSelectorRequirement

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'key' field in a FieldSelectorRequirement?
response: The 'key' field in a FieldSelectorRequirement is the field selector key that the requirement applies to.
title: FieldSelectorRequirement

instruction: Can the 'values' array be empty for all operators in a FieldSelectorRequirement?
response: No, the 'values' array cannot be empty for all operators. It must be non-empty if the operator is In or NotIn, and it must be empty if the operator is Exists or DoesNotExist.
title: FieldSelectorRequirement

instruction: What type of data is the 'values' field in a FieldSelectorRequirement?
response: The 'values' field in a FieldSelectorRequirement is an array of string values.
title: FieldSelectorRequirement

instruction: Is it possible for the list of operators in a FieldSelectorRequirement to grow in the future?
response: Yes, according to the documentation, the list of operators may grow in the future.
title: FieldSelectorRequirement

instruction: Where should I ask a specific, answerable question about how to use Kubernetes?
response: You should ask it on Stack Overflow.
title: Kubernetes Issues and Security

instruction: How do I report a problem or suggest an improvement in Kubernetes?
response: You can open an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Kubernetes Issues and Security

instruction: What is the official feed for Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) in Kubernetes?
response: The official CVE feed.
title: Kubernetes Issues and Security

instruction: Where can I find information about Kubernetes security and disclosure?
response: Kubernetes Security and Disclosure Information.
title: Kubernetes Issues and Security

instruction: What should I do if I want to provide feedback on the Kubernetes documentation page?
response: You can click on 'Yes' or 'No' on the page to provide feedback, or ask a question on Stack Overflow, or open an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Kubernetes Issues and Security

instruction: What is the purpose of the Coordinated Leader Election feature in Kubernetes?
response: The Coordinated Leader Election feature allows control plane components to deterministically select a leader, which is useful for satisfying Kubernetes version skew constraints during cluster upgrades.
title: Coordinated Leader Election

instruction: How do I enable the Coordinated Leader Election feature when starting the API Server?
response: To enable the Coordinated Leader Election feature, you need to set the flags `--feature-gates="CoordinatedLeaderElection=true"` and `--runtime-config="coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1=true"` when starting the API Server.
title: Coordinated Leader Election

instruction: Which control plane components automatically use coordinated leader election in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: In Kubernetes 1.36, two control plane components, kube-controller-manager and kube-scheduler, automatically use coordinated leader election when the feature gate and API group are enabled.
title: Coordinated Leader Election

instruction: What is the purpose of the Lease API in Kubernetes leader election?
response: The Lease API acts as a lightweight distributed lock, stored by the Kubernetes API server, to perform leader election among multiple instances of the same control-plane component in a high-availability cluster.
title: Coordinated Leader Election

instruction: What happens when the Lease object does not exist or has expired in Kubernetes leader election?
response: When the Lease object does not exist or has expired (current time > renewTime + leaseDurationSeconds), candidate instances consider the leadership to be available and may attempt to acquire the lease.
title: Coordinated Leader Election

instruction: What are the two broad categories of multi-tenancy in Kubernetes clusters?
response: The two broad categories of multi-tenancy in Kubernetes clusters are: multiple teams and multiple customers. Multiple teams involve sharing a cluster between multiple teams within an organization, while multiple customers involve a Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) vendor running multiple instances of a workload for customers.
title: Multi-tenancy

instruction: What are some essential Kubernetes policies for safely and fairly sharing clusters between multiple teams?
response: Kubernetes policies such as RBAC, quotas, and network policies are essential to safely and fairly share clusters between multiple teams.
title: Multi-tenancy

instruction: Do customers have direct access to the Kubernetes cluster in a multi-customer tenancy scenario?
response: No, in a multi-customer tenancy scenario, the customers do not have access to the cluster; Kubernetes is invisible from their perspective and is only used by the vendor to manage the workloads.
title: Multi-tenancy

instruction: What is a common concern for SaaS vendors running multiple instances of a workload for customers?
response: Cost optimization is frequently a critical concern for SaaS vendors running multiple instances of a workload for customers.
title: Multi-tenancy

instruction: How do team members typically access Kubernetes resources in a multi-team tenancy scenario?
response: Team members often have direct access to Kubernetes resources via tools such as kubectl, or indirect access through GitOps controllers or other types of release automation tools.
title: Multi-tenancy

instruction: What are the key considerations for setting up a production-ready Kubernetes cluster?
response: A production-quality Kubernetes cluster requires planning and preparation, and must be configured to be resilient. Key considerations include availability, scale, security, and access management.
title: Production environment

instruction: How can you ensure high availability in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To ensure high availability, consider separating the control plane from the worker nodes, replicating control plane components on multiple nodes, load balancing traffic to the cluster's API server, and having enough worker nodes available or able to quickly become available as changing workloads warrant it.
title: Production environment

instruction: What is the purpose of role-based access control (RBAC) in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: RBAC is used to refine access to cluster resources, ensuring that users and workloads can access the resources they need while keeping workloads and the cluster itself secure.
title: Production environment

instruction: How can you manage resources for users and workloads in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can manage resources by setting limits on the resources that users and workloads can access through managing policies and container resources.
title: Production environment

instruction: What are the main differences between a production Kubernetes environment and a personal learning or development environment?
response: A production Kubernetes environment has more requirements than a personal learning, development, or test environment, including secure access by many users, consistent availability, and resources to adapt to changing demands.
title: Production environment

instruction: How can I verify the location and credentials that kubectl knows about?
response: You can use the command `kubectl config view` to check the location and credentials that kubectl knows about.
title: Accessing Clusters

instruction: What is the recommended approach to directly access the REST API with an http client?
response: The recommended approach is to run kubectl in proxy mode using the command `kubectl proxy --port=8080`. This handles locating the apiserver and authenticating.
title: Accessing Clusters

instruction: How can I create a token for the default service account?
response: You can create a token for the default service account by creating a Secret and requesting a token. First, create the Secret using `kubectl apply -f - <<EOF ... EOF`. Then, wait for the token controller to populate the Secret with a token using `while ! kubectl describe secret default-token | grep -E 'Token|Ca'; do sleep 1; done`.
title: Accessing Clusters

instruction: What are the benefits of using kubectl proxy mode?
response: Using kubectl proxy mode provides several benefits, including verifying the identity of the apiserver using a self-signed certificate, authenticating to the apiserver, and potentially doing intelligent client-side load-balancing and failover in the future.
title: Accessing Clusters

instruction: How can I access the API using curl after running kubectl proxy?
response: After running `kubectl proxy --port=8080`, you can access the API using curl by visiting `http://localhost:8080/api/`. You can replace `localhost` with `[::1]` for IPv6.
title: Accessing Clusters

instruction: What is the recommended cgroup driver for kubeadm-based setups?
response: The systemd driver is recommended for kubeadm-based setups instead of the kubelet's default cgroupfs driver, because kubeadm manages the kubelet as a systemd service.
title: Configuring a cgroup driver

instruction: How can I configure the kubelet's cgroup driver to match the container runtime cgroup driver for kubeadm clusters?
response: You can pass a KubeletConfiguration structure during kubeadm init, which includes the cgroupDriver field that controls the cgroup driver of the kubelet.
title: Configuring a cgroup driver

instruction: What is the default cgroup driver in Kubernetes v1.22 and later if the user does not set the cgroupDriver field?
response: In v1.22 and later, if the user does not set the cgroupDriver field under KubeletConfiguration, kubeadm defaults it to systemd.
title: Configuring a cgroup driver

instruction: How can I prevent kubeadm upgrade from modifying the KubeletConfiguration cgroup driver on existing setups?
response: To use cgroupfs and to prevent kubeadm upgrade from modifying the KubeletConfiguration cgroup driver on existing setups, you must be explicit about its value.
title: Configuring a cgroup driver

instruction: Where is the KubeletConfiguration stored in a kubeadm cluster?
response: The KubeletConfiguration is stored in a ConfigMap object under the kube-system namespace, and also written as a file under /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml on each node.
title: Configuring a cgroup driver

instruction: What is the purpose of owner references in Kubernetes object specifications?
response: Owner references help different parts of Kubernetes avoid interfering with objects they don’t control. They allow Kubernetes to determine which objects are dependents of an owner object and prevent unauthorized users from delaying owner object deletion.
title: Owners and Dependents

instruction: How does Kubernetes set the metadata.ownerReferences field for dependent objects?
response: Kubernetes sets the value of this field automatically for objects that are dependents of other objects like ReplicaSets, DaemonSets, Deployments, Jobs and CronJobs, and ReplicationControllers.
title: Owners and Dependents

instruction: What is the effect of setting metadata.ownerReferences.blockOwnerDeletion to true for a dependent object?
response: If a controller sets the value of the metadata.ownerReferences field, Kubernetes automatically sets this field to true, which blocks garbage collection from deleting the owner object if the dependent object exists.
title: Owners and Dependents

instruction: Can a namespaced dependent object specify a cluster-scoped owner?
response: Yes, namespaced dependents can specify cluster-scoped or namespaced owners.
title: Owners and Dependents

instruction: What happens if a namespaced owner object does not exist in the same namespace as its dependent object?
response: If a namespaced owner does not exist in the same namespace as the dependent, the owner reference is treated as absent, and the dependent is subject to deletion once all owners are verified absent.
title: Owners and Dependents

instruction: What is the first line of defense for controlling and limiting access to a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Controlling and limiting who can access the cluster and what actions they are allowed to perform through the Kubernetes API.
title: Securing a Cluster

instruction: What is the recommended way to secure API traffic in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Use Transport Layer Security (TLS) for all API traffic. Kubernetes expects that all API communication in the cluster is encrypted by default with TLS.
title: Securing a Cluster

instruction: How do API clients, such as nodes, proxies, and volume plugins, authenticate with the API server?
response: These clients are typically service accounts or use x509 client certificates, and they are created automatically at cluster startup or are setup as part of the cluster installation.
title: Securing a Cluster

instruction: What authorization component does Kubernetes provide to control access to cluster resources?
response: Kubernetes provides an integrated Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) component that matches an incoming user or group to a set of permissions bundled into roles.
title: Securing a Cluster

instruction: What information can be found in the authentication reference document?
response: The authentication reference document provides more information on authentication mechanisms for the API servers.
title: Securing a Cluster

instruction: What is a requirement for using kubectl cp to copy files to and from containers?
response: The 'tar' binary must be present in your container image. If 'tar' is not present, 'kubectl cp' will fail.
title: kubectl cp

instruction: How do you copy a local file /tmp/foo to /tmp/bar in a remote pod in namespace <some-namespace> using kubectl cp?
response: kubectl cp /tmp/foo <some-namespace>/<some-pod>:/tmp/bar
title: kubectl cp

instruction: What is the purpose of the --no-preserve option in kubectl cp?
response: The --no-preserve option prevents the copied file/directory's ownership and permissions from being preserved in the container.
title: kubectl cp

instruction: How can you specify a container to use when copying files to and from a pod with multiple containers using kubectl cp?
response: You can specify a container using the -c or --container option followed by the container name.
title: kubectl cp

instruction: What happens if the --retries option is set to 0 in kubectl cp?
response: If the --retries option is set to 0, no retries will be attempted to complete a copy operation from a container.
title: kubectl cp

instruction: How do I view resources found in a directory containing a kustomization file?
response: You can view resources found in a directory containing a kustomization file by running the command: kubectl kustomize <kustomization_directory>
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize

instruction: What is Kustomize and what does it do?
response: Kustomize is a standalone tool to customize Kubernetes objects through a kustomization file. It has features to manage application configuration files, including generating resources from other sources, setting cross-cutting fields for resources, and composing and customizing collections of resources.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize

instruction: How do I generate a ConfigMap from a file using Kustomize?
response: To generate a ConfigMap from a file, add an entry to the files list in configMapGenerator in the kustomization file. For example, you can create a ConfigMap with a data item from a .properties file by specifying the file in the configMapGenerator section of the kustomization file.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize

instruction: What command do I use to apply resources generated by Kustomize?
response: To apply resources generated by Kustomize, run kubectl apply with the --kustomize or -k flag: kubectl apply -k <kustomization_directory>
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize

instruction: Can I use Kustomize with kubectl version prior to 1.14?
response: No, since 1.14, kubectl also supports the management of Kubernetes objects using a kustomization file, implying that earlier versions do not have this support.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize

instruction: What is the maximum number of nodes supported by Kubernetes v1.36?
response: Kubernetes v1.36 supports clusters with up to 5,000 nodes.
title: Considerations for large clusters

instruction: What are some cloud provider resource quotas to consider when creating a large cluster?
response: When creating a cluster with many nodes, consider requesting a quota increase for cloud resources such as computer instances, CPUs, storage volumes, in-use IP addresses, packet filtering rule sets, number of load balancers, network subnets, and log streams.
title: Considerations for large clusters

instruction: How should control plane components be deployed in a large cluster?
response: For a large cluster, you need a control plane with sufficient compute and other resources. Typically, you would run one or two control plane instances per failure zone, scaling those instances vertically first and then scaling horizontally after reaching the point of falling returns to (vertical) scale.
title: Considerations for large clusters

instruction: Why is it recommended to run multiple control plane hosts in each failure zone?
response: Running multiple control plane hosts in each zone makes it less likely that all control-plane traffic for nodes in a zone will be sent between zones if a single control-plane host or endpoint failure zone goes offline.
title: Considerations for large clusters

instruction: How can the performance of large clusters be improved with etcd storage?
response: To improve performance of large clusters, you can store Event objects in a separate dedicated etcd instance. When creating a cluster, you can start and configure an additional etcd instance and configure the API server to use it for storing events.
title: Considerations for large clusters

instruction: What is the purpose of a StatefulSet in Kubernetes?
response: A StatefulSet is used to manage stateful applications, which require persistent storage and have specific ordering and uniqueness requirements.
title: Stateful Applications

instruction: Can you provide an example of deploying a stateful application with Persistent Volumes in Kubernetes?
response: Yes, you can deploy WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes as an example of a stateful application in Kubernetes.
title: Stateful Applications

instruction: How does a StatefulSet handle network identities for its pods?
response: A StatefulSet uses a Pod's hostname to assign a network identity to each Pod, and the hostname follows the pattern: $(podname).$(headless-service-name).$(namespace).svc.$(cluster-domain).
title: Stateful Applications

instruction: What are some examples of applications that can be deployed using a StatefulSet?
response: Examples of applications that can be deployed using a StatefulSet include Cassandra, ZooKeeper, and MySQL.
title: Stateful Applications

instruction: Is it possible to use a StatefulSet to deploy a distributed system coordinator like ZooKeeper?
response: Yes, it is possible to use a StatefulSet to deploy a distributed system coordinator like ZooKeeper, which requires stable network identities and persistent storage.
title: Stateful Applications

instruction: How do I list all available kubectl plugins?
response: You can list all available plugins using the command 'kubectl plugin list'.
title: kubectl plugin

instruction: How do I list only binary names of available plugins without paths?
response: You can list only binary names of available plugins without paths by using the command 'kubectl plugin list --name-only'.
title: kubectl plugin

instruction: What is the recommended way to discover and install kubectl plugins?
response: The easiest way to discover and install plugins is via the kubernetes sub-project krew: [krew.sigs.k8s.io]. To install krew, visit https://krew.sigs.k8s.io/docs/user-guide/setup/install.
title: kubectl plugin

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl plugin command?
response: The kubectl plugin command provides utilities for interacting with plugins. Plugins provide extended functionality that is not part of the major command-line distribution.
title: kubectl plugin

instruction: How do I get help for the kubectl plugin command?
response: You can get help for the kubectl plugin command by using the flag '-h, --help'.
title: kubectl plugin

instruction: What is the purpose of SelfSubjectAccessReview in Kubernetes?
response: SelfSubjectAccessReview checks whether the current user can perform an action. It allows users to check their access rights.
title: SelfSubjectAccessReview

instruction: What does not filling in a spec.namespace in SelfSubjectAccessReview imply?
response: Not filling in a spec.namespace means 'in all namespaces'.
title: SelfSubjectAccessReview

instruction: What are the required fields in SelfSubjectAccessReviewSpec?
response: Exactly one of resourceAttributes and nonResourceAttributes must be set. The user and groups fields must be empty.
title: SelfSubjectAccessReview

instruction: How is the status of a SelfSubjectAccessReview request determined?
response: The status is filled in by the server and indicates whether the request is allowed or not.
title: SelfSubjectAccessReview

instruction: Can a user check their own access rights using SelfSubjectAccessReview?
response: Yes, Self is a special case, because users should always be able to check whether they can perform an action.
title: SelfSubjectAccessReview

instruction: What is the purpose of a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy in Kubernetes?
response: A ValidatingAdmissionPolicy describes the definition of an admission validation policy that accepts or rejects an object without changing it.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

instruction: What is the significance of the failurePolicy field in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicySpec?
response: The failurePolicy field defines how to handle failures for the admission policy. Failures can occur from CEL expression parse errors, type check errors, runtime errors and invalid or mis-configured policy definitions or bindings.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

instruction: What are the requirements for the auditAnnotations and validations fields in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicySpec?
response: At least one of validations or auditAnnotations is required; validations and auditAnnotations may not both be empty.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

instruction: Is the status field in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy modifiable?
response: No, the status field represents the current status of the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy and is populated by the system. It is read-only.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

instruction: What is the purpose of the auditAnnotations field in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicySpec?
response: The auditAnnotations field contains CEL expressions which are used to produce audit annotations for the audit event of the API request.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

instruction: How can a Go client library be configured to access the Kubernetes API from within a Pod?
response: The rest.InClusterConfig() function handles API host discovery and authentication automatically.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod

instruction: What environment variables can be used by a container to construct an HTTPS URL for the Kubernetes API server?
response: KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST and KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT_HTTPS.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod

instruction: What is the recommended way to authenticate to the API server from within a Pod?
response: The recommended way to authenticate to the API server is with a service account credential.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod

instruction: How do official client libraries handle API host discovery and authentication?
response: Official client libraries can automatically discover the API server and authenticate. For example, the Python client library uses the config.load_incluster_config() function, while the Go client library uses the rest.InClusterConfig() function.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod

instruction: What is the DNS name that can be used by pods to reference the local API server?
response: kubernetes.default.svc
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod

instruction: What is the purpose of the Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer?
response: The aggregation layer allows Kubernetes to be extended with additional APIs, beyond what is offered by the core Kubernetes APIs.
title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer

instruction: How does the aggregation layer register an API?
response: To register an API, you add an APIService object, which 'claims' the URL path in the Kubernetes API.
title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer

instruction: What is the typical architecture for implementing an extension API server?
response: The extension API server is typically run in Pod(s) that run in your cluster, paired with one or more controllers.
title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer

instruction: What is the latency requirement for discovery requests in the aggregation layer?
response: Discovery requests are required to round-trip from the kube-apiserver in five seconds or less.
title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer

instruction: How does the aggregation layer handle requests to the registered API path?
response: The aggregation layer will proxy anything sent to the registered API path to the registered APIService.
title: Kubernetes API Aggregation Layer

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'AllNodes' field in BasicDevice?
response: The 'AllNodes' field indicates that all nodes have access to the device. It must only be set if Spec.PerDeviceNodeSelection is set to true. At most one of NodeName, NodeSelector and AllNodes can be set.
title: BasicDevice

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'AllowMultipleAllocations' to true in BasicDevice?
response: If 'AllowMultipleAllocations' is set to true, the device can be allocated more than once, and all of its capacity is consumable, regardless of whether the requestPolicy is defined or not.
title: BasicDevice

instruction: What are the constraints on the 'attributes' and 'capacity' fields in BasicDevice?
response: The 'attributes' and 'capacity' fields define sets of attributes and capacities for the device. The name of each attribute and capacity must be unique in their respective sets. The maximum number of attributes and capacities combined is 32.
title: BasicDevice

instruction: What is the purpose of 'bindingConditions' and 'bindingFailureConditions' in BasicDevice?
response: The 'bindingConditions' field defines conditions that must be set to true in the per-device status conditions to proceed with binding the pod to the node. The 'bindingFailureConditions' field defines conditions that, if any are true, indicate a binding failure occurred. Both fields are beta and require specific feature gates to be enabled.
title: BasicDevice

instruction: What does the 'bindsToNode' field control in BasicDevice?
response: The 'bindsToNode' field indicates if the usage of an allocation involving this device must be limited to exactly the node that was chosen when allocating the claim. If set to true, the scheduler will set the ResourceClaim.Status.Allocation.NodeSelector to match the node where the allocation was made. This field is beta and requires enabling specific feature gates.
title: BasicDevice

instruction: How do I enable kubectl shell completion for bash on macOS?
response: For macOS, you can enable kubectl shell completion for bash by sourcing the completion code from the .bash_profile. Detailed instructions are available at https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl-macos/#enable-shell-autocompletion. You can also install bash completion using homebrew and then run 'kubectl completion bash > $(brew --prefix)/etc/bash_completion.d/kubectl'.
title: kubectl completion

instruction: What are the supported shells for kubectl completion?
response: The supported shells for kubectl completion are bash, zsh, fish, and powershell.
title: kubectl completion

instruction: How do I load kubectl completion code for zsh into the current shell?
response: You can load the kubectl completion code for zsh into the current shell by running 'source <(kubectl completion zsh)'. Note that zsh completions are only supported in versions of zsh >= 5.2.
title: kubectl completion

instruction: How do I set up kubectl completion for fish to load completions for each session?
response: To load completions for each session in fish, execute once: 'kubectl completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/kubectl.fish'.
title: kubectl completion

instruction: How do I load kubectl completion code for powershell into the current shell?
response: You can load the kubectl completion code for powershell into the current shell by running 'kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression'.
title: kubectl completion

instruction: What is the principle of least privilege in Kubernetes RBAC?
response: The principle of least privilege in Kubernetes RBAC is to assign minimal RBAC rights to users and service accounts, only providing permissions that are explicitly required for their operation.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices

instruction: Why should administrators avoid using cluster-admin accounts?
response: Administrators should avoid using cluster-admin accounts except where specifically needed, as these accounts have unrestricted superuser access which can lead to security incidents if misused.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices

instruction: What are the risks of providing wildcard permissions in Kubernetes RBAC?
response: Providing wildcard permissions gives rights not just to all object types that currently exist in the cluster, but also to all object types which are created in the future, potentially leading to excessive access.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices

instruction: Why should users not be added to the system:masters group?
response: Users who are members of the system:masters group bypass all RBAC rights checks and will always have unrestricted superuser access, which cannot be revoked by removing RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices

instruction: How can you limit the risks associated with running powerful pods in Kubernetes?
response: To limit the risks, consider limiting the number of nodes running powerful pods, ensuring that any DaemonSets are necessary and run with least privilege, and avoiding running powerful pods alongside untrusted pods.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices

instruction: What information does the Node Reference Information section in Kubernetes documentation cover?
response: The Node Reference Information section covers various topics about nodes, including the kubelet's sync loop, checkpoint API, Pod Info gRPC API, dockershim removal, CRI-compatible runtimes, Kubelet Device Manager API versions, node labels, local files and paths used by the kubelet, node status information, Linux node swap behaviors, seccomp information, and what happens after a node restart.
title: Node Reference Information

instruction: Where else can node reference details be found in the Kubernetes documentation?
response: Node reference details can also be found in other parts of the Kubernetes documentation, including Node Metrics Data, CRI Pod & Container Metrics, and Understand Pressure Stall Information (PSI) Metrics.
title: Node Reference Information

instruction: What should I do if I have a specific, answerable question about using Kubernetes?
response: If you have a specific, answerable question about how to use Kubernetes, you should ask it on Stack Overflow.
title: Node Reference Information

instruction: How can I report a problem or suggest an improvement to the Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement to the Kubernetes documentation by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Node Reference Information

instruction: What happened to the 'what happens on restart page' in the Kubernetes documentation?
response: The 'what happens on restart page' was updated on June 26, 2016, at 4:16 PM PST, with the update noted as 'what happens on restart page (fa9e2b43f1)'.
title: Node Reference Information

instruction: What is the default diff command used by kubectl diff?
response: The default diff command used by kubectl diff is the "diff" command available in your path, run with the "-u" (unified diff) and "-N" (treat absent files as empty) options.
title: kubectl diff

instruction: How can you specify a custom diff command for kubectl diff?
response: You can specify a custom diff command for kubectl diff by setting the KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF environment variable. For example, you can set it to "colordiff -N -u" to use the colordiff command.
title: kubectl diff

instruction: What is the exit status of kubectl diff when no differences are found?
response: The exit status of kubectl diff when no differences are found is 0.
title: kubectl diff

instruction: What is the purpose of the --concurrency option in kubectl diff?
response: The --concurrency option in kubectl diff specifies the number of objects to process in parallel when diffing against the live version. A larger number means faster processing, but also more memory, I/O, and CPU usage.
title: kubectl diff

instruction: How can you include managed fields in the diff output of kubectl diff?
response: You can include managed fields in the diff output of kubectl diff by setting the --show-managed-fields option to true.
title: kubectl diff

instruction: What is the primary purpose of a Deployment in Kubernetes?
response: A Deployment manages a set of Pods to run an application workload, usually one that doesn't maintain state, and provides declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets.
title: Deployments

instruction: How do you update the state of Pods in a Deployment?
response: You declare the new state of the Pods by updating the PodTemplateSpec of the Deployment. A new ReplicaSet is created, and the Deployment gradually scales it up while scaling down the old ReplicaSet, ensuring Pods are replaced at a controlled rate.
title: Deployments

instruction: What happens when you rollback to an earlier Deployment revision?
response: Each rollback updates the revision of the Deployment, allowing you to return to a previous state of the Deployment if the current state is not stable.
title: Deployments

instruction: How do you create a Deployment that brings up three nginx Pods?
response: You create a Deployment YAML file, such as controllers/nginx-deployment.yaml, with the specified configuration, including apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec, and container details. An example configuration is provided, which includes a Deployment named nginx-deployment, three replicas, and an nginx image.
title: Deployments

instruction: What is the purpose of the selector field in a Deployment spec?
response: The .spec.selector field defines how the created ReplicaSet finds which Pods to manage. In the example, it selects Pods with the label app: nginx.
title: Deployments

instruction: When is the PostStart hook executed in a container's lifecycle?
response: The PostStart hook is executed immediately after a container is created. It runs concurrently with the container's ENTRYPOINT (main process), meaning the hook may run before, during, or after the main process starts.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks

instruction: What happens if the PreStop hook fails when a container is being terminated?
response: If the PreStop hook fails, the container will still terminate within the Pod's termination grace period. The hook must complete before the TERM signal to stop the container can be sent.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks

instruction: What are the types of hook handlers that can be implemented for Containers?
response: There are three types of hook handlers that can be implemented for Containers: Exec, HTTP, and (no third type mentioned).
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks

instruction: Can the PostStart hook delay container status updates?
response: Yes, the PostStart hook can delay container status updates; the container may not transition to Running until the hook completes.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks

instruction: What is the purpose of the StopSignal lifecycle hook?
response: The StopSignal lifecycle hook is used to define a stop signal which would be sent to the container when it is stopped, overriding any STOPSIGNAL instruction defined within the container image.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks

instruction: How do you use field selectors to get all Pods with the status.phase equal to Running?
response: You can use the kubectl command `kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running` to select all Pods for which the value of the status.phase field is Running.
title: Field Selectors

instruction: What are the supported field selectors for a Pod resource?
response: The supported field selectors for a Pod resource are: spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, spec.hostNetwork, status.phase, status.podIP, status.podIPs, and status.nominatedNodeName.
title: Field Selectors

instruction: Can you use field selectors with custom resources?
response: Yes, all custom resource types support the metadata.name and metadata.namespace fields. Additionally, the spec.versions[*].selectableFields field of a CustomResourceDefinition declares which other fields in a custom resource may be used in field selectors.
title: Field Selectors

instruction: What operators are supported with field selectors?
response: The supported operators with field selectors are =, ==, and !=. The = and == operators mean the same thing.
title: Field Selectors

instruction: How do you get all Services that are not in the default namespace?
response: You can use the kubectl command `kubectl get services --all-namespaces --field-selector metadata.namespace!=default` to select all Kubernetes Services that aren't in the default namespace.
title: Field Selectors

instruction: What is the default encryption behavior for API data at rest in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: By default, the API server uses an identity provider that stores plain-text representations of resources, which does not provide any confidentiality protection.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest

instruction: How can I check if encryption at rest is already enabled in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can check if encryption at rest is enabled by verifying if the kube-apiserver process is using an encryption provider configuration file, specified by the --encryption-provider-config argument. If it is not specified, then encryption at rest is not enabled.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest

instruction: What are the requirements for encrypting a custom resource in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To encrypt a custom resource, your cluster must be running Kubernetes v1.26 or newer.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest

instruction: Can I use the same encryption approach for data in filesystems mounted into containers?
response: No, to manage encryption for data in filesystems that are mounted into containers, you need to either use a storage integration that provides encrypted volumes or encrypt the data within your own application.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest

instruction: What is the format of the encryption provider configuration file used by the kube-apiserver?
response: The format of the encryption provider configuration file is YAML, representing a configuration API kind named Encryption.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest

instruction: What is required to pull a private image from a registry like Docker Hub in Kubernetes?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, the kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster, and you must authenticate with the registry using a tool like docker login.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry

instruction: How do you create a Secret to pull an image from a private registry if you have already run docker login?
response: You can create a Secret by copying the credential into Kubernetes using the command: kubectl create secret generic regcred --from-file=.dockerconfigjson=<path/to/.docker/config.json> --type=kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry

instruction: What type of Secret does a Kubernetes cluster use to authenticate with a container registry to pull a private image?
response: A Kubernetes cluster uses the Secret of kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson type to authenticate with a container registry to pull a private image.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry

instruction: What happens when you run docker login to authenticate with Docker Hub?
response: The login process creates or updates a config.json file that holds an authorization token. This file contains a section with an auth entry that holds the encoded authentication details.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry

instruction: If you use a Docker credentials store, how do you create a Secret in Kubernetes?
response: If you use a Docker credentials store, you won't see the auth entry in the config.json file but a credsStore entry with the name of the store as value. In that case, you can create a secret directly using the command line.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry

instruction: What is the format of a Bootstrap Token in Kubernetes?
response: Bootstrap Tokens take the form of abcdef.0123456789abcdef. More formally, they must match the regular expression [a-z0-9]{6}\.[a-z0-9]{16}.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens

instruction: How do you enable Bootstrap Token authentication on the API server?
response: The Bootstrap Token authenticator can be enabled using the flag --enable-bootstrap-token-auth on the API server.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens

instruction: What is the username that tokens authenticate as?
response: Tokens authenticate as the username system:bootstrap:<token id> and are members of the group system:bootstrappers.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens

instruction: How can expired tokens be deleted automatically?
response: Expired tokens can be deleted automatically by enabling the tokencleaner controller on the controller manager with the flag --controllers=*,tokencleaner.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens

instruction: In which namespace and with what type must Bootstrap Token secrets be created?
response: Bootstrap Token secrets must be created in the kube-system namespace with the type bootstrap.kubernetes.io/token.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens

instruction: What does the Troubleshooting Applications documentation cover?
response: The documentation covers common issues with Kubernetes resources like Pods, Services, or StatefulSets, advice on making sense of container termination messages, and ways to debug running containers.
title: Troubleshooting Applications

instruction: How can I get a shell to a running container?
response: The documentation provides information on how to get a shell to a running container under the section 'Get a Shell to a Running Container'.
title: Troubleshooting Applications

instruction: What should I do if I want to report a problem or suggest an improvement for the Kubernetes documentation?
response: You should open an issue in the GitHub Repository if you want to report a problem or suggest an improvement.
title: Troubleshooting Applications

instruction: What are some common Kubernetes resources that may have issues in containerized applications?
response: According to the documentation, common Kubernetes resources that may have issues include Pods, Services, and StatefulSets.
title: Troubleshooting Applications

instruction: Where can I ask a specific, answerable question about how to use Kubernetes?
response: You can ask a specific, answerable question about how to use Kubernetes on Stack Overflow.
title: Troubleshooting Applications

instruction: What is the purpose of declarative API validation in Kubernetes?
response: Declarative API validation allows Kubernetes developers and people extending the Kubernetes API to define validation rules directly alongside the API type definitions, ensuring that requests against the API are valid.
title: Declarative API Validation

instruction: How are validation rules defined in declarative API validation?
response: Validation rules are defined using special comment tags, such as +k8s:minimum=0, alongside the API type definitions in types.go files. A code generator, validation-gen, then uses these tags to produce optimized Go code for API validation.
title: Declarative API Validation

instruction: What is the purpose of the DeclarativeValidationBeta feature gate?
response: The DeclarativeValidationBeta feature gate controls the enforcement of beta-stage validation rules. When enabled, rules marked as +k8s:beta are authoritative; when disabled, they revert to shadow mode.
title: Declarative API Validation

instruction: What happens when DeclarativeValidationBeta is set to true, which is the default in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: With DeclarativeValidationBeta=true, both validation systems run for Alpha and shadowed rules. Beta rules are enforced. The results of the hand-written validation are used for Alpha rules, and the declarative validation runs in a mismatch mode for comparison.
title: Declarative API Validation

instruction: What happens when there is a mismatch between the hand-written validation and declarative validation results?
response: Mismatches between the two validation systems are logged by the API server and increment the declarative_validation_mismatch_total metric.
title: Declarative API Validation

instruction: What is the default expiration duration for certificates issued to the kubelet for authenticating to the Kubernetes API?
response: The default expiration duration for certificates issued to the kubelet for authenticating to the Kubernetes API is one year.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: How can I enable certificate rotation for the kubelet?
response: You can enable certificate rotation for the kubelet by passing the --rotate-certificates argument to the kubelet process.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: What is the purpose of the --cluster-signing-duration argument in the kube-controller-manager process?
response: The --cluster-signing-duration argument in the kube-controller-manager process controls how long certificates will be issued for.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: How can I view the status of certificate signing requests in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can view the status of certificate signing requests using the command: kubectl get csr
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: At what point does the kubelet automatically issue a new certificate signing request as the current certificate approaches expiration?
response: The kubelet will automatically issue a new certificate signing request at any point between 30% and 10% of the time remaining on the certificate.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: What is the primary purpose of node-pressure eviction in Kubernetes?
response: Node-pressure eviction is the process by which the kubelet proactively terminates pods to reclaim resource on nodes.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: How does the kubelet handle pod termination during node-pressure eviction?
response: During a node-pressure eviction, the kubelet sets the phase for the selected pods to Failed and terminates the Pod. The kubelet does not respect your configured PodDisruptionBudget or the pod's terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: What is the behavior of the kubelet when a static pod is evicted due to resource pressure?
response: If a static pod is evicted due to resource pressure, the kubelet tries to create a replacement because static Pods always represent an intent to run a Pod on that node. The kubelet takes the priority of the static pod into account when creating a replacement.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: How does the kubelet prioritize pod replacement during node-pressure eviction?
response: If the pods are managed by a workload management object (such as StatefulSet or Deployment) that replaces failed pods, the control plane (kube-controller-manager) creates new pods in place of the evicted pods. For static pods, the kubelet takes the priority of the static pod into account when creating a replacement.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: What are eviction signals used for in node-pressure eviction?
response: Eviction signals are the current state of a particular resource at a specific point in time. The kubelet uses eviction signals to make eviction decisions by comparing the signals to eviction thresholds, which are the minimum amount of the resource that should be available on the node.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: What is the primary goal of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler in Kubernetes?
response: The primary goal of a HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) is to automatically update a workload resource, such as a Deployment or StatefulSet, to scale the workload to match demand by deploying more Pods.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough

instruction: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling in Kubernetes?
response: Horizontal scaling means deploying more Pods to respond to increased load, whereas vertical scaling means assigning more resources (such as memory or CPU) to the Pods that are already running for the workload.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough

instruction: What is required to be deployed and configured in the Kubernetes cluster to use HorizontalPodAutoscaler?
response: A Metrics Server needs to be deployed and configured in the Kubernetes cluster to use HorizontalPodAutoscaler. The Metrics Server collects resource metrics from the kubelets and exposes them through the Kubernetes API.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough

instruction: What command can be used to enable metrics-server in a Minikube cluster?
response: To enable metrics-server in a Minikube cluster, you can run the command: minikube addons enable metrics-server.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough

instruction: What is the recommended Kubernetes server version to follow this HorizontalPodAutoscaler walkthrough?
response: The Kubernetes server must be at or later than version 1.23 to follow this walkthrough. You can check the version by running kubectl version.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough

instruction: What happens when a client tries to update the Data field of a ControllerRevision after it has been successfully created?
response: The API Server will fail validation of the request.
title: ControllerRevision

instruction: Can ControllerRevisions be deleted?
response: Yes, ControllerRevisions can be deleted.
title: ControllerRevision

instruction: What happens when a container uses more CPU than its specified limit?
response: The kernel will restrict access to the CPU corresponding to the container's limit through CPU throttling.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the difference between CPU and memory limit enforcement?
response: CPU limits are enforced by CPU throttling and are a hard limit, while memory limits are enforced by the kernel with out of memory (OOM) kills and are reactive.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What happens when a container uses more memory than its specified limit?
response: The kernel may terminate it with an out of memory (OOM) kill when memory pressure is detected.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: How does the kube-scheduler use resource requests?
response: The kube-scheduler uses resource requests to decide which node to place a Pod on.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What happens if a limit is specified for a resource but no request is specified?
response: Kubernetes copies the limit and uses it as the request if no admission-time mechanism has applied a default request for that resource.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl certificate approve command?
response: The kubectl certificate approve command is used to approve a certificate signing request (CSR). This action tells a certificate signing controller to issue a certificate to the requester with the attributes requested in the CSR.
title: kubectl certificate approve

instruction: What are the potential risks of approving a CSR?
response: Depending on the requested attributes, the issued certificate can potentially grant a requester access to cluster resources or to authenticate as a requested identity. Before approving a CSR, ensure you understand what the signed certificate can do.
title: kubectl certificate approve

instruction: How do you approve a CSR named 'csr-sqgzp' using kubectl certificate approve?
response: You can approve CSR 'csr-sqgzp' by running the command: kubectl certificate approve csr-sqgzp
title: kubectl certificate approve

instruction: What is the use of the --force option with kubectl certificate approve?
response: The --force option allows you to update the CSR even if it is already approved.
title: kubectl certificate approve

instruction: Can you specify multiple files or directories to identify the resource to update with kubectl certificate approve?

instruction: What is the purpose of the UserInfo object in Kubernetes?
response: The UserInfo object holds the information about the user needed to implement the user.Info interface.

instruction: What is the significance of the uid field in the UserInfo object?
response: The uid field is a unique value that identifies this user across time. If this user is deleted and another user by the same name is added, they will have different UIDs.

instruction: What type of data is stored in the extra field of the UserInfo object?
response: The extra field stores any additional information provided by the authenticator.
