response: You need to install kubectl, which lets you communicate with a Kubernetes cluster and run commands against it. See Install and Set Up kubectl for installation instructions.
title: Learning environment

instruction: What are some options for running Kubernetes locally on my machine?
response: You can use kind (Kubernetes IN Docker), minikube, Docker Desktop, Podman Desktop, Rancher Desktop, MicroK8s, or Red Hat CodeReady Containers (CRC) to run a local Kubernetes cluster.
title: Learning environment

instruction: What is the purpose of kind in Kubernetes?
response: kind (Kubernetes IN Docker) runs Kubernetes clusters using Docker containers as nodes. It is lightweight and designed specifically for testing Kubernetes itself, but works great for learning too.
title: Learning environment

instruction: How can I practice setting up a more production-like Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can use kubeadm to practice setting up a more production-like cluster. Setting up a cluster with kubeadm is an advanced task that requires multiple machines (physical or virtual).
title: Learning environment

instruction: What are some online playgrounds where I can try Kubernetes without installing anything on my computer?
response: You can use Killercoda, which provides interactive Kubernetes scenarios and a playground environment, to try Kubernetes without installing anything on your computer.
title: Learning environment

instruction: What is the purpose of a DeviceClass in Kubernetes?
response: A DeviceClass is a vendor- or admin-provided resource that contains device configuration and selectors. It can be referenced in the device requests of a claim to apply these presets.
title: DeviceClass

instruction: What is the scope of a DeviceClass in Kubernetes?
response: DeviceClass is cluster scoped.
title: DeviceClass

instruction: What happens to claim allocations when the DeviceClass spec is updated?
response: Changing the spec automatically increments the metadata.generation number. Claim allocations are done once based on whatever was set in classes at the time of allocation.
title: DeviceClass

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'config' field in a DeviceClassSpec?
response: Config defines configuration parameters that apply to each device that is claimed via this class. These configurations are passed to the driver, but are not considered while allocating the claim.
title: DeviceClass

instruction: What is the requirement for the 'extendedResourceName' field in a DeviceClassSpec?
response: ExtendedResourceName should be unique among all the device classes in a cluster. It has the same format as the name of a pod's extended resource. If two device classes have the same name, then the class created later is picked to satisfy a pod's extended resource requests.
title: DeviceClass

instruction: What are the possible values for the effect field in a Toleration?
response: The possible values for the effect field in a Toleration are NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, and NoExecute.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is the purpose of the operator field in a Toleration?
response: The operator field in a Toleration represents a key's relationship to the value. Valid operators are Exists, Equal, Lt, and Gt. Defaults to Equal.
title: Toleration

instruction: What happens when a pod with a Toleration of effect NoExecute and tolerationSeconds set to 0 is scheduled on a node with a taint?
response: When a pod with a Toleration of effect NoExecute and tolerationSeconds set to 0 is scheduled on a node with a taint, the pod will be evicted immediately.
title: Toleration

instruction: Can a Toleration with an empty key and operator set to Exists match all taints?
response: Yes, a Toleration with an empty key and operator set to Exists can match all taints of a particular category, effectively acting as a wildcard for value.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is the default behavior of a Toleration if tolerationSeconds is not set?
response: If tolerationSeconds is not set, the Toleration will tolerate the taint forever, meaning the pod will not be evicted due to the taint.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is telepresence and what problem does it solve for Kubernetes developers?
response: Telepresence is a tool that eases the process of developing and debugging services locally while proxying the service to a remote Kubernetes cluster. It allows developers to use custom tools, such as a debugger and IDE, for a local service and provides the service full access to ConfigMap, secrets, and the services running on the remote cluster.
title: Developing and debugging services locally using telepresence

instruction: How do I connect my local machine to a remote Kubernetes cluster using telepresence?
response: To connect your local machine to a remote Kubernetes cluster using telepresence, run the command 'telepresence connect' to launch its Daemon and connect your local workstation to the cluster.
title: Developing and debugging services locally using telepresence

instruction: How can I develop or debug an existing service running on a remote Kubernetes cluster using telepresence?
response: To develop or debug an existing service, use the 'telepresence intercept $SERVICE_NAME --port $LOCAL_PORT:$REMOTE_PORT' command to create an intercept for rerouting remote service traffic to your local service.
title: Developing and debugging services locally using telepresence

instruction: What are the prerequisites for using telepresence with a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The prerequisites for using telepresence with a Kubernetes cluster are: a Kubernetes cluster installed, kubectl configured to communicate with the cluster, and telepresence installed.
title: Developing and debugging services locally using telepresence

instruction: How can I access services running on the remote cluster from my local machine after connecting with telepresence?
response: After connecting with telepresence, you can access services using the Kubernetes syntax, for example: 'curl -ik https://kubernetes.default'.
title: Developing and debugging services locally using telepresence

instruction: How do you create a priority class named 'high-priority' with a value of 1000 and a description?
response: kubectl create priorityclass high-priority --value=1000 --description="high priority"
title: kubectl create priorityclass

instruction: What is the purpose of the --global-default option when creating a priority class?
response: The --global-default option specifies whether the created PriorityClass should be considered as the default priority.
title: kubectl create priorityclass

instruction: How do you create a priority class that never preempts pods with lower priority?
response: kubectl create priorityclass high-priority --value=1000 --description="high priority" --preemption-policy="Never"
title: kubectl create priorityclass

instruction: What is the default preemption policy for a priority class?
response: The default preemption policy is "PreemptLowerPriority".
title: kubectl create priorityclass

instruction: Can you create a priority class and save its configuration for future kubectl apply operations?
response: Yes, you can use the --save-config option to save the configuration of the current object in its annotation.
title: kubectl create priorityclass

instruction: How do I print the supported API resources on the server?
response: You can use the command kubectl api-resources to print the supported API resources on the server.
title: kubectl api-resources

instruction: How can I print the supported API resources with more information?
response: You can use the command kubectl api-resources -o wide to print the supported API resources with more information.
title: kubectl api-resources

instruction: How do I limit the output to resources in a specific API group?
response: You can use the --api-group flag followed by the API group name. For example, kubectl api-resources --api-group=rbac.authorization.k8s.io.
title: kubectl api-resources

instruction: Can I sort the list of resources by a specific field?
response: Yes, you can use the --sort-by flag followed by the field name, which can be either 'name' or 'kind'. For example, kubectl api-resources --sort-by=name.
title: kubectl api-resources

instruction: How do I print only the namespaced resources?
response: You can use the --namespaced=true flag. For example, kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true.
title: kubectl api-resources

instruction: How do I create a new resource quota named my-quota with specific hard limits?
response: You can create a new resource quota named my-quota with specific hard limits using the command: kubectl create quota my-quota --hard=cpu=1,memory=1G,pods=2,services=3,replicationcontrollers=2,resourcequotas=1,secrets=5,persistentvolumeclaims=10
title: kubectl create quota

instruction: What is the purpose of the --scopes option when creating a quota?
response: The --scopes option specifies a comma-delimited set of quota scopes that must all match each object tracked by the quota.
title: kubectl create quota

instruction: How can I test the creation of a quota without actually creating it?
response: You can use the --dry-run option with the client or server strategy to test the creation of a quota without actually creating it. For example: kubectl create quota my-quota --hard=cpu=1,memory=1G --dry-run=client
title: kubectl create quota

instruction: What is the default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option?
response: The default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option is true, which means that any errors in templates due to missing fields or map keys will be ignored.
title: kubectl create quota

instruction: How do I create a new resource quota named best-effort with a specific scope?
response: You can create a new resource quota named best-effort with a specific scope using the command: kubectl create quota best-effort --hard=pods=100 --scopes=BestEffort
title: kubectl create quota

instruction: How do you create a deployment named 'my-dep' that runs the 'busybox' image?
response: You can create a deployment named 'my-dep' that runs the 'busybox' image by running the command: kubectl create deployment my-dep --image=busybox
title: kubectl create deployment

instruction: What is the default number of replicas created when using kubectl create deployment?
response: The default number of replicas created when using kubectl create deployment is 1. This can be verified by the option '-r, --replicas int32     Default: 1' in the command.
title: kubectl create deployment

instruction: Can a deployment have multiple images set for a multi-container pod?
response: Yes, a deployment can have multiple images set for a multi-container pod. This can be achieved by specifying multiple images with the '--image' option, as shown in the example: kubectl create deployment my-dep --image=busybox:latest --image=ubuntu:latest --image=nginx
title: kubectl create deployment

instruction: How do you expose a port in a deployment created with kubectl?
response: You can expose a port in a deployment created with kubectl by using the '--port' option followed by the port number. For example, to expose port 5701, you would use: kubectl create deployment my-dep --image=busybox --port=5701
title: kubectl create deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of the '--dry-run' option in kubectl create deployment?
response: The '--dry-run' option in kubectl create deployment allows you to test the command without actually creating the deployment. It can be set to 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If set to 'client', it will print the object that would be sent without sending it. If set to 'server', it will submit a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create deployment

instruction: What is the recommended configuration for storing sensitive data like the MySQL root password in a Kubernetes Deployment?
response: The password is defined in the config yaml, and this is insecure. A secure solution is to use Kubernetes Secrets.
title: Run a Single-Instance Stateful Application

instruction: What is the purpose of the PersistentVolumeClaim in the MySQL Deployment?
response: The PersistentVolumeClaim looks for a 20G volume to satisfy the requirements of the MySQL Deployment, and can be satisfied by any existing volume that meets the requirements or by a dynamic provisioner.
title: Run a Single-Instance Stateful Application

instruction: How is MySQL exposed to other pods in the cluster?
response: MySQL is exposed to other pods in the cluster at a known DNS name through a Kubernetes Service with a ClusterIP of None.
title: Run a Single-Instance Stateful Application

instruction: What is the storage class name of the PersistentVolume defined in mysql-pv.yaml?
response: The storage class name of the PersistentVolume is 'manual'.
title: Run a Single-Instance Stateful Application

instruction: What is the image used for the MySQL container in the Deployment?
response: The image used for the MySQL container in the Deployment is 'mysql:9'.
title: Run a Single-Instance Stateful Application

instruction: What is the purpose of CertificateSigningRequest objects in Kubernetes?
response: CertificateSigningRequest objects provide a mechanism to obtain x509 certificates by submitting a certificate signing request, and having it asynchronously approved and issued.
title: Certificates

instruction: What type of certificates do kubelets use the CertificateSigningRequest API to obtain?
response: Kubelets use this API to obtain client certificates to authenticate to kube-apiserver (with the "kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet" signerName) and serving certificates for TLS endpoints kube-apiserver can connect to securely (with the "kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving" signerName).
title: Certificates

instruction: Who can read ClusterTrustBundle objects in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: ClusterTrustBundle objects are considered to be readable by any authenticated user in the cluster, because they can be mounted by pods using the clusterTrustBundle projection. All service accounts have read access to ClusterTrustBundles by default.
title: Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of PodCertificateRequest in Kubernetes?
response: PodCertificateRequest encodes a pod requesting a certificate from a given signer. Kubelets use this API to implement podCertificate projected volumes.
title: Certificates

instruction: Can users with only namespace-level access to a cluster read ClusterTrustBundles?
response: Yes, users who only have namespace-level access to a cluster can read ClusterTrustBundles by impersonating a serviceaccount that they have access to.
title: Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of a CronJob in Kubernetes?
response: A CronJob is meant for performing regular scheduled actions such as backups, report generation, and so on. It creates Jobs on a repeating schedule.
title: CronJob

instruction: What are the constraints on the name of a CronJob?
response: The name of a CronJob must be a valid DNS subdomain value, but for best compatibility, it should follow the more restrictive rules for a DNS label. The name must be no longer than 52 characters.
title: CronJob

instruction: How does a CronJob schedule work?
response: The .spec.schedule field of a CronJob is required and follows the Cron syntax, which consists of five fields: minute, hour, day of the month, month, and day of the week. For example, '0 3 * * 1' means the task is scheduled to run weekly on a Monday at 3 AM.
title: CronJob

instruction: What happens when a CronJob creates Jobs and Pods?
response: When the control plane creates new Jobs and (indirectly) Pods for a CronJob, the .metadata.name of the CronJob is part of the basis for naming those Pods. The CronJob controller will automatically append 11 characters to the name you provide.
title: CronJob

instruction: Can a single CronJob create multiple concurrent Jobs?
response: Yes, in certain circumstances, a single CronJob can create multiple concurrent Jobs. See the limitations section for more information.
title: CronJob

instruction: What are the two ways to expose Pod and container fields to a running container in Kubernetes?
response: Environment variables and Volume files. Together, these two ways of exposing Pod and container fields are called the downward API.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables

instruction: How can you expose Pod fields as environment variables to a container?
response: You can use the env field in the container specification, and define environment variables with a valueFrom section that references a Pod field using a fieldRef.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables

instruction: What is the purpose of the downward API in Kubernetes?
response: The downward API allows a Pod to expose information about itself to containers running in the Pod, using environment variables or volume files.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables

instruction: What environment variables are exposed to the container in the example Pod manifest?
response: The environment variables exposed to the container are MY_NODE_NAME, MY_POD_NAME, MY_POD_NAMESPACE, MY_POD_IP, and MY_POD_SERVICE_ACCOUNT.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables

instruction: From which Pod fields are the environment variables MY_NODE_NAME, MY_POD_NAME, and MY_POD_NAMESPACE derived?
response: The environment variable MY_NODE_NAME is derived from the Pod's spec.nodeName field. The environment variable MY_POD_NAME is derived from the Pod's metadata.name field. The environment variable MY_POD_NAMESPACE is derived from the Pod's metadata.namespace field.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables

instruction: How do I create a Deployment and an associated ReplicaSet with five Pods, each running the Hello World application?
response: Apply the configuration in the file https://k8s.io/examples/service/load-balancer-example.yaml using kubectl apply -f. This will create a Deployment and an associated ReplicaSet with five Pods.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster

instruction: What command can I use to expose a Deployment named 'hello-world' as a Service with type LoadBalancer?
response: Use kubectl expose deployment hello-world --type=LoadBalancer --name=my-service.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster

instruction: How can I verify that a Service object has been created and obtain its external IP address?
response: Use kubectl get services my-service. The external IP address will be listed under the EXTERNAL-IP column. If the external IP address is shown as <pending>, wait for a minute and enter the same command again.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of setting type=LoadBalancer for a Service object?
response: The type=LoadBalancer service is backed by external cloud providers, which allows it to create an external load balancer.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster

instruction: How do I access the running application using the Service object?
response: You can access the running application using the external IP address and port number of the Service object. The external IP address can be obtained using kubectl get services my-service, and the port number is 8080 in this example.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of namespaces in Kubernetes?
response: Namespaces in Kubernetes provide a mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single cluster. They allow for unique names of resources within a namespace, but not across namespaces.
title: Namespaces

instruction: When should I use multiple namespaces in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You should use multiple namespaces in environments with many users spread across multiple teams or projects. For clusters with a few to tens of users, you likely don't need to create or think about namespaces at all.
title: Namespaces

instruction: How do I distinguish between slightly different resources, such as different versions of the same software, within the same namespace?
response: You should use labels to distinguish resources within the same namespace, rather than creating separate namespaces.
title: Namespaces

instruction: What are the four initial namespaces created by Kubernetes?
response: The four initial namespaces created by Kubernetes are: default, kube-node-lease, kube-public, and kube-system.
title: Namespaces

instruction: Is it recommended to use the default namespace for a production cluster?
response: No, for a production cluster, it is recommended not to use the default namespace. Instead, create other namespaces and use those.
title: Namespaces

instruction: How do I set the username for a Pod's container processes on a Windows node?
response: To specify the username with which to execute the Pod's container processes, include the securityContext field (PodSecurityContext) in the Pod specification, and within it, the windowsOptions (WindowsSecurityContextOptions) field containing the runAsUserName field.
title: Configure RunAsUserName for Windows pods and containers

instruction: What is the purpose of the runAsUserName field in a Windows Pod or container?
response: The runAsUserName field allows you to run applications in a container as a different username than the default, similar to the Linux-specific runAsUser setting.
title: Configure RunAsUserName for Windows pods and containers

instruction: How do I verify that a Pod's Container is running with the correct username?
response: To verify that a Pod's Container is running with the correct username, you can get a shell to the running Container using kubectl exec, and then check the username using the echo $env:USERNAME command.
title: Configure RunAsUserName for Windows pods and containers

instruction: Can I override the runAsUserName setting at the Pod level for a specific Container?
response: Yes, the Windows security context options that you specify for a Container apply only to that individual Container, and they override the settings made at the Pod level.
title: Configure RunAsUserName for Windows pods and containers

instruction: What is the effect of setting runAsUserName at the Pod level versus at the Container level?
response: The Windows security context options that you specify for a Pod apply to all Containers and init Containers in the Pod, while the settings for a Container apply only to that individual Container and override the Pod-level settings.
title: Configure RunAsUserName for Windows pods and containers

instruction: What is the minimum amount of memory required for each node in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Each node in your cluster must have at least 300 MiB of memory.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods

instruction: How do you specify a memory request and a memory limit for a container in a Kubernetes pod?
response: To specify a memory request for a container, include the resources.requests.memory field in the container's resource manifest. To specify a memory limit, include resources.limits.memory.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods

instruction: What happens if a container tries to use more memory than its limit?
response: A Container is guaranteed to have as much memory as it requests, but is not allowed to use more memory than its limit.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods

instruction: How do you enable the metrics-server in a Minikube cluster?
response: Run the command 'minikube addons enable metrics-server' to enable the metrics-server.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods

instruction: How can you verify if the resource metrics API is available in your Kubernetes cluster?
response: Run the command 'kubectl get apiservices' and check if the output includes a reference to metrics.k8s.io.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods

instruction: How do you wait for a pod named 'busybox1' to be in the 'Ready' status condition?
response: You can use the command kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready pod/busybox1. The default value of status condition is true; you can wait for other targets after an equal delimiter.
title: kubectl wait

instruction: Can you wait for a pod to be created and then reach a specific status condition?
response: Yes, you can wait for a pod to be created and then reach a specific status condition using the command kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready --for=create pod/busybox1.
title: kubectl wait

instruction: How do you wait for a service named 'loadbalancer' to have ingress?
response: You can use the command kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress}' service/loadbalancer.
title: kubectl wait

instruction: What is the purpose of the --for flag in the kubectl wait command?
response: The --for flag specifies the condition to wait for. It can be used to wait for a resource to be created (--for=create), deleted (--for=delete), or to reach a specific condition (--for=condition=available) or jsonpath (--for=jsonpath='{}'[=value]).
title: kubectl wait

instruction: How do you wait for a pod to be deleted with a timeout of 60 seconds?
response: You can use the command kubectl wait --for=delete pod/busybox1 --timeout=60s. This command waits for the pod to be deleted and times out after 60 seconds if the pod is not deleted.
title: kubectl wait

instruction: What is the total amount of memory that can be requested by all Pods in the quota-mem-cpu-example namespace?
response: The total amount of memory that can be requested by all Pods in the quota-mem-cpu-example namespace must not exceed 1 GiB.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace

instruction: What are the requirements for each container in a Pod in the quota-mem-cpu-example namespace?
response: For every Pod in the namespace, each container must have a memory request, memory limit, CPU request, and CPU limit.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace

instruction: How do you create a ResourceQuota object to set quotas for a namespace?
response: You specify quotas in a ResourceQuota object. You can create a ResourceQuota object by defining a YAML file with the quota specifications and then applying it to the namespace using kubectl apply.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace

instruction: What is the meaning of '1 CPU' in Kubernetes?
response: The meaning of '1 CPU' in Kubernetes can be found by referring to the 'meaning of CPU' documentation.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace

instruction: What is the command to view detailed information about the ResourceQuota object mem-cpu-demo in the quota-mem-cpu-example namespace?
response: The command to view detailed information about the ResourceQuota object mem-cpu-demo in the quota-mem-cpu-example namespace is: kubectl get resourcequota mem-cpu-demo --namespace=quota-mem-cpu-example --output=yaml
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace

instruction: What happens to the kubelet on a Linux node if swap is enabled?
response: By default, the kubelet will not start on a Linux node that has swap enabled.
title: Swap memory management

instruction: What is the default swap behavior for Linux nodes in Kubernetes?
response: The default swap behavior for Linux nodes is NoSwap, which means workloads running as Pods on this node do not and cannot use swap.
title: Swap memory management

instruction: Can processes outside of Kubernetes-managed containers utilize swap if the NoSwap behavior is chosen and the kubelet is configured to tolerate swap space?
response: Yes, processes outside of Kubernetes-managed containers, such as systemd services (and even the kubelet itself!), can utilize swap if the NoSwap behavior is chosen and the kubelet is configured to tolerate swap space (failSwapOn: false).
title: Swap memory management

instruction: What is the purpose of the memorySwap configuration in the KubeletConfiguration?
response: The memorySwap configuration in the KubeletConfiguration exposes the swap configuration on a node to a cluster admin, allowing them to specify the node's behavior in the presence of swap memory by setting memorySwap.swapBehavior.
title: Swap memory management

instruction: What happens to the kubelet on a Windows node if swap is disabled?
response: By default, the kubelet does not start on a Windows node that has swap disabled.
title: Swap memory management

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes version required to use Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) for device allocation?
response: The minimum Kubernetes version required to use DRA is v1.34.
title: Allocate Devices to Workloads with DRA

instruction: How can I list the available DeviceClasses in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can list the available DeviceClasses in your cluster by running the command 'kubectl get deviceclasses'.
title: Allocate Devices to Workloads with DRA

instruction: What are the two ways to create a ResourceClaim for device allocation?
response: You can create a ResourceClaim either by manually creating a ResourceClaim or by using a ResourceClaimTemplate.
title: Allocate Devices to Workloads with DRA

instruction: When should I use a ResourceClaimTemplate instead of a ResourceClaim?
response: You should use a ResourceClaimTemplate if you want every Pod to have access to separate devices, and a ResourceClaim if you want multiple Pods to share access to the same devices or if you want a claim to exist beyond the lifetime of a Pod.
title: Allocate Devices to Workloads with DRA

instruction: What needs to be set up by the cluster administrator before I can use DRA for device allocation?
response: The cluster administrator needs to set up DRA, attach devices, and install drivers in the cluster.
title: Allocate Devices to Workloads with DRA

instruction: What are the objectives of running kubelet in standalone mode?
response: The objectives are to install cri-o and kubelet on a Linux system and run them as systemd services, launch a Pod running nginx that listens to requests on TCP port 80 on the Pod's IP address, and learn how the different components of the solution interact among themselves.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode

instruction: Why does kubelet fail to start by default if swap memory is detected on a node?
response: kubelet fails to start by default if swap memory is detected on a node. This means that swap should either be disabled or tolerated by kubelet by adding failSwapOn: false to the kubelet configuration file.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode

instruction: How can you check if swap is enabled on a Linux system?
response: You can check if swap is enabled by running the command 'sudo swapon --show'. If there is no output from the command, then swap memory is already disabled.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode

instruction: What should you do to make the change of disabling swap persistent across reboots?
response: To make the change persistent across reboots, you should make sure swap is disabled in either /etc/fstab or systemd.swap, depending on how it was configured on your system.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode

instruction: Is the kubelet configuration used for this tutorial suitable for a production environment?
response: No, the kubelet configuration used for this tutorial is insecure by design and should not be used in a production environment.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode

instruction: What is the purpose of the Workload API in Kubernetes?
response: The Workload API allows for expressing scheduling constraints that should be used when managing the lifecycle of workloads from the scheduling perspective, including scheduling, preemption, eviction and other phases.
title: Workload

instruction: How is the Workload API enablement toggled?
response: The Workload API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.
title: Workload

instruction: What is the maximum number of PodGroupTemplates that can be included in a Workload?
response: The maximum number of PodGroupTemplates that can be included in a Workload is 8.
title: Workload

instruction: Is the controllerRef field in WorkloadSpec mutable or immutable?
response: The controllerRef field in WorkloadSpec is immutable.
title: Workload

instruction: What is the purpose of the controllerRef field in WorkloadSpec?
response: The controllerRef field is an optional reference to the controlling object, such as a Deployment or Job, and is intended for use by tools like CLIs to provide a link back to the original workload definition.
title: Workload

instruction: What is the name of the ConfigMap created in the example?
response: The name of the ConfigMap created in the example is 'example-redis-config'.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: How is the ConfigMap exposed as a file inside the Redis Pod?
response: The ConfigMap is exposed as a file named 'redis.conf' on the 'config' volume, which is mounted at '/redis-master' inside the Pod. This is achieved by setting 'spec.volumes[1].configMap.items[0].key' to 'redis-config' and 'spec.volumes[1].configMap.items[0].path' to 'redis.conf', and then mounting the 'config' volume at '/redis-master' using 'spec.containers[0].volumeMounts[1]'.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the command used to apply the ConfigMap and Redis Pod manifests?
response: The commands used to apply the ConfigMap and Redis Pod manifests are 'kubectl apply -f example-redis-config.yaml' and 'kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/website/main/content/en/examples/pods/config/redis-pod.yaml'.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the path to the Redis configuration file inside the Pod?
response: The path to the Redis configuration file inside the Pod is '/redis-master/redis.conf'.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the image used for the Redis container in the example?
response: The image used for the Redis container in the example is 'redis:8.0.2'.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the purpose of defining a LimitRange object in a Kubernetes namespace?
response: To define a range of valid memory resource limits for a namespace, so that every new Pod in that namespace falls within the range you configure.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum Memory Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: How do you create a LimitRange object that sets minimum and maximum memory constraints for containers in a namespace?
response: You create a LimitRange object by specifying the minimum and maximum memory values in a YAML file, and then applying it to the namespace using kubectl. For example, you can use the provided YAML file and apply it using kubectl apply -f memory-constraints.yaml --namespace=constraints-mem-example.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum Memory Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What happens if a Pod does not meet the constraints imposed by the LimitRange object?
response: If a Pod does not meet the constraints imposed by the LimitRange object, it cannot be created in the namespace.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum Memory Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What are the default memory request and limit values assigned to a container in a Pod if they are not specified?
response: If a container in a Pod does not specify its own memory request and limit, the control plane assigns the default memory request and limit to that container. In this case, the default memory request and limit are both 1Gi.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum Memory Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What is the minimum amount of memory required for a container in a Pod within the constraints-mem-example namespace?
response: The minimum amount of memory required for a container in a Pod within the constraints-mem-example namespace is 500Mi.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum Memory Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What is the purpose of Seccomp in Linux and how does it relate to Kubernetes?
response: Seccomp stands for secure computing mode and is a feature of the Linux kernel that restricts the calls a process can make from userspace into the kernel. Kubernetes uses Seccomp to automatically apply seccomp profiles loaded onto a node to Pods and containers.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: How can you specify a seccomp profile for a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: You can specify a seccomp profile for a Pod in Kubernetes using spec.securityContext.seccompProfile for the whole Pod, or for individual containers using spec.containers[*].securityContext.seccompProfile, for init containers using spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.seccompProfile, and for ephemeral containers using spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.seccompProfile.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: What happens if a container does not set the seccompProfile field explicitly?
response: If a container does not set the seccompProfile field explicitly, it inherits the profile from the Pod. The same applies to ephemeral and init containers.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: Can you apply a seccomp profile to a Pod or container running with privileged: true?
response: No, it is not possible to apply a seccomp profile to a Pod or container running with privileged: true set in the container's securityContext. Privileged containers always run as Unconfined.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: What are the possible values for the seccompProfile.type field?
response: The possible values for the seccompProfile.type field are Unconfined, which runs without any seccomp restrictions, and RuntimeDefault, which applies a default seccomp profile defined by the container runtime.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: What is the purpose of the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) in Kubernetes?
response: The CRI is a plugin interface which enables the kubelet to use a wide variety of container runtimes, without having a need to recompile the cluster components.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: What is the required CRI API version for container runtimes in Kubernetes v1.26 and later?
response: For Kubernetes v1.26 and later, the kubelet requires that the container runtime supports the v1 CRI API.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: How can the kubelet be configured to connect to a container runtime with a custom endpoint?
response: The kubelet can be configured to connect to a container runtime with a custom endpoint by using the --container-runtime-endpoint command line flag.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: What is the purpose of the CRIListStreaming feature gate in Kubernetes?
response: The CRIListStreaming feature gate enables the kubelet to use server-side streaming RPCs to retrieve results from the container runtime, bypassing the per-message size limit for large numbers of containers.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: What happens if a container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API during a Kubernetes upgrade?
response: If a container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API, the kubelet will fail to register and report an error. This might require a restart of the kubelet after the container runtime is correctly configured.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: What is the purpose of the causes field in StatusDetails?
response: The causes field in StatusDetails is an array of StatusCause that includes more details associated with the StatusReason failure. Not all StatusReasons may provide detailed causes.
title: StatusDetails

instruction: How should clients handle fields in StatusDetails that do not match the defined type of each attribute?
response: Clients must ignore fields that do not match the defined type of each attribute, and should assume that any attribute may be empty, invalid, or under defined.
title: StatusDetails

instruction: What does the retryAfterSeconds field in StatusDetails indicate?
response: The retryAfterSeconds field in StatusDetails indicates the time in seconds before the operation should be retried. Some errors may indicate the client must take an alternate action - for those errors this field may indicate how long to wait before taking the alternate action.
title: StatusDetails

instruction: What is the difference between kind and name fields in StatusDetails?
response: The kind field in StatusDetails is the kind attribute of the resource associated with the status StatusReason, which on some operations may differ from the requested resource Kind. The name field is the name attribute of the resource associated with the status StatusReason (when there is a single name which can be described).
title: StatusDetails

instruction: What is the significance of the uid field in StatusDetails?
response: The uid field in StatusDetails is the UID of the resource (when there is a single resource which can be described). It provides a unique identifier for the resource.
title: StatusDetails
